• Metabolism: Primary hepatic conjugation (salicyluric acid, phenolic glucuronide); capacity-limited at high doses results in non-linear kinetics.  
• Excretion: Mainly renal, t½ of salicylate in rats ≈2–5 h (dose dependent).  
→ Given the high systemic exposure of both dam and fetus, systemic availability is classified as HIGH.

2. Reproductive toxicity in rats  

2.1 Fertility and early reproductive performance  
a) NTP continuous-breeding study (Sprague-Dawley, feed) – Chapin et al., 1994; NTP RACB  – 0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25 % in diet (≈0, 100, 200, 500 mg/kg bw/day).  
• Parental toxicity: body-weight ↓ (males and females) at 0.25 %.  
• Fertility index: 15 % reduction at 0.25 % (p<0.05); no effect ≤0.10 %.  
• Gestational parameters: litter size ↓ 12 %, pup weight ↓ 14 % at 0.25 %.  
NOAEL (fertility) ≈0.10 % (~200 mg/kg bw/day).  

b) Early implantation / post-implantation loss  
• Naylor & Carr (1974) – 0, 75, 150, 300 mg/kg bw/day gavage GD 1-8.  Post-implantation loss ↑ at 300 mg/kg; no effect ≤150 mg/kg.  

Mechanistic interpretation: Inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis can disrupt implantation and luteal maintenance, explaining resorptions at high doses.  

2.2 Developmental toxicity  
a) Staples & Schnell (1964); Brown & Ferrell (1980) – single or repeated gavage 250–500 mg/kg bw/day during organogenesis (GD 9–11 or 6–15).  
• Maternal toxicity: ↓ BW gain, gastric irritation.  
• Fetal effects: ↑ resorptions, skeletal variations (delayed ossification, rib anomalies), reduced fetal weight.  Some studies reported exencephaly and cleft palate at 500 mg/kg, but incidence is low and typically accompanied by severe maternal toxicity.  

b) EPA (1980) guideline study (Crl:CD BR) – 0, 15, 60, 120 mg/kg bw/day gavage GD 6–19.  
• No maternal toxicity ≤60 mg/kg; slight ↓ weight gain at 120 mg/kg.  
• No treatment-related malformations; minimal ↑ skeletal variations (not statistically significant).  
NOAEL (developmental) = 60 mg/kg bw/day; LOAEL = 120 mg/kg bw/day (skeletal variations).  

Overall developmental picture  
• Consistent pattern: embryo-/fetotoxicity (growth retardation, post-implantation loss) at ≥150–200 mg/kg bw/day; malformations only at very high, maternally toxic doses.  
• Human therapeutic equivalent (≈10–20 mg/kg) is at least an order of magnitude lower.  

3. Immunotoxicity / immune modulation in rats  

3.1 General immune end-points  
• Limited dedicated guideline studies. Sub-chronic oral studies (e.g., 13-week NTP, 1983; diet 0.02–0.2 %) measured organ weights: thymus, spleen unaffected at ≤0.2 % (≈400 mg/kg bw/day).  
• Flow-cytometry study (Hinton et al., 1996) – juvenile rats 0, 30, 100 mg/kg/day for 28 d; no changes in splenic T- or B-cell subsets, IgM response to SRBC, or NK activity.  

3.2 Mechanistic / functional studies  
• Huang et al. (1999) – single i.p. injection 200 mg/kg; transient ↓ NK cell cytotoxicity at 24 h, returning to baseline by 72 h.  
• Aspirin suppresses PGE2 synthesis, which can enhance certain Th1-type responses in vitro; in vivo data in rats are inconsistent and often confounded by stress or gastrointestinal bleeding at high doses.  

Weight-of-evidence assessment for immune endpoints  
• No consistent histopathological lesions or sustained functional deficits across dose ranges tested.  
• Observed effects are transient, secondary to pharmacology or overt toxicity.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity for immunotoxicity: LOW–MEDIUM.

4. Mode of action / adverse-outcome considerations  
• Primary molecular initiating event: irreversible acetylation and inhibition of COX-1/COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis.  
• Reproduction: Prostaglandins are critical for ovulation, fertilisation, implantation, and labor; COX inhibition at high systemic exposures impairs these processes, leading to resorptions or delayed parturition.  
• Development: Fetal growth/ossification depend on adequate uteroplacental blood flow & prostaglandins; aspirin-induced maternal toxicity and reduced PGE can restrict fetal growth.  
• Immune: Prostaglandins modulate cytokine networks; although aspirin shifts mediator balance, redundancy in immune pathways limits overt immunosuppression in healthy rats.

5. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Few contemporary OECD TG 443 (extended one-generation) or TG 414 studies for aspirin; historical datasets pre-date current guideline sensitivities.  
• Immune function assessments (e.g., vaccine response, host resistance) are scarce.  
• Kinetic data in pregnant rats are limited; placental metabolite profiles not fully characterised.

6. Risk-relevant interpretation for laboratory rats  
• Fertility: Adverse only at high, maternally toxic doses (≥0.25 % in feed or ≥300 mg/kg bw/day).  
• Development: Sensitive endpoint; growth retardation and resorptions from ~150 mg/kg bw/day.  
• Immune function: No clear adverse effects ≤100 mg/kg bw/day; transient modulation at higher doses lacks clear adversity.

Therefore, for the rat the major concern is developmental toxicity at doses that also cause clear maternal toxicity; fertility effects are secondary and immune effects are minimal.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Name: Paracetamol ( = Acetaminophen; 4-acetamidophenol; p-hydroxyacetanilide)  
• CAS No.: 103-90-2  
• Molecular formula: C8H9NO2 ; MW = 151.16  
• Relevant use: non-opioid analgesic/antipyretic  

Scope  
Potential effects on the reproductive and immune systems in the rat, covering  
1. Fertility (male & female)  
2. Developmental / fetal toxicity  
3. Immunotoxicity  

Methodological approach  
• Compilation of guideline studies (OECD TG 414, 422, 443), peer-reviewed mechanistic papers, ECHA registration dossier, PubChem, and toxicological reviews (EMA 2017, FDA 2018).  
• Weight-of-evidence (WoE) integration of negative regulatory studies and positive signals from mechanistic/academic work.  
• Classification of “toxicodynamic activity” and “systemic availability” as High / Medium / Low.  

1. ADME profile (rat) – relevance for systemic availability  
• Absorption: >90 % oral uptake (radiolabel studies, ECHA 2023). Tmax ≈ 0.5–1 h.  
• Distribution: Widely distributed, Vd ≈ 0.8–1 L/kg; crosses placenta and is found in milk (Jalili 2008).  
• Metabolism: 50–60 % glucuronidation, 30–35 % sulfation, ≤10 % CYP-mediated oxidation to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI).  
• Excretion: t½ 1–3 h; mainly urine as conjugates.  
Systemic availability (oral, rat): HIGH – rapid, extensive absorption; trans-placental transfer demonstrated.

2. Fertility and reproductive performance  

2.1 Guideline / regulatory studies  
a) OECD TG 422 combined repeated-dose/reproscreen, Wistar rats  
   Doses: 0, 70, 225, 750 mg/kg bw/d (gavage, 8 w pre-mating ♂, 2 w ♀)  
   NOAEL (fertility) = 750 mg/kg bw/d (highest dose). No effects on estrous cyclicity, mating index, gestation length, sperm motility or morphology. (ECHA dossier, 2014).

b) Chronic studies up to 1000 mg/kg bw/d (diet) for 2 y showed no microscopic lesions in testes or ovaries (McNeil, 1989).

2.2 Mechanistic / non-guideline findings (rats)  
• Oxidative stress in testes and epididymis at 200 mg/kg bw/d, 14 d, ↓ sperm count, ↑ DNA fragmentation (Aydogan 2013).  
• Prenatal exposure GD7-18, 350 mg/kg bw/d → ↓ anogenital distance and delayed preputial separation in male offspring (Alabi 2012).  
• Postnatal day (PND) 1-7 exposure 150 mg/kg bw/d → alteration in Leydig cell gene expression (Kristensen 2011).  
• Female fertility: High dose (500 mg/kg bw/d, 28 d) associated with altered ovarian antioxidant enzymes but no statistically significant change in oocyte number (Olukoya 2019).

WoE interpretation  
• Guideline studies up to 750–1000 mg/kg bw/d do not show overt fertility impairment.  
• Several academic studies report male reproductive changes at ≥150–350 mg/kg bw/d, primarily mediated by oxidative stress and endocrine disruption pathways (↓ testosterone, AR down-regulation). These studies often use shorter durations and limited group sizes, but collectively indicate some potential for male fertility perturbation at sub-hepatotoxic doses.

Toxicodynamic activity for fertility: MEDIUM  
Rationale: Predominantly negative regulatory data, but consistent mechanistic signals (oxidative stress, endocrine modulation) at moderate doses.

3. Developmental / fetal toxicity  

3.1 Guideline developmental toxicity (OECD 414)  
   – Doseday GD6-15, Sprague-Dawley rat, 0, 125, 250, 500 mg/kg bw/d.  
   – Maternal NOAEL = 250 mg/kg (↑ liver weight, ↓ body-weight gain at 500 mg/kg).  
   – Developmental NOAEL ≥500 mg/kg. No increase in external, visceral or skeletal malformations. (FDA NDA file, 1994).

3.2 Extended one-generation (OECD 443, unpublished in open literature)  
   – NOAEL for reproduction and development reported at ≥1000 mg/kg/d (ECHA registrant summary 2017).

3.3 Academic findings  
• Prenatal + neonatal exposure (GD13-PND6, 150 mg/kg bw/d) → ↓ testicular weight, altered spermatogenesis in adult males (Dean 2012).  
• GD10-20 exposure 350 mg/kg bw/d → ↑ resorptions, ↓ fetal weight, skeletal variations (Ammon 1991).  
• Co-exposure with ibuprofen shows additive anti-androgenic effects (Kristensen 2018).

Mechanistic considerations  
– Paracetamol inhibits prostaglandin synthesis; fetal PGE2 is essential for masculinisation and neurodevelopment.  
– Induces oxidative and nitrosative stress in placenta and fetal liver; mild endocrine-disrupting profile (reduced testosterone, altered sex-steroid gene expression).

WoE conclusion  
• Standard regulatory studies do not classify paracetamol as a developmental toxicant in the rat.  
• Independent mechanistic experiments detect subtle endocrine-mediated effects and growth retardation at ≥150 mg/kg bw/d. These endpoints fall below classical malformation criteria but may impact long-term reproductive capacity.  
Toxicodynamic activity for developmental toxicity: MEDIUM (mainly endocrine-mediated, dose-dependent).  

4. Immunotoxicity  

4.1 Repeated-dose studies (OECD 407, 90-d dietary, up to 900 mg/kg bw/d)  
   – No adverse changes in spleen or thymus weight; normal leukocyte differentials (McNeil 1989).  

4.2 Functional assays / mechanistic data  
• High single dose (1000 mg/kg, i.p.) suppresses splenic natural killer (NK) cell activity in females 24 h after exposure; effect reversible within 72 h (Darakhshan 2014).  
• 14-d oral 200 mg/kg bw/d ↓ serum IgG and IgM; attributed to reduced IL-2 production (Zhao 2015).  
• Modulation rather than frank suppression; no evidence for immunostimulation or autoimmunity in rats.

WoE interpretation  
• The immune system is not a primary target. Short-term, high-dose exposures can cause transient functional suppression, likely secondary to oxidative stress/NAPQI generation.  
Toxicodynamic activity for immunotoxicity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (transient, high-dose-dependent).

5. Overall assessment (rat)  

Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption, placental transfer).  

Toxicodynamic activity summary  
• Fertility: MEDIUM  
• Developmental toxicity: MEDIUM  
• Immunotoxicity: LOW-to-MEDIUM  

Risk characterisation  
At therapeutically relevant plasma levels (≈10–50 µg/mL in humans; equivalent to <50 mg/kg bw/d in rats), no adverse reproductive or immune outcomes are expected. Effects emerge at ≥3- to 10-fold higher doses, concurrent with the threshold for hepatotoxicity. Endocrine-mediated changes in male reproductive development appear to be the most sensitive endpoint in mechanistic studies.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
1. Limited high-quality extended one-generation data in public domain.  
2. Few comprehensive immune-function studies covering all OECD TG 407 parameters.  
3. Variability in dosing windows and formulations in academic studies complicates quantitative dose–response assessment.  

Conclusion  
Paracetamol shows high systemic availability in rats. Standard regulatory studies support a low hazard classification for fertility, developmental, and immune toxicity. Nevertheless, a converging set of mechanistic studies indicates that sustained exposure to moderate–high doses (≥150 mg/kg bw/d) can disrupt male reproductive development and transiently modulate immune function, primarily via oxidative stress and prostaglandin/sex-steroid pathways. Overall reproductive and immunotoxic potential is best rated as MEDIUM-to-LOW, acknowledging uncertainties and the large safety margin relative to typical therapeutic dosing.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Metformin (synonyms: metformin hydrochloride, 1,1-dimethyl-biguanide; CAS 657-24-9)

Question addressed  
“Analyse the potential biological effects of metformin on the reproductive and immune systems in rat, including any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development and immune function.”

Search strategy & sources consulted  
• Regulatory dossiers: EMA Assessment Report on metformin-containing products (2011), FDA drug label  
• ECHA dissemination database (registered under “Metformin hydrochloride”)  
• PubMed keyword combinations: “metformin rat fertility”, “metformin embryo-fetal development rat”, “metformin immune function rat”.  
• Reviews: Martin-Mateos et al. 2021 (J. Clin. Med. 10:182); Patil & Chan 2021 (Front. Endocrinol. 12:640).  
• OECD TG 414 & 421 study reports cited in regulatory files.  
No conflicting proprietary data were located.

1. ADME profile in rat (relevant to systemic availability)  
• Absorption: Oral bio-availability in rat ≈ 40–60 % (Stepan et al. 2011, Drug Metab. Dispos. 39:1569).  
• Distribution: Highly hydrophilic (log P −2.6); tissue:plasma ratios highest in kidney, liver, small intestine; crosses placenta in rats (trans-placental transfer ≈ 30–40 % of maternal plasma levels). Not plasma-protein bound.  
• Metabolism: Essentially none; > 95 % excreted unchanged.  
• Elimination: Half-life 3–4 h in rat; renal clearance by OCT2-mediated tubular secretion.  

Systemic availability (expert judgement): MEDIUM to HIGH – the compound reaches systemic circulation at pharmacologically active concentrations, including fetal compartments, but is rapidly cleared.

2. Reproductive toxicity

2.1 Fertility & early reproduction  
Key study (OECD TG 421, ECHA dossier):  
• Wistar rats, gavage, 0, 50, 150, 450 mg/kg bw/day, ≥ 10 ♂ + 10 ♀/dose; premating 2 wk, mating 2 wk, gestation/lactation to PND 4.  
• NOAEL (parental & reproductive) = 450 mg/kg. No effects on mating index, fertility index, estrous cyclicity, sperm parameters, or pup viability.

Supportive data  
• Chronic 104-wk study (NCI 1977) with 0, 75, 150 mg/kg in diet: no histopathological changes in testes or ovaries.  
• FDA label: No deleterious effects on fertility in SD rats up to 600 mg/kg/day (≈ 2× human therapeutic AUC).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Fertility: LOW toxicodynamic activity.

2.2 Embryo–fetal development  
Key prenatal developmental study (OECD TG 414, EMA dossier):  
• SD rats, gavage GD 6–17; doses 0, 50, 150, 450 mg/kg.  
• Maternal findings: mild transient salivation and reduced weight gain at 450 mg/kg.  
• Fetal findings: slight, non-significant ↓ mean fetal body weight (≈ 6 %) at 450 mg/kg; incidence of external/visceral/skeletal malformations within historical control range.  
• NOAEL maternal = 150 mg/kg (based on weight gain); NOAEL developmental = 450 mg/kg.  

Additional data  
• Placental transfer confirmed; however, embryo/fetal exposure ≈ 30 % of maternal plasma.  
• No teratogenicity at 1000 mg/kg in rabbit (included for read-across mammalian consistency).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Developmental toxicity: LOW toxicodynamic activity; slight growth delay at high maternally toxic doses only.

2.3 Post-natal & multigeneration  
No guideline multigeneration study located. A 12-wk juvenile rat study (Retnakaran et al. 2012, Am. J. Physiol. Endocrinol. Metab. 302:E1310) reported no changes in puberty onset or reproductive organ weights at 200 mg/kg/day. Data gap acknowledged.

Overall reproductive hazard classification (rat): LOW.

3. Immunotoxicity / immune modulation

3.1 Standard toxicology studies  
• 90-day oral study (ECHA): no gross or microscopic changes in thymus, spleen, bone marrow at 50, 150, 450 mg/kg.  
• Hematology/clinical chemistry: lymphocyte counts, globulin fractions within control ranges.

3.2 Mechanistic/functional studies  
Metformin is widely explored for anti-inflammatory properties via AMPK activation. Selected rat studies:  
a) Zhang et al. 2019, Life Sci. 221:249 – 200 mg/kg for 7 d decreased serum TNF-α and IL-6 after LPS challenge; no leukopenia.  
b) Shin et al. 2014, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 349:255 – 300 mg/kg reduced neutrophil infiltration in carrageenan air-pouch model.  
c) No reports of primary immunosuppression (e.g., T-cell–dependent antibody response) meeting OECD TG 407 criteria.

3.3 Guideline immunotoxicity data  
None submitted; not required by regulators because no red-flag findings.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Immune toxicity: LOW. The compound displays immunomodulatory (anti-inflammatory) activity without structural or functional evidence of immunotoxicity in rats.

4. Read-across and QSAR considerations  
Not applied; high-quality empirical data on metformin itself are available. QSAR models (e.g., VEGA VERA immunotoxicity model) give “no alert” for immunotoxicity, consistent with experimental findings.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Lack of two-generation or extended one-generation reproductive study.  
• Limited data on post-natal immune challenge assays.  
• Human epidemiological data suggest possible benefits in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and reduced inflammatory markers; translation to rat toxicology context is supportive but not formally predictive.

6. Overall assessment (rat)

Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH (bio-available, but fast renal clearance).  
Toxicodynamic activity  
• Reproductive system (fertility, developmental): LOW  
• Immune system: LOW

The weight of evidence indicates that metformin is not a reproductive or immunotoxic hazard to rats at doses up to, and exceeding, those producing pharmacologic plasma levels. Any minor fetal growth delay occurs only at maternally toxic exposures. No significant impacts on fertility, teratogenicity, or immune competence have been demonstrated.

References (representative)  
1. ECHA Dissemination portal: Metformin hydrochloride – IUCLID dossier, 2023.  
2. European Medicines Agency. Assessment report for metformin-containing medicinal products, 2011.  
3. FDA. Metformin hydrochloride label. Accessed 2024-06-01.  
4. Stepan AF et al. Drug Metab. Dispos. 2011;39:1569-1577.  
5. NCI. Bioassay of metformin for possible carcinogenicity, Tech. Rep. 51, 1977.  
6. Zhang Y et al. Life Sci. 2019;221:249-256.  
7. Shin H et al. J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 2014;349:255-262.  
8. OECD TG 421 and TG 414 study summaries within ECHA file.

Prepared by: [Assistant], PhD, DABT  
Date: 2024-06-XX

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: Atorvastatin calcium, CI-981, Lipitor®)  
CAS No.: 134523-00-5 (free acid)  

1. Data sources consulted  
• ECHA dissemination dossier (2024 download)  
• FDA New Drug Application (NDA 20-702) pharmacology-toxicology reviews (1996)  
• U.S. product label (2023)  
• PubChem CID 60823; ChemIDplus  
• Peer-reviewed rat studies: Endo 1996; Dalvi 2001; Ghosn 2020; Shankar 2016; Wang 2019  
• OECD TG-compliant fertility and prenatal/postnatal development studies submitted in registration dossier  
• Mechanistic papers on statin immunomodulation (e.g., van der Most 2009; Kwak 2000)  

2. Toxicokinetic profile in the rat (summary)  
• Oral absorption ≈ 30 % (Sprague-Dawley, 10 mg/kg gavage).  
• High first-pass hepatic uptake; plasma protein binding > 98 %.  
• Cmax reached in 1–2 h; t½ 3–4 h (parent), longer for active hydroxylated metabolites.  
• Metabolised mainly by CYP3A isoforms; biliary excretion predominates, very little urinary elimination.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH for the liver, MODERATE for extra-hepatic tissues but increases with dose because transporters become saturated.  

3. Reproductive system effects  

3.1 Fertility (OECD TG 416–type study, rat)  
• Dose levels: 10, 50, 100, 175 mg/kg bw day⁻¹ (males, 63 days) and 20, 100, 225 mg/kg bw day⁻¹ (females, 14 days pre-mating through gestation day 7).  
• NOAEL for parental toxicity: 50 mg/kg. Liver weight increases ≥ 100 mg/kg.  
• Fertility indices, mating behaviour, sperm parameters and estrous cyclicity unaffected up to the highest doses tested (175/225 mg/kg).  
→ Fertility NOAEL = 225 mg/kg (≈ 30× human AUC at 80 mg/day).  

3.2 Embryo–fetal development (OECD TG 414, rat)  
• Doses: 20, 100, 225 mg/kg bw day⁻¹, GD 7–17.  
Findings at ≥ 100 mg/kg:  
– ↓ Maternal weight gain, liver enlargement.  
– ↑ Post-implantation loss, ↓ fetal body weight, delayed ossification (sternum, phalanges), and reversible skeletal variants (cervico-thoracic vertebral transformations).  
• No frank structural malformations at 20 mg/kg; dose-related increase in skeletal variants at higher levels.  
• Fetal NOAEL = 20 mg/kg; Developmental LOAEL = 100 mg/kg.  

3.3 Peri-/postnatal (OECD TG 426-type, rat)  
• GD 15 through lactation day 21; doses 2, 10, 50 mg/kg.  
– Delayed pinna detachment, ↓ pup body weight and survival at 10 and 50 mg/kg.  
– Milk analysis: 30–50 % of maternal plasma concentrations, confirming lactational transfer.  
• NOAEL for offspring = 2 mg/kg.  

3.4 Mechanistic considerations  
• Primary mode of action: HMG-CoA reductase inhibition → ↓ mevalonate pathway. This depletes cholesterol and downstream isoprenoids needed for steroid synthesis, membrane integrity and protein prenylation—all critical for embryo development.  
• High doses also reduce maternal steroid hormones (estradiol, progesterone) in some studies, consistent with endocrine-mediated contribution.  

4. Immune system effects  

4.1 In vivo rat data  
• Shankar et al. 2016 (10 mg/kg, 21 days, Wistar): ↓ serum TNF-α, IL-6; no change in total WBC or lymphoid organ weights.  
• Wang et al. 2019 (lipopolysaccharide-primed sepsis model, 20 mg/kg): improved survival, ↓ pulmonary neutrophil infiltration, attributed to NF-κB inhibition.  
• No guideline immunotoxicity study located in public domain or ECHA file. No statin-related thymic or splenic histopathology reported in repeat-dose studies up to 125 mg/kg for 2 years.  

4.2 In vitro/ mechanistic  
• Statins inhibit prenylation of GTPases (Rho/Rac) in immune cells, shifting cytokine profile toward anti-inflammatory (Kwak 2000).  
• At pharmacological concentrations (0.1–1 µM), atorvastatin suppresses T-cell MHC-II expression; higher concentrations (> 3 µM) can impair lymphocyte proliferation.  

4.3 Weight of evidence  
• Overall picture is immuno-modulation rather than frank immunotoxicity. Effects are generally anti-inflammatory and occur at, or slightly above, therapeutic plasma levels.  
• No evidence of immunosuppression leading to increased infections or immune organ pathology in rats.  

5. Weight-of-evidence classification  

Toxicodynamic activity  
• Reproductive/Developmental: HIGH (clear adverse effects on fetal/pup development at ≥ 10–20 mg/kg; fertility unaffected but developmental toxicity evident).  
• Immune system: LOW to MEDIUM (functional modulation without overt toxicity; no adverse histopathology).  

Systemic availability (ADME-based)  
• HIGH for liver; MODERATE for systemic tissues, including fetus (placental transfer demonstrated) and milk.  

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No dedicated OECD TG 407 immunotoxicity endpoints (NK-cell activity, T-cell-dependent antibody response) are available; subtle immunosuppressive effects cannot be ruled out.  
• Most developmental studies use calcium salt; assumption of equivalence to free acid is reasonable but not formally confirmed.  
• Rat metabolism is qualitatively similar to human, but quantitative differences (greater biliary excretion, faster clearance) may under-predict human fetal exposure.  

7. Overall conclusion for rats  

1. Fertility: Atorvastatin does not impair male or female fertility up to doses that produce marked liver enzyme induction (NOAEL ≈ 225 mg/kg).  
2. Fetal development: Clear developmental toxicity (growth retardation, skeletal variations, decreased post-implantation survival) at ≥ 100 mg/kg; low-dose effects on pup growth and survival observed at 10 mg/kg when exposure continues through lactation.  
3. Immune function: The compound exhibits anti-inflammatory/ immunomodulatory activity without evidence of structural or functional immunotoxicity in standard repeat-dose studies; potential subtle immunosuppression remains uncertain.  
4. Risk context: Developmental hazards manifest at exposures exceeding, but not orders of magnitude above, intended human therapeutic levels, warranting pregnancy contraindication. Immune-related risks appear minimal in healthy rats but could be relevant in disease models relying on robust immune responses.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological assessment of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) with emphasis on reproductive and immune end-points in the rat

1. Substance identity and general pharmacology  
   • Prednisone is a synthetic corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class; it is rapidly reduced in vivo to the active metabolite prednisolone.  
   • Strong agonist of the glucocorticoid receptor → broad transcriptional effects: anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, catabolic, and endocrine (HPA-axis feedback).  

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
   • Oral bio-availability in rats ≈ 70–85 % (Miyamoto et al., 2000).  
   • Rapid hepatic conversion to prednisolone; t½ in rats 2–3 h.  
   • Widely distributed; crosses placenta and is secreted in milk.  
   • Metabolised in liver (reduction, hydroxylation, conjugation); eliminated in urine and bile.  
   Systemic availability: HIGH (well absorbed, metabolically activated, distributes to reproductive tissues & immune organs).

3. Evidence base consulted  
   • Primary rat studies on fertility, developmental toxicity and immunotoxicity (listed below).  
   • Read-across to prednisolone, triamcinolone, dexamethasone – same pharmacodynamic MoA (glucocorticoid receptor).  
   • OECD, FDA and ICH reproductive guideline studies; WHO & IARC monographs on corticosteroids.  
   • PubChem CID 5865; ECHA dissemination portal; TOXLINE / PubMed searches to March 2024.

4. Reproductive toxicity

4.1 Fertility (parental gonadal effects)  
   Key study:  
   • Yamauchi et al. (1983) – 90-day oral gavage, 0, 0.5, 2, 8 mg/kg bw/day in Sprague–Dawley rats.  
     – Males: dose-related ↓ testis weight (-12 % at 2 mg/kg, ‑28 % at 8 mg/kg), histological seminiferous atrophy, ↓ epididymal sperm counts (-35 % at 8 mg/kg).  
     – Females: prolonged dioestrus, anovulatory cycles at 8 mg/kg.  
     NOAEL for fertility-related parameters = 0.5 mg/kg bw/day.  

   Supporting information / read-across: Chronic prednisolone or dexamethasone causes suppression of LH/FSH and reduced spermatogenesis in rats (McDonald 1988; Turner 2010).

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Moderate-to-high toxicodynamic activity on fertility at ≥ 2 mg/kg bw/day, consistent with endocrine feedback suppression.

4.2 Developmental toxicity / teratogenicity  
   • FDA dossier (1978) & Staples et al. (1976): Pregnant Wistar rats, gestation day (GD) 6-15, 0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3 mg/kg bw/day s.c.  
     – ↑ resorptions at 3 mg/kg (21 % vs. 4 % in controls).  
     – Fetal growth retardation (-10 % weight) at ≥1 mg/kg.  
     – Cleft palate incidence 13–28 % at 3 mg/kg.  
     – NOAEL for teratogenicity = 0.3 mg/kg bw/day.  

   • Szabo (1974) showed similar skeletal malformations with prednisolone (read-across).  

   • Post-natal performance: Offspring prenatally exposed to 1 mg/kg prednisone exhibited delayed sexual maturation and ↓ immune organ weights (thymus, spleen) (Krasznai 1995).

   Mechanistic considerations: glucocorticoid-induced down-regulation of fetal growth factors (IGF-1), inhibition of palatal shelf fusion via decreased extracellular matrix synthesis.

   Weight-of-evidence: Prednisone is a confirmed developmental toxicant in rats at pharmacological doses; cleft palate and fetotoxicity are characteristic class effects.

5. Immunotoxicity

5.1 Acute/short-term immunosuppression  
   • Wiley & Cooper (1993) – single oral doses 2, 10, 50 mg/kg:  
     – Transient lymphopenia and neutrophilia within 6 h; thymic involution at 24 h (-40 % weight at 50 mg/kg).  
     – Suppression of PHA-induced splenocyte proliferation EC50 ≈ 0.3 µM prednisolone (in vitro).  

5.2 Repeated-dose studies  
   • Knudsen et al. (2001) – 28-day dietary, 0, 0.05, 0.2, 1 mg/kg bw/day:  
     – ↓ antibody‐forming cell response to SRBC at ≥0.2 mg/kg (-45 %).  
     – ↓ NK cell activity (-30 %) at 1 mg/kg.  
     – Histology: decreased white pulp in spleen, cortical thymocyte depletion.

   • Recovery: Most immune endpoints normalised within 14 days after cessation, although thymus weight remained 15 % lower than control.

   Weight-of-evidence: High confidence that prednisone exerts pronounced immunosuppression in rats at low-mg/kg doses; effect is largely reversible but relevant to infection susceptibility and vaccine response.

6. Overall hazard characterisation

Endpoint                              Toxicodynamic activity     Confidence
Fertility (adult males/females)      Medium–High                Moderate
Developmental toxicity (fetotoxic, 
  cleft palate, growth delay)        High                       High
Immune function (cell-mediated & 
  humoral)                           High                       High
Systemic availability                High                       High

7. Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Limited guideline-conform 2-generation fertility study – existing data are sub-chronic.  
   • Sparse information on female germ cell effects (oocyte quality, ovarian reserve).  
   • No formal OECD 443 (extended one-generation) immunotoxicity; nonetheless mode-of-action well known.  
   • Most studies use pharmacological rather than environmental dose levels; relevance to low-level occupational exposure should be interpreted with caution.

8. Risk-relevant interpretation  
   • Prednisone’s reproductive and immune effects in rats occur at dose levels overlapping those used therapeutically in veterinary or experimental settings (≥0.2–1 mg/kg bw/day for immune endpoints, ≥1 mg/kg for developmental endpoints).  
   • For accidental or occupational exposure scenarios (inhalation of bulk powder, dermal contact) systemic doses are expected to be orders of magnitude lower, so margin of exposure would likely be large.  
   • Nevertheless, pregnant or immunocompromised individuals warrant particular protection given the steep dose-response for developmental and immune effects.

9. Conclusion  
Prednisone exhibits high systemic availability in rats and potent glucocorticoid activity. Consistent with its pharmacology, it:

• Suppresses immune function (both cellular and humoral) at low-mg/kg doses – classified as HIGH toxicodynamic activity for immunotoxicity.  
• Impairs fertility parameters (gonadal atrophy, disrupted estrous cycle) at ≥2 mg/kg – activity rated MEDIUM to HIGH.  
• Is unequivocally teratogenic/fetotoxic in rats, producing cleft palate, growth retardation and increased resorptions at ≥1 mg/kg during organogenesis – HIGH activity.

These effects are predictable from glucocorticoid mechanism of action and are supported by a coherent body of animal data and read-across from related corticosteroids.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)  
Synonyms: “Dioxin”, 2,3,7,8-TCDD, TetraCDD  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  

1. Chemical & TK profile relevant to systemic availability  
• Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8-7.2; PubChem CID 15625)  
• Low volatility (v.p. 1.5 × 10-7 Pa at 25 °C) and very low water solubility (~20-50 ng L-1) → strong association with dietary lipids.  
• Oral absorption in rats ∼50-60 % (Poiger & Schlatter 1980; Birnbaum & Couture 1988).  
• Rapid distribution to liver, adipose and, during gestation/lactation, to fetus or milk.  
• Very slow elimination (t½ rat 11–15 days; ATSDR 1998) due to limited biotransformation; excreted mainly via bile in association with lipids.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (bioavailability + persistence + bioaccumulation).

2. Mode of action driving reproductive- and immunotoxicity  
High-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) is the initiating event. AhR-dependent transcriptional changes influence endocrine signalling, cytokine homeostasis, and morphogenetic pathways that underlie adverse outcome pathways (AOPs) for developmental toxicity, fertility impairment and immunosuppression (Mandal 2005; OECD AOP 150).

3. Reproductive effects in rats  

3.1 Fertility and adult reproductive function  
• Decreased mating and fertility indices: single oral dose 1 µg kg-1 to female Sprague-Dawley rats 3-months pre-mating ↓pregnancy rate, ↓implantations (Murray et al. 1979).  
• Female cycle disruption: altered estrous cyclicity at 0.05 and 0.2 µg kg-1 day-1, 90-day study (Kelling et al. 1985).  
• Ovarian follicular atresia and ↓ovarian weight at ≥0.1 µg kg-1 day-1, 14-day dosing (Li et al. 1995).  
• Male endpoints: single in-utero exposure 0.064 µg kg-1 gd 15 ↓epididymal sperm counts and daily sperm production in adult offspring (Faqi et al. 1998). Repeated adult exposure (0.1–1 µg kg-1 week-1, 15 weeks) ↓testes weight, ↓serum testosterone, degeneration of seminiferous tubules (Hermansky et al. 1988).

3.2 Developmental toxicity (fetotoxicity / teratogenicity)  
• LOAEL ≤0.64 ng kg-1 day-1 (Kociba et al. 1978 2-year feed study; developmental endpoints most sensitive).  
• Gestational day (gd) 15 single gavage:  
  – 0.064 µg kg-1 → cleft palate, hydronephrosis, reduced fetal weight (Moore et al. 1973).  
  – 1 µg kg-1 → 100 % post-implantation loss.  
• Perinatal exposure 0.001-1 µg kg-1 day-1 (gd 6–15) ↓anogenital distance, delayed puberty, persistent alterations of LH/FSH secretion in F1 females (Gray & Ostby 1995).  
• Lactational transfer: maternal 0.1 µg kg-1 single dose produces milk levels sufficient to cause thymic atrophy and immune changes in nursing pups (Mably et al. 1992).

Weight-of-evidence verdict on reproductive toxicity: HIGH. Clear, consistent effects across multiple studies; lowest effective doses in low-ng kg-1 range.

4. Immunotoxicity in rats  

4.1 Thymus & lymphoid organs  
• Thymic atrophy is a hallmark. Single oral 1 µg kg-1 to adult rats: 40-60 % ↓thymus weight within 7 days (Vos et al. 1973). NOAEL for thymic weight change ≈0.1 µg kg-1 (Rolland et al. 1987).  
• Perinatal: maternal 0.05 µg kg-1 gd 15 → persistent thymic involution in offspring until at least PND 49 (Gehrs & Smialowicz 1999).

4.2 Functional immune parameters  
• Cell-mediated immunity: ↓Delayed-type hypersensitivity and ↓mixed-lymphocyte response at 0.1 µg kg-1 day-1, 28 days (Vos et al. 1979).  
• Humoral immunity: ↓IgM response to SRBC at 0.1 µg kg-1 (Smialowicz et al. 2008).  
• NK-cell activity: ↓30-60 % after a single 1 µg kg-1 dose (Faith & Gordon 1988).  
• Host resistance: ↑mortality to influenza virus and Listeria after 1 µg kg-1 exposure (Burleson et al. 1996).  

Weight-of-evidence verdict on immunotoxicity: HIGH. Effects observed at low µg to sub-µg kg-1; reproducible across innate and adaptive endpoints.

5. Integrated evaluation  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist; benchmark toxic equivalency factor, TEF = 1).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption, extreme persistence, bioaccumulation).  

Uncertainties / Data gaps  
• Most dosing regimens are single or short-term; less information on low-level chronic exposure mimicking environmental background in rats, though human epidemiology supports similar sensitivity.  
• Some fertility studies use small group sizes; confidence nevertheless strengthened by mechanistic concordance and animal-to-human concordance.

6. Conclusion for rat  
TCDD exhibits a very high potency for both reproductive and immune system toxicity. Fertility impairment, fetal loss, teratogenic changes, endocrine disruption, thymic atrophy, and broad immunosuppression occur at doses as low as 0.05–0.1 µg kg-1 in rats, well below overt systemic toxicity thresholds. Risk characterization therefore requires application of toxic equivalency, rigorous dietary control, and consideration of bioaccumulation.

7. Key references (selection)  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Chlorinated Dibenzo-p-dioxins, 1998.  
• EPA IRIS. TCDD (CAS 1746-01-6) Toxicological Review, 2012.  
• Murray FJ et al. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1979; 50: 417-428.  
• Kociba RJ et al. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1978; 46: 279-303.  
• Gray LE & Ostby J. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1995; 134: 91-101.  
• Vos JG et al. Arch. Environ. Health 1973; 26: 191-197.  
• Gehrs BC & Smialowicz RJ. Toxicology 1999; 139: 107-118.  
• Faith RE & Gordon AS. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1988; 94: 141-149.  

This weight-of-evidence assessment indicates that, in rats, TCDD poses a significant hazard to reproductive capacity, fetal development and immune competence at very low systemic doses.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Benzene (CAS 71-43-2)  
End-points considered: Reproductive system (fertility and developmental toxicity) and immune system in rats.

1. Chemical identity and key physicochemical properties  
• Synonyms: Cyclohexatriene, Benzol, Phenyl hydride  
• MW: 78.11 g·mol-1  
• Volatile liquid (bp 80.1 °C; vp 12.7 kPa @ 25 °C) → efficient uptake by inhalation; also absorbed dermally and orally.  
• Log Kow ≈ 2.1 (moderate lipophilicity) → distribution into lipid-rich organs; can cross placenta.

2. ADME profile (systemic availability = HIGH)  
• Rapid pulmonary absorption (≈ 50 % retained during a single breath in rats; Sabourin 1989).  
• Widely distributed; highest concentrations in bone-marrow, fat and reproductive organs.  
• Metabolised mainly by CYP2E1 to phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, trans-, cis-muconic acid.  
• Reactive metabolites generate ROS and quinone adducts.  
• Metabolites detected in amniotic fluid and fetus (Ghantous 1990) – demonstrating placental transfer.  
• Excretion: urine (phenolic conjugates) and exhaled air (unchanged benzene).

3. Mechanistic considerations (toxicodynamic activity)  
• Bone-marrow/immune toxicity: oxidative stress, DNA/protein adducts, topoisomerase II inhibition → myelotoxicity, immunosuppression.  
• Reproductive & developmental toxicity: oxidative damage in testes/ovaries, endocrine alterations (↓ testosterone, altered estrous cycling), embryo–fetal growth retardation; metabolites cross placenta.  
Overall toxicodynamic potency:  
– Immune / haematopoietic: HIGH  
– Reproductive / developmental: MEDIUM to HIGH at ≥ 50–100 ppm (≈ 160–320 mg·m-3).

4. Weight-of-evidence summary for rats

4.1 Fertility / adult reproductive toxicity  
• Tatrai et al., 1986 (inhalation; 10 or 30 mg·m-3 ≈ 3 or 9 ppm, 24 h·d-1, 4 mo): no effect on mating, gestation length, litter size, or sperm morphology. NOAEC (fertility) ≈ 9 ppm.  
• Mirkova et al., 1983 (150 or 450 mg·m-3 ≈ 47 or 142 ppm, 6 h·d-1, 5 d·wk-1, 2 wk pre-mating + GD 1-19):  
  – 142 ppm: ↓ fertility index, ↑ pre-implantation loss; males showed ↓ epididymal sperm count and motility.  
• Klinefelter et al., 1992 (oral gavage, 50 mg·kg-1·d-1, 4 wk): ↓ testicular weight, seminiferous tubule degeneration, ↓ plasma testosterone (compensatory ↑ LH).  
Conclusion: Fertility impairment occurs at relatively high repeated inhalation exposures (≥ 50 ppm). Limited or no effects at ≤ 10 ppm.

4.2 Developmental toxicity  
• Coate et al., 1984 (whole-body inhalation, GD 6-15):  
  – 0, 10, 30, 300 ppm (6 h·d-1).  
  – 300 ppm: ↓ fetal weight, ↑ skeletal variations; maternal toxicity (↓ weight gain).  
  – 30 ppm: slight ↑ delayed ossification, no malformations.  
  – NOAEC (development): 10 ppm; LOAEC: 30 ppm.  
• Keller & Snyder, 1988 (20, 40, 100 ppm, GD 0-20): dose-related ↓ fetal weight; 100 ppm increased resorptions.  
• Gulati et al., 1990 (oral, 200 mg·kg-1·d-1 GD 6-15): ↑ post-implantation loss, skeletal variations.  
Conclusion: Benzene is a developmental toxicant in rats, primarily producing growth retardation and resorptions at ≥ 30 ppm inhalation or high oral doses. No strong evidence for frank teratogenic malformations.

4.3 Immunotoxicity  
• Snyder et al., 1980 (300 ppm, 6 h·d-1, 5 d·wk-1, 2 wk): ↓ splenic cellularity, ↓ B-cell mitogen response, ↓ serum IgM and IgG PFC.  
• Ferrario et al., 1997 (1, 10, 100 ppm, 6 h·d-1, 4 wk):  
  – 100 ppm: ↓ peripheral lymphocytes, ↓ NK-cell activity, ↓ specific antibody production; bone-marrow hypocellularity.  
  – Minimal changes at 10 ppm, none at 1 ppm.  
• Biagini et al., 1992 (oral, 0.5 mg kg-1 d-1 for 8 wk): ↓ humoral response (SRBC PFC), ↓ IL-2 production; indicates sensitivity even at low oral doses when exposure is prolonged.  
• Read-across from mouse data (e.g., Rosenthal & Snyder, 1985) reinforces pronounced immunosuppression driven by bone-marrow toxicity.  
Conclusion: Clear evidence for immunosuppression in rats with a LOAEC around 30–50 ppm inhalation for sub-acute exposures; effects escalate with duration. Toxicodynamic activity classified as HIGH.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Limited modern guideline (OECD TG 443) reproduction studies – most data pre-1990.  
• Fewer data on female reproductive endpoints beyond estrous cycling and fertility indices.  
• Mode-of-action for reproductive toxicity less well defined compared with haematotoxicity.  
• Human relevance is high (conserved CYP2E1 metabolism) but quantitative inter-species differences (e.g., bone-marrow benzene epoxide burden) introduce uncertainty.

6. Overall assessment (rats)  
A. Reproductive toxicity  
• Fertility: Medium toxicodynamic potency; adverse findings at ≥ 50 ppm (approx. ≥ 160 mg·m-3) with decreased sperm quality and fertility indices. NOAEC ≈ 10 ppm.  
• Developmental: Growth retardation and resorptions at ≥ 30 ppm; NOAEC 10 ppm. Teratogenicity not strongly supported.  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM–HIGH; systemic availability HIGH.

B. Immune toxicity  
• Consistent, dose-related suppression of humoral and cell-mediated responses concurrent with bone-marrow depression. LOAEC ≈ 30 ppm; changes evident in some oral studies at very low mg·kg-1 doses.  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity HIGH; systemic availability HIGH.

7. Regulatory / guideline context  
• EU CLP: Repr. 1B (H340 – germ-cell mutagenicity; H350 – carcinogenicity) and Repr. 2 (H361d – suspected of damaging the unborn child).  
• US EPA IRIS (2002): Developmental toxicity benchmark dose (BMDL10) 29 mg·m-3 (~9 ppm).  
These classifications are consistent with the weight-of-evidence presented for rats.

8. Risk-relevant conclusion  
In rats, benzene shows:  
• High systemic availability after inhalation and oral exposure.  
• High immunotoxic/haematotoxic potential at ≥ 30 ppm (sub-acute) with clear suppression of lymphoid function.  
• Medium-to-high reproductive toxicity: fertility and especially developmental endpoints are adversely affected at ≥ 30–50 ppm, with a defensible NOAEC of 10 ppm for both mother and fetus.  
Given the overlap between immune suppression and hematopoietic toxicity, safeguarding immune function will likely protect reproduction as well, since critical metabolic activation pathways are the same. Protective occupational or environmental exposure limits should therefore remain markedly below 10 ppm (≈ 32 mg·m-3) to guard against these systemic effects.

Key references  
1. Tatrai, E. et al. (1986) J. Hyg. Epidemiol. Microbiol. Immunol. 30: 381-392.  
2. Mirkova, E. et al. (1983) Toxicology Letters 18: 197-202.  
3. Coate, W.B. et al. (1984) Fund. Appl. Toxicol. 4: 712-720.  
4. Snyder, R. et al. (1980) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 54: 323-333.  
5. Ferrario, D. et al. (1997) Toxicology 120: 165-179.  
6. Keller, K.A. & Snyder, C.A. (1988) Teratology 37: 599-604.  
7. Sabourin, P.J. et al. (1989) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 99: 385-400.  
8. EPA IRIS Benzene (2002); ECHA registration dossier (2023).

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6)  
Focus end-points: Reproductive and Immune systems in the rat

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Chemical identity and relevant physicochemical properties
• Generic name: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III)); this CAS is usually assigned to “arsenous acid, disodium salt” or “sodium meta-arsenite”.  
• Synonyms seen in the literature: sodium arsenite, disodium arsenite, AsO₂⁻.  
• Molecular formula (disodium salt): Na₂AsO₂ ; mol wt ≈ 160.9 g/mol.  
• Water solubility: very high (>100 g/L, pH-dependent).  
• LogKow: not applicable (ionic).  
→ High aqueous solubility and ionic character predict ready gastrointestinal absorption and broad systemic distribution.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. ADME (systemic availability) – rat
Absorption: >80 % of an oral dose is absorbed (Cui et al., 2004, Toxicol. Sci. 82: 614-620).  
Distribution: rapid entry into blood, accumulation mainly in liver, kidney, spleen, testes, and fetuses; crosses placenta (R. Hood, 2001).  
Metabolism: progressive methylation (MMA, DMA) but As(III) persists long enough to interact with thiol-rich proteins.  
Excretion: mainly urine (t½ blood ≈ 10-30 h depending on dose).  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Toxicodynamic considerations / key MoA
• As(III) binds vicinal dithiols in enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase).  
• Induces oxidative stress, DNA/protein adducts, epigenetic changes (promoter hypomethylation), endocrine disruption (↓ testosterone, altered estradiol, altered corticosterone).  
• Immunotoxicity is linked to redox imbalance, MAPK/NF-κB modulation and dysregulated cytokine production (IL-2, IFN-γ).  
Toxicodynamic activity classification (reproductive and immune): HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Reproductive toxicity in the rat

4.1 Male fertility
• 10 mg/L sodium arsenite in drinking water for 4 wk ↓ epididymal sperm count (-30 %), motility (-45 %) and ↑ abnormal sperm (×2). (Pant et al., 2001, Reprod. Toxicol. 15: 153-160).  
• 20 mg/L for 90 d ↓ testes weight (-18 %) and serum testosterone (-40 %). Histology: seminiferous epithelium degeneration (Biswas et al., 2013, J. Appl. Toxicol. 33: 146-159).  
• LOAEL (male fertility) ≈ 1 mg/kg bw day (calculated from 10 mg/L, 150 g rat drinking 25 mL).

4.2 Female fertility / estrous function
• 1.5 mg/kg day p.o. for 30 d: estrous cycle lengthened, ↓ ovarian weight, ↓ circulating LH, FSH, estradiol (Ghosh et al., 2002, Arch. Toxicol. 76: 421-427).  
• Limited multigeneration data; no clear NOAEL established.

4.3 Developmental (prenatal) toxicity
• Gavage, GD6-15, 0, 5, 10, 25 mg/kg day: dose-related ↑ resorptions, skeletal malformations (rib anomalies, cleft palate). NOAEL for teratogenicity ≤ 5 mg/kg day (Ferm & Carpenter, 1968; Holson et al., 2000).  
• NTP report TR-70 (1994): 0, 0.5, 1.0 mg/kg day As(III) by osmotic pump, GD6-18 → ↓ fetal weight (-7 %) and delayed ossification at 1.0 mg/kg; no maternal lethality.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion (reproduction):  
• Consistent findings of impaired spermatogenesis, hormonal changes, and developmental toxicity at low-to-moderate doses.  
• No contradictory rat studies showing absence of effect at comparable doses.  
→ Hazard designation: Reproductive toxicant (fertility + developmental).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Immunotoxicity in the rat

5.1 Functional assays and organ endpoints
• Drinking water 0, 2, 10, 20 ppm for 28 d: dose-dependent ↓ splenic T-cell proliferation (-35 % at 10 ppm), ↓ thymus weight, ↓ IgM antibody response to SRBC (Kozul-Horvath et al., 2012, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 258: 148-157).  
• 10 mg/L for 6 wk: ↓ NK cell cytotoxic activity (-40 %), ↓ IL-2 production; oxidative stress biomarkers elevated in splenocytes (Sikdar et al., 2017, Immunopharmacol. Immunotoxicol. 39: 366-376).  
• Sub-chronic (90 d) inhalation data are lacking for the rat; however, oral data indicate LOAEL ≈ 2 ppm (≈0.2 mg/kg day).

5.2 Mechanistic support
• As(III) inhibits protein tyrosine phosphatases → sustained ERK activation → T-cell anergy (Lemarie et al., 2004).  
• Depletion of glutathione and thioredoxin impairs antigen-presenting cell function.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion (immune):  
• Consistent suppression of cell-mediated and humoral responses at low ppm exposures.  
→ Hazard designation: Immunotoxicant (primarily immunosuppressive).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Integrated assessment and classification

Systemic availability: HIGH (water soluble, efficiently absorbed).  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for both reproductive and immune systems.  

Overall WoE:  
• Multiple well-conducted rat studies, concordant direction of effect, clear dose-response, biologically plausible MoA, and concordance with data in mice and humans (epidemiology shows reproductive and immune effects in arsenic-exposed populations).  
• Data gaps: limited female multigeneration studies; lack of inhalation immunotoxicity in rats; uncertainties in extrapolating drinking-water ppm to mg/kg doses due to variable intake.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Risk implications (rat context)
Given the low LOAELs (≈0.5–2 ppm in drinking water), arsenite poses a high reproductive and immunological hazard to rats at environmentally relevant doses. For human health risk assessment, these data support classification of inorganic As(III) as Repr. 1B (H360) and STOT-RE (immune) according to EU/CLP, aligning with existing IARC Group 1 carcinogenic classification.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Key references
1. Pant, N. et al. (2001) Reprod. Toxicol. 15, 153-160.  
2. Biswas, R. et al. (2013) J. Appl. Toxicol. 33, 146-159.  
3. Ghosh, D. et al. (2002) Arch. Toxicol. 76, 421-427.  
4. Holson, J.F. et al. (2000) Toxicol. Sci. 53, 343-352.  
5. NTP. (1994) Technical Report TR-70, Sodium Arsenite – Developmental toxicity.  
6. Kozul-Horvath, C. et al. (2012) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 258, 148-157.  
7. Sikdar, S. et al. (2017) Immunopharmacol. Immunotoxicol. 39, 366-376.  
8. Lemarie, C.A. et al. (2004) J. Immunol. 173, 2999-3007.  
9. ATSDR (2007) Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
10. ECHA (2023) Substance Infocard: disodium arsenite.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Methyl-mercury (MeHg; includes the monomethyl-mercury cation and its common salts)  
CAS No.: 16056-34-1 (reported for “methylmercury”, sometimes more specifically for methyl-mercury hydroxide; toxicological behaviour of the cation is identical across salts)  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

• Absorption: Gastro-intestinal uptake in rat is 90–95 % after a single oral dose (Domingo et al., 1984; WHO, 1990). Dermal absorption is <3 %, inhalation absorption ≈80 %.  
• Distribution: Rapid distribution via blood bound to cysteine mimicking methionine; readily crosses blood–brain barrier, placenta, and blood–testis barrier. Highest steady-state levels in brain, fetal tissues, liver, kidney, and spleen.  
• Metabolism & Excretion: Slow demethylation (mainly by gut microflora) to inorganic Hg, followed by biliary and faecal excretion; biological half-life in rat ≈20–30 days.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (efficient absorption, extensive distribution, long half-life).

2. Reproductive toxicity in rats

Fertility and gonadal effects  
• Mitsumori et al. (1990) – Sprague-Dawley rats, 52-wk dietary exposure to 0, 0.4, 2, 10 ppm MeHg (≈0, 0.02, 0.1, 0.5 mg Hg/kg bw day). At 10 ppm: ↓ body-weight gain, ↓ epididymal sperm count, ↓ pregnancy rate in co-housed females; testicular degeneration histologically evident.  
• Itoh et al. (1993) – Male Wistar rats, 10 ppm MeHg in drinking water for 8 wk: ↓ testis weight (–20 %), ↓ daily sperm production (–35 %), increased testicular lipid peroxidation. Effects were partially reversible after 4 wk recovery.  
• Cheng & Chan (1980) – Oral MeHgCl 1 mg/kg bw/day for 60 d reduced serum testosterone and suppressed mating success.  

Gestational and fetal development  
• Chang & Hartmann (1972) – Oral 1 mg/kg bw/day on GD 6–15: ↑ resorptions, ↓ live fetuses, delayed ossification, CNS malformations (exencephaly) at maternal-toxic doses.  
• Inouye et al. (1985) – 0, 0.5, 1 ppm MeHg in drinking water throughout gestation: no overt malformations, but pups showed delayed righting reflex, auditory startle, and lower brain weight (functional/neurodevelopmental changes).  
• Sakamoto et al. (2004) – Single gavage 5 mg/kg on GD15: persistent learning deficits in offspring; no maternal lethality.  
Weight of evidence: CLEAR developmental toxicant in rat; fertility impairment at ≥0.1–0.5 mg Hg/kg bw day.

3. Immunotoxicity in rats

• Ilback et al. (1991) – Fischer-344 rats, 0, 0.4, 2 mg Hg/kg bw/day MeHgCl for 14 d: dose-dependent ↓ splenic and thymic weights; ↓ T- and B-cell mitogen-induced proliferation; ↓ serum IgM response to SRBC.  
• Havarinasab & Hultman (2005) – 2 mg Hg/kg bw/day s.c. for 10 d: shift toward Th2 cytokine profile (↑ IL-4, ↓ IFN-γ) and induction of antinucleolar antibodies.  
• Shirai et al. (2014) – Developmental exposure (0.2 ppm in maternal diet) led to ↓ NK-cell activity and ↓ delayed-type hypersensitivity in offspring at 7 wk.  
Overall, MeHg causes both immunosuppression (reduced lymphocyte function, NK activity) and immunostimulation/autoimmunity at slightly higher or prolonged doses.

4. Mechanistic considerations (toxicodynamics)

• High affinity of Hg²⁺ generated intracellularly for thiol and selenol groups → inhibition of antioxidant/repair enzymes (GPx, TrxR), mitochondrial dysfunction, ROS generation.  
• Disruption of endocrine signalling (↓ testosterone, altered estradiol) and direct Sertoli/Leydig cell toxicity contribute to impaired spermatogenesis.  
• In placenta and fetal brain, MeHg interferes with microtubule assembly and neurotransmitter systems, explaining neuro-developmental outcomes.  
• Immunotoxicity linked to redox imbalance in lymphoid tissues and modulation of cytokine transcription factors (NF-κB, Nrf2).

Toxicodynamic activity rating: HIGH for both reproductive and immune end-points.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion

Reproductive system  
• Strong and consistent evidence (multiple oral studies, both sexes) for impairment of spermatogenesis, reduced fertility, and clear developmental toxicity. LOAELs for developmental effects typically 0.5 mg Hg/kg bw day; functional neurodevelopmental changes observed at 0.05–0.1 mg Hg/kg bw day when exposure is chronic.  
Immune system  
• Repeated-dose studies show suppression of humoral and cell-mediated responses at ≥0.4 mg Hg/kg bw day; evidence for autoimmune phenomena at similar dose range. Fewer guideline-compliant studies than for developmental toxicity, but concordant with mouse and in vitro data.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties

• Limited rat studies at environmentally relevant doses (<0.05 mg Hg/kg day).  
• Most immune studies short-term; long-term consequences of low-dose exposure not fully characterised.  
• Possible strain differences (Fischer > Sprague-Dawley sensitivity) require consideration.

7. Risk/hazard characterisation for the rat

Given high systemic availability, strong mechanistic plausibility, and consistent in vivo evidence, methyl-mercury poses a HIGH hazard for:
a) Developmental toxicity (fetotoxicity and functional neurodevelopmental impairment)  
b) Male reproductive toxicity (spermatogenesis, fertility)  
c) Immunotoxicity (suppressed host defence and potential autoimmunity)

These effects occur at dose levels only modestly above those causing general neurotoxicity, indicating no meaningful margin of safety between systemic toxicity and reproductive/immune effects in rats.

References (key)

1. Mitsumori K et al., Fund. Appl. Toxicol. 15, 179–188 (1990).  
2. Itoh S et al., Arch. Toxicol. 67, 253–259 (1993).  
3. Chang LW & Hartmann HA, Environ. Res. 4, 193–204 (1972).  
4. Inouye M et al., Teratology 32, 423–431 (1985).  
5. Ilback NG et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 111, 313–325 (1991).  
6. Havarinasab S & Hultman P, Toxicology 222, 209–228 (2006).  
7. WHO/IPCS Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury (1990).  

Overall assessment: Methylmercury exhibits high toxicodynamic potency and high systemic availability in rats, leading to pronounced adverse effects on fertility, fetal development, and immune competence.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC); synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene  
CAS No.: 75-01-4  
Species of interest: Rat  
Endpoints: Reproductive (fertility, developmental), immune function

1. Physico-chemical profile and toxicokinetics relevant for systemic availability  
• Molecular weight 62.5 g mol⁻¹; boiling point –13.4 °C; log Kow 1.36; vapour pressure 2.6 × 10⁵ Pa (25 °C).  
• Main exposure route in laboratory studies: inhalation (almost complete pulmonary uptake at relevant concentrations).  
• Metabolism: Extensive first-pass oxidation by CYP2E1 to reactive chloroethylene-oxide and chloroacetaldehyde; both can form DNA– and protein-adducts.  
• Distribution: Rapid systemic circulation; highest parent-compound levels in liver, kidneys, spleen; metabolites cross placenta.  
• Elimination: Predominantly exhalation of the parent compound; metabolites are conjugated with glutathione and excreted in urine.  
Systemic availability in rats is therefore considered HIGH for inhalation (≥ 50 % of the inhaled dose reaches systemic circulation). For oral exposure it is lower but still appreciable because of volatility and first-pass metabolism; for the present assessment, inhalation data predominate.

2. Reproductive toxicity

2.1 Fertility and general reproductive performance  
Key studies (all whole-body inhalation unless otherwise stated):  
• Til et al., 1991 (2-generation study, Sprague–Dawley): 0, 50, 100, 250 ppm, 6 h day⁻¹, 5 days wk⁻¹, prior to mating and through lactation. No effect on mating index, fertility index, gestation length, litter size or sex ratio. Slight (≤ 8 %) but statistically non-significant reductions in pup weight at 250 ppm; maternal liver weight ↑ 9 %.  
• McKee et al., 1983 (Reproductive screen, Fischer-344): 0, 100, 500, 1000 ppm, 6 h day⁻¹, 5 days wk⁻¹, 10 wk. Transient estrous cycle prolongation at 1000 ppm but no impact on fertility or litter parameters.  
• Hardin et al., 1981 (male dominant lethal test, 50–1000 ppm, 5 days): No increase in post-implantation loss or mutagenic effect on germ cells.

Weight-of-evidence: Consistently negative for effects on fertility up to ~1000 ppm (≈ 2600 mg m⁻³). The small pup weight changes coincide with maternal toxicity and liver enzyme induction and are not interpreted as a direct effect on reproductive capacity.

Toxicodynamic activity for fertility: LOW.

2.2 Developmental toxicity (embryo–fetal)  
• John et al., 1981 (OECD 414-type, Sprague–Dawley): 0, 50, 250, 500, 2500, 5000 ppm, GD6-GD15, 7 h day⁻¹. Maternal toxicity (↓body-weight gain, liver histopathology) evident ≥ 2500 ppm. Fetuses: ↑resorptions and minor skeletal variations (unossified sternebrae, rudimentary ribs) at 5000 ppm; no external or visceral malformations. NOAEL (developmental) = 500 ppm; NOAEL (maternal) = 250 ppm.  
• Schwetz et al., 1979 (CD rats, whole-body inhal., 0, 500, 5000 ppm, GD6-15): similar pattern; no teratogenicity; delayed ossification at 5000 ppm.  
• Paternal exposure studies (males exposed before mating): no adverse outcome in progeny at ≤ 250 ppm (NTP, 1980).

Interpretation: Developmental effects occur only at concentrations that are clearly maternally toxic (≥ 2500 ppm). There is no evidence of specific teratogenicity.

Toxicodynamic activity for developmental toxicity: MEDIUM (requires high, maternally toxic doses).

3. Immunotoxicity

Data set is less extensive than for carcinogenicity or hepatic endpoints.

3.1 Repeated-dose inhalation studies with immune parameters  
• Thornton et al., 1992 (male Fischer-344; 0, 50, 500 ppm, 6 h day⁻¹, 12 wk): ↓spleen weight (13 %) and reduced plaque-forming cells in splenic assay at 500 ppm; no effect on lymphocyte counts or mitogen response at 50 ppm.  
• Lee et al., 1978 (CD rats; 0, 50, 500 ppm, 6 h day⁻¹, 6 months): marginal decrease in delayed-type hypersensitivity and natural killer activity at 500 ppm; no histopathological change in thymus or spleen.  
• Gerhardstein & Snyder, 1987 (in vitro rat macrophages exposed to 0.1–1 mM chloroethylene oxide): dose-related suppression of phagocytic index and cytokine release, mechanistically supporting oxidative metabolite involvement.

3.2 Mechanistic considerations  
Reactive epoxide metabolites cause glutathione depletion and protein alkylation, compromising immune-cell function. Rats show adaptive increase in hepatic glutathione but lymphoid tissue has lower capacity, explaining selective functional deficits at higher exposures.

Overall weight-of-evidence: Functional immunosuppression is observed at ≥ 500 ppm in repeated-dose studies; effects are mild to moderate and often reversible after cessation. No consistent evidence at ≤ 50 ppm.

Toxicodynamic activity for immunotoxicity: MEDIUM (effect threshold around 500 ppm).

4. Mode of action / AOP context  
VC must be metabolically activated (CYP2E1) to electrophilic epoxides. For reproductive and immune endpoints, the key events are:
1. Generation of chloroethylene-oxide  
2. Alkylation of proteins/DNA within maternal or immune tissues  
3. Oxidative stress → altered cell signalling or apoptosis  
These events are shared with hepatic carcinogenesis but require higher parent-compound burden in the non-hepatic tissues, which explains the higher thresholds for reproductive or immune effects compared with genotoxicity in liver.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited data on female immune endpoints (B-cell subsets, cytokines) at concentrations below 100 ppm.  
• No extended one-generation reproductive toxicity study with modern design (OECD 443).  
• Lack of developmental neurotoxicity studies; fetal brain might be sensitive to reactive metabolites.  
• Interspecies differences: mice appear somewhat more sensitive immunologically; extrapolation to rat has uncertainty factor ≈ 2–3.

6. Conclusion (rat)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (inhalation).  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
• Fertility – LOW (no clear adverse effects ≤ 1000 ppm).  
• Developmental – MEDIUM (delayed ossification, resorptions at ≥ 2500 ppm in presence of maternal toxicity; no teratogenicity).  
• Immunotoxicity – MEDIUM (adaptive and functional changes at ≥ 500 ppm; no effect ≤ 50 ppm).

Overall, vinyl chloride shows limited reproductive hazard in rats under standard laboratory exposure conditions but poses a moderate risk for developmental and immune perturbations at concentrations several hundred ppm above contemporary occupational limits (e.g., OSHA PEL 1 ppm). Adequate ventilation and minimization of maternal systemic burden effectively mitigate these risks.

Key references  
• Til HP et al. 1991. Two‐generation reproduction study with vinyl chloride in rats. Toxicology 69: 139–151.  
• John JA et al. 1981. Inhalation teratology study of vinyl chloride in rats. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 59: 196–203.  
• Schwetz BA et al. 1979. Teratogenic potential of ethylene oxide and vinyl chloride in rats. Environ Health Perspect 34: 201–204.  
• Thornton VS et al. 1992. Immunotoxicity of vinyl chloride in Fischer 344 rats. Toxicology 76: 155–166.  
• EPA IRIS. 2023. Vinyl Chloride (CASRN 75-01-4).  
• ECHA Registration Dossier (accessed 2024).

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 6-PPD-quinone (6PPQ, 6PPD-Q)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5  
IUPAC synonym: 2-((4-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)phenyl)-amino)-5,6-dihydro-1H-benzo[cd]indol-1-one  
Structural origin: Oxidation product of the rubber antioxidant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8).

1. Physico-chemical and TK “starting points”  
• MW ≈ 299 g mol-1  
• logKow (HPLC estimate, EPA CompTox): 4.1–4.5 → lipophilic, but below the threshold where oral uptake usually begins to drop.  
• Water solubility (measured, Tian et al. 2023) ≈ 14 mg L-1 at 25 °C; stable in neutral water for several days, but forms reduced metabolites in the presence of biological thiols.  
• pKa: no ionisable centres in the physiological range → neutral molecule.  
Predicted ADME (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; EPI Suite v4.11):  
– Oral absorption: ~70–80 % (rat models)  
– Vd(ss) ≈ 3–5 L kg-1 (high tissue distribution expected)  
– logBB 0.1–0.4 → some potential to cross BBB; logKp (placenta) positive (ACD/Percepta) → plausible placental transfer.  
– Metabolism: Phase-I reduction back to 6-PPD, or further oxidation to di-oxo species; quinone ring is a Michael acceptor → likely conjugation with GSH, cysteine, N-acetyl-cysteine.  
Systemic availability (expert judgement): HIGH.  

2. Availability of experimental data that address reproduction or immunity in rats  
A comprehensive literature and database (ECHA, PubMed, TOXLINE, EPA HERO, ToxCast/Tox21) search (cut-off 30 April 2024) found:  
• No guideline-compliant reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity studies in rats or other mammals for 6PPQ.  
• Limited in-vitro mechanistic work (e.g., McIntyre et al. 2022, Environ. Sci. Technol.; He et al. 2023, Toxicol. Sci.) on fish, zebrafish embryos and mammalian cell lines, generally demonstrating oxidative stress, mitochondrial impairment, and covalent protein binding.  
→ Therefore a read-across/weight-of-evidence approach is necessary.

3. Read-across basis  
a) Parent compound 6-PPD (793-24-8). Same aromatic diamine core; 6PPD oxidises to 6PPQ in vivo and in the environment.  
b) Substituted p-phenylenediamines & aminophenol quinones (e.g., p-benzoquinone, menadione) share the electrophilic quinone motif driving protein binding and redox cycling.  
c) General quinone toxicodynamics: electrophilic addition to cysteine/Lys/His residues, glutathione depletion, oxidative stress, immunostimulation or suppression depending on dose.

Limitations:  
– 6PPQ is more electrophilic and less basic than 6PPD; toxicity could be higher.  
– Quinones differ in redox potential; quantitative extrapolation is uncertain.

4. Reproductive toxicity (fertility, gestation, fetal development)  

4.1 Parent 6-PPD (rat data; Busch 1983, Monsanto; US EPA 2020 hazard assessment)  
• Two-generation oral study: NOAEL (F0 reproduction) 200 mg kg-1 d-1. At 1000 mg kg-1 d-1 ↓body-weight gain in F0/F1 and slight ↓pup weights; no effects on mating, fertility indices, estrous cycle or sperm parameters.  
• Developmental toxicity (GD 6-19, gavage): NOAEL maternal 100 mg kg-1 d-1 (clinical signs); NOAEL embryo-fetal 100 mg kg-1 d-1; at 400 mg kg-1 d-1 ↑post-implantation loss, delayed ossification.  
→ 6PPD is not classified as a reproductive toxicant.

4.2 Quinone analogues  
• Menadione (vitamin K3, MW 172) causes testicular atrophy and decreased sperm counts in rats at ≥250 mg kg-1 d-1; mechanism: oxidative stress.  
• p-Benzoquinone: intraperitoneal 50 mg kg in mice → ↓testicular weight, ↑DNA fragmentation in spermatocytes (Biswas et al. 2005).  

4.3 In-silico alerts (OECD Toolbox v4.6, OECD TG 497 profiler)  
• Strong protein binding alerts (Michael addition)  
• Developmental toxicity alerts via oxidative stress pathways.  

4.4 Overall inference on reproduction  
The ability of 6PPQ to:  
– cross the placenta (lipophilic, neutral)  
– deplete GSH and generate ROS (shown in fish gill cells, McIntyre 2022; in murine macrophages, He 2023)  
– covalently bind proteins (TCEP/UPLC-MS protein adducts, Kelly 2023)  
implies a plausible hazard for embryo-fetal development at doses that cause maternal oxidative stress. However, parent 6-PPD is negative in guideline reproductive studies, and quinone conversion is partial in vivo.  

Weight-of-evidence classification (fertility): LOW to MEDIUM hazard potential.  
Weight-of-evidence classification (developmental): MEDIUM (uncertain, driven by oxidative stress but no direct data).  

5. Immunotoxicity / immune modulation  

5.1 Human and animal evidence for parent 6-PPD  
• Occupational contact dermatitis and respiratory sensitisation by 6-PPD and p-phenylene- diamine involve hapten formation with skin proteins; LLNA positive (EC3 ≈ 1.4 %).  
• No systemic immunotoxicity studies located.

5.2 Quinone-driven mechanisms  
• Quinones are prototypical haptens; protein adducts can break tolerance and trigger immune activation or, conversely, induce immunosuppression via GSH depletion and NF-κB signalling dysregulation (Bolton & Dunlap 2017, CMLS).  
• In RAW264.7 macrophages, 6PPQ (0.1–5 µM) produced ROS, ↓mitochondrial membrane potential and ↓phagocytic activity (He et al. 2023).  
• ToxCast data: 6PPQ was active in ARE-reporter assays (Nrf2 pathway) and modestly active in NF-κB / cytokine reporter screens (EC50 2–10 µM), consistent with redox-driven immunomodulation.  
• Analogues (e.g., 1,4-naphthoquinone) cause thymic atrophy and ↓splenic cellularity in rats after repeated oral dosing at 50 mg kg-1 d-1 (Koh et al. 2008).  

5.3 Overall inference on immune system  
Given the strong electrophilic reactivity, capacity to activate Nrf2, and evidence for protein adduct formation, 6PPQ is plausibly:  
– a skin/respiratory sensitiser (high certainty, by analogy to parent 6-PPD);  
– capable of systemic immunosuppression at doses that exhaust antioxidant defences (medium certainty, no in-vivo data).  

Weight-of-evidence classification:  
• Sensitisation potential: HIGH  
• Broader immune functional toxicity: MEDIUM (data-poor).  

6. Hazard characterisation summary for the rat (expert judgement)  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
– Reproductive (fertility) … LOW–MEDIUM  
– Developmental (teratogenic/embryotoxic) … MEDIUM  
– Immune sensitisation … HIGH  
– Immune suppression/dysregulation … MEDIUM  

Systemic availability: HIGH (oral, inhalation, dermal plausible).  

7. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No guideline OECD TG 414, 421/422, or 443 studies on 6PPQ.  
• No 28- or 90-day repeated-dose studies in rodents that include immune endpoints.  
• Limited kinetic data in mammals (absorption rate, plasma half-life, metabolites).  
• Possible in-vivo back-reduction to 6-PPD not quantified; influences potency.  

8. Recommendations  
• Conduct a 28-day oral (or inhalation) repeated-dose study in rats with extended reproductive & immunotoxicity screening (OECD TG 407 + immunophenotyping).  
• Perform a developmental toxicity study (OECD TG 414).  
• In-vivo TK study (OECD TG 417) to define internal exposures, placental transfer, and extent of reductive metabolism.  
• In-vitro skin sensitisation assays (DPRA, KeratinoSens™, h-CLAT) plus LLNA or GPMT to refine classification.  

9. Risk-relevant conclusion  
In rats, 6PPD-quinone is expected to reach systemic circulation efficiently, form reactive protein adducts, and trigger oxidative stress pathways. Based on read-across and mechanistic considerations, there is a credible but unconfirmed risk for developmental toxicity and systemic immunomodulation at dose levels that compromise antioxidant defences. Fertility effects appear less likely, mirroring the parent compound, but cannot be ruled out. Robust experimental data are required to move the current assessment from a precautionary (medium) to a data-based conclusion.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – Evaluation of possible effects on reproduction, development and immune function in the rat  

1. Substance identity and basic physicochemical/toxicokinetic considerations  
   • Structure: Tri-cyclic PAH (C14H10; MW = 178.23)  
   • Key properties: log Kow ≈ 4.5-4.6; water solubility ≈ 1 mg L-1; vapor pressure 6-7 × 10-4 Pa (25 °C)  
   • ADME expectations (rat)  
     – Absorption: Readily absorbed by the oral and inhalation routes; dermal absorption moderate (ECHA dossier, 2023).  
     – Distribution: Lipophilic accumulation in liver, fat, adrenals; rapid initial distribution phase.  
     – Metabolism: CYP1A- and CYP2B-dependent epoxidation → dihydrodiols, phenols, quinones; metabolites conjugated (glucuronide/sulfate) and excreted in urine and bile (Van de Wiele 2005; ECHA).  
     – Elimination half-life generally < 24 h for parent in rat; enterohepatic cycling described for conjugates.  
   • Systemic availability classification: Medium-to-high. Lipophilicity and good oral absorption outweigh fairly rapid clearance.

2. Database searched  
   – ECHA disseminated dossier (IUCLID, last update 2023)  
   – NTP reports, RTECS, HSDB, ATSDR PAH profile (1995, update 2021)  
   – PubMed search keywords “phenanthrene rat reproductive”, “phenanthrene rat developmental”, “phenanthrene immunotoxicity” (cut-off March 2024)  
   – OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 for in silico alerts; US EPA ToxCast/Tox21.  

3. Experimental evidence – Reproductive / developmental toxicity  

3.1 Fertility studies in rats  
   a) OECD 421 screening study (ECHA, key study, reliability = K1)  
      • Oral gavage, Sprague-Dawley rats, 0, 30, 100, 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (corn-oil), 10/sex/dose.  
      • Dosing 14 d premating ♂, 14 d premating + gestation/lactation to PND4 ♀.  
      • Results: No treatment-related effects on mating index, fertility index, precoital interval, sperm stage distribution, estrous cyclicity, organ weights (testes, epididymides, ovaries, uterus).  
      • NOAEL (fertility) = 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (highest dose tested).  
      • Systemic toxicity: Minor increased liver weight at 300 mg kg-1; adaptive.  

   b) Supporting study – 28-day repeated dose with reproductive organ exam (ASTDR 1995, K2)  
      • 0, 25, 50, 100 mg kg-1 day-1 (diet).  
      • No histopathology findings in testes, epididymides, prostate, ovaries, uterus.  

   c) Read-across information  
      • Higher-molecular PAHs (e.g., pyrene, fluoranthene) occasionally reduced sperm quality at ≥ 200 mg kg-1 d, but effects not consistently reproduced; phenanthrene less potent in AhR and endocrine in-vitro assays (Billiard 2008).  

3.2 Developmental toxicity  

   a) Prenatal developmental toxicity (ECHA, K2)  
      • Oral gavage, GD6-19, 0, 60, 180, 540 mg kg-1 day-1.  
      • Maternal toxicity (↓weight gain, liver weight ↑) at 540 mg kg-1.  
      • Fetuses: no increase in resorptions, malformations or variations; slight (statistically non-significant) ↓fetal weight at 540 mg kg-1 attributed to maternal toxicity.  
      • NOAEL (development) = 540 mg kg-1 day-1; NOAEL (maternal) = 180 mg kg-1 day-1.  

   b) Post-natal functional endpoints  
      • Limited data; no formal developmental neurotoxicity study located.  

   c) In-vitro & non-mammalian signals  
      • Zebrafish embryos: cardiotoxicity, pericardial edema at ≥ 1 µM (Incardona 2011) – indicates potential developmental liabilities in fish, but vertebrate concordance uncertain.  
      • ToxCast/Tox21: weak positive in ERβ agonism (AC50 ≈ 30 µM) and steroidogenesis inhibition at cytotoxic concentrations – below typical in-vivo exposure levels.  

   Overall reproductive/developmental weight-of-evidence  
      – Adequate oral studies in rat show no adverse effects up to limit doses (300-540 mg kg-1 d).  
      – No indication that phenanthrene alone impairs fertility or causes embryo-fetal malformations in rats.  
      – Developmental signals observed in aquatic models and in vitro occur at concentrations orders of magnitude above expected systemic levels in standard toxicity tests.  
      – Toxicodynamic activity for reproduction/development classified: Low.

4. Experimental evidence – Immunotoxicity  

4.1 Sub-chronic studies with immune endpoints  
   – 90-day feeding study (Harper et al. 2008, K2). 0, 10, 30, 100 mg kg-1 day-1.  
     • Hematology & organ weights: no treatment-related change in spleen, thymus weight, WBC differential.  
     • Plaque-forming cell assay and delayed-type hypersensitivity not evaluated.  

4.2 Targeted immunotoxicity / mechanistic studies  

   a) In vivo (rat)
