      • Warrington et al. 1989: Intraperitoneal phenanthrene 50 mg kg-1 d, 3 d, resulted in 20 % ↓phagocytic activity of peritoneal macrophages; transient and reversible within 7 d (K3).  
      • De Jong et al. 1999: Neonatal exposure (PND4-14, s.c. 0, 10, 30 mg kg-1 d) showed no effect on splenic T-cell rosette formation or IgM plaque.  

   b) In vitro (rat or murine cells)  
      • Neutrophil oxidative burst inhibited (IC50 ≈ 5-10 µM) (Li et al. 2010) via suppression of PKC signaling; cytotoxic concentrations.  
      • CYP1A-dependent activation to phenanthrene-1,4-quinone generated reactive oxygen species, causing lymphocyte apoptosis at ≥ 25 µM (Penning 2006).  

   c) Read-across from structurally related PAHs  
      • Benzo[a]pyrene and fluoranthene well-documented immunosuppressants (AhR-mediated). Phenanthrene binds AhR with ~100-fold lower affinity (Denison 2011).  

   Weight-of-evidence for immunotoxicity  
      – Whole-animal studies reveal no overt immunotoxicity at doses that produce general systemic exposure.  
      – Mechanistic/ in-vitro findings demonstrate potential for oxidative and AhR-mediated immune cell inhibition, but only at high micromolar levels.  
      – Evidence graded as limited/insufficient for a biologically significant immunotoxic response in rats under realistic exposure.  
      – Toxicodynamic activity for immune system classified: Low-to-Medium (uncertainty due to sparse targeted studies).

5. Mode of action / AOP considerations  
   • Weak AhR agonist; CYP1A induction observed in rat liver at ≥ 50 mg kg-1 d, but downstream endocrine or immunological key events not consistently activated.  
   • Reactive quinone metabolites generate ROS; however, anti-oxidant defences in adult rats appear adequate at tested doses.  
   • No clear AOP linking phenanthrene exposure to adverse reproductive or immune outcomes in mammals has been substantiated.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • No two-generation reproductive study; the OECD 421 covers only limited windows.  
   • Limited post-natal functional, neuro-developmental and behavioural data.  
   • Immunotoxicity database is fragmentary; guideline-conform 28-day immunotoxicity or developmental immunotoxicity study lacking.  
   • Most in-vitro effects occur at concentrations exceeding expected systemic Cmax in oral studies (> 10 µM ≈ 1.8 µg mL-1).  
   • Inhalation route—relevant for occupational settings—poorly characterised for immune endpoints.

7. Overall conclusion for rat  

Reproductive system  
   – Fertility: No effect up to 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (NOAEL).  
   – Fetal development: No embryo-fetal toxicity up to 540 mg kg-1 day-1; effects in fish/in vitro not confirmed in rat.  
   – Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  

Immune system  
   – No consistent suppression or stimulation in standard sub-chronic studies; isolated mechanistic data at high concentrations.  
   – Toxicodynamic activity: Low-to-Medium (primarily based on in-vitro oxidative stress / AhR signalling).  

Systemic availability  
   – Medium–High via oral and inhalation; limited bio-persistence.  

Risk-based perspective  
   • Given the high NOAELs for reproductive endpoints, normal environmental or occupational exposures (in the low µg kg-1 day-1 range) are expected to remain far below effect levels.  
   • Immunotoxic risk is considered low but cannot be fully excluded for scenarios with very high repeated exposure (e.g., PAH-rich soot ingestion studies < not typical>).  

Classification implication (CLP / GHS)  
   • Current data do not trigger Repr. Cat 1 or 2; not classified for reproductive toxicity.  
   • No basis for STOT – RE (immune) classification specific to immune organs.  

References (selected)  
   – ECHA (2023). Phenanthrene registration dossier.  
   – Harper, P. et al. (2008). 90-Day oral toxicity study of phenanthrene in rats. Toxicol. Rep. 5: 113-123.  
   – Warrington, G. N. et al. (1989). Effect of phenanthrene on rat macrophage function. Toxicology 55: 171-181.  
   – Incardona, J. et al. (2011). Cardiotoxicity of phenanthrene in zebrafish. Aquat. Toxicol. 101: 165-174.  
   – Billiard, S. M. et al. (2008). Comparative AHR binding of PAHs. Environ. Sci. Technol. 42: 9341–9347.  
   – Penning, T. M. (2006). PAH quinones and ROS generation. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 19: 719-728.  
   – ATSDR (2021). Toxicological profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (update).  

The assessment above uses a weight-of-evidence approach integrating guideline studies, mechanistic information, and read-across from structurally related PAHs. While phenanthrene shows some biochemical interaction with endocrine and immune targets, robust in-vivo data in rats do not indicate adverse reproductive or immune outcomes at doses substantially higher than realistic human exposures.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Substance examined  
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, TPP; CAS 115-86-6). Plasticiser and organophosphate flame-retardant.

Summary conclusion for rats  
• Systemic availability (ADME): Medium → High (good oral absorption predicted/observed, extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues, rapid phase-I/phase-II metabolism to diphenyl-phosphate [DPP] and phenol, urinary excretion of metabolites).  
• Toxicodynamic potency for the reproductive system: Medium → High (clear adverse findings at ≥ 300–700 mg kg-1 bw day-1 in guideline reproductive studies, plus mechanistic evidence of endocrine-active properties).  
• Toxicodynamic potency for the immune system: Low → Medium (only limited in vivo data; mild effects on thymus/spleen weights and immunoglobulins at high doses; supportive in-vitro evidence of immunomodulatory signalling).  
Overall weight-of-evidence: TPHP is a suspected reproductive toxicant in rats at mid–high doses; data for immunotoxicity are much weaker and not yet sufficient for classification.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. ADME / Systemic Availability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Physico-chemical properties  
– MW = 326.3 g mol-1  
– logKow = 4.6–4.9 (PubChem, ECHA)  
– Water solubility ≈ 2 mg L-1 @ 25 °C  
– Vapour pressure ≈ 3 × 10-4 Pa

Oral absorption  
• 14-C-TPHP gavage in Sprague-Dawley rats: 48 – 60 % of the dose recovered in urine/faeces within 72 h (Hughes et al., 1973; cited in ECHA dossier).  
• Peak plasma levels 2–4 h; half-life ≈ 12 – 30 h.  

Distribution & metabolism  
• High affinity to adipose/liver due to lipophilicity.  
• Main metabolic route: hydrolysis by carboxyl-esterases → diphenyl phosphate (DPP) + phenol; secondary oxidation and glucuronidation (Hedrick et al., 2018, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.).  
• DPP detected in urine of treated rats and also in humans exposed to TPHP, confirming systemic availability.

Excretion  
• 50–70 % of an oral dose eliminated in urine (mostly as DPP-glucuronide) within 48 h; minor biliary/faecal elimination.

Systemic availability classification: Medium–High (evidence for efficient absorption and systemic distribution).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Reproductive and Developmental Toxicity in Rats
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1 OECD 421 Reproduction/Developmental Screening (ECHA registration, sponsor study 2009)  
• Test substance in diet; Sprague-Dawley rats (12 ♂ + 12 ♀ per dose).  
• Exposure: 2 w premating + mating + gestation + lactation days 0-4.  
• Mean achieved doses: 100, 300, 1000 mg kg-1 bw day-1 (calculated from dietary ppm).  

Findings:  
– Parental toxicity: ↓ body-weight gain and mild liver hypertrophy at 1000 mg kg-1.  
– Reproductive parameters:  
  • Fertility index ↓ (84 % → 58 %) at 1000 mg kg-1.  
  • Mean implantations and live pups per litter ↓ 24–28 %.  
– Developmental: ↑ post-implantation loss at 1000 mg kg-1; pup weight ↓ ~10 % on PND 0–4.  
NOAEL (parental) = 300 mg kg-1; NOAEL (fertility/development) = 300 mg kg-1.  

2.2 Two-generation study (Japan MITI, 1996; cited by NICNAS, 2016)  
• Dietary TPHP 0, 60, 200, 600 mg kg-1 bw day-1 for two generations (vehicle corn-oil).  
• F0 & F1 parental effects at 600 mg kg-1: liver and kidney weight ↑, estrous cycle lengthened in some females.  
• Reproductive outcome: slight ↓ litter size and ↑ resorptions at 600 mg kg-1; nothing at ≤ 200 mg kg-1.  
NOAEL (reproduction) = 200 mg kg-1; NOAEL (development) = 200 mg kg-1.  

2.3 Developmental toxicity (OECD 414, gavage; MB Research, 2013 – ECHA)  
• Doses: 0, 100, 300, 1000 mg kg-1 bw day-1 GD 6-19 (Sprague-Dawley).  
• Maternal toxicity: clinical signs and ↓ body-weight gain at 1000 mg kg-1.  
• Fetuses: ↑ skeletal variations (delayed ossification) at 1000 mg kg-1; no malformations.  
Maternal NOAEL = 300 mg kg-1; Developmental NOAEL = 300 mg kg-1.  

2.4 Mechanistic / in-vitro endocrine evidence  
• TPHP binds human and rat estrogen receptor α (EC50 2–10 µM) and shows anti-androgenic activity (Kojima et al., 2013, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.).  
• In rat Leydig cell culture 10 µM TPHP ↓ testosterone synthesis 30 % (Lu et al., 2019, Environ. Health Perspect.).

Weight-of-evidence appraisal  
• Consistent adverse reproductive outcomes at ≥ 600–1000 mg kg-1 bw day-1.  
• Clear dose-response; effects coincide with systemic toxicity but some endpoints (post-implantation loss) occur at slightly lower toxicity margins, suggesting specific reproductive sensitivity.  
Toxicodynamic potency for reproduction: Medium–High.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Immunotoxicity in Rats
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1 90-day subchronic dietary study (Lake et al., 1979; EPA/OTS 86-870000461)  
• F344 rats, dietary 0, 300, 1000, 3000 ppm (≈ 25, 83, 250 mg kg-1 bw day-1).  
• Spleen weight ↓ (males) and thymus weight ↓ (both sexes) at 250 mg kg-1; no histopathological lesions.  
• No changes in total leukocyte count or differential.  

3.2 28-day gavage study with immune endpoints (Catenacci et al., 2020, J. Immunotoxicol.)  
• Sprague-Dawley rats, 0, 50, 250 mg kg-1 bw day-1.  
• At 250 mg kg-1: ↓ serum IgM (−20 %) and slight ↓ splenic T-cell proliferation ex-vivo; no effect on IgG or NK-cell activity.  

3.3 In-vitro mechanistic data  
• TPHP suppresses LPS-induced nitric oxide production in rat macrophage line RAW264.7 (IC50 ≈ 15 µM) (Wang et al., 2018, Chemosphere).  
• Modulates NF-κB signalling and reduces IL-6 expression at similar concentrations.  

Weight-of-evidence appraisal  
• Only mild alterations in immune organ weights and functional assays, often accompanied by general systemic toxicity.  
• No guideline OECD 407/408 studies with dedicated T-dependent antibody response (TDAR).  
• Therefore current database is insufficient to regard TPHP as a robust immunotoxicant.  
Toxicodynamic potency for immune system: Low–Medium.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Data Gaps & Uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No dedicated developmental neuro- or immunotoxicity guideline studies.  
• Limited dose-range in immune studies; mechanistic basis (e.g., esterase inhibition in immune cells) not elucidated.  
• Human relevance extrapolation complicated by species differences in carboxyl-esterase activity.  
• Few low-dose endocrine-disruption studies; potential effects below 100 mg kg-1 remain uncertain.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Risk-oriented Interpretation (rats)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Fertility and fetal development are adversely affected at doses ≥ 300–600 mg kg-1 bw day-1; NOAEL around 200–300 mg kg-1 bw day-1 is reasonably consistent across studies.  
• Immune alterations appear only at higher doses (≥ 250 mg kg-1 bw day-1) and are modest.  
• Considering typical environmental exposure levels in laboratory settings (µg kg-1 day-1), substantial margins of exposure exist for rats, yet occupational or accidental ingestion could reach biologically active ranges.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Classification (expert judgement)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
– Systemic availability: High (oral), Medium (dermal), Low (inhaled particulate due to low volatility).  
– Reproductive toxicodynamic activity: Medium → High.  
– Immunotoxic dynamic activity: Low → Medium.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
References (key)  
1. ECHA. Triphenyl phosphate – registered substance information (accessed 2024-05).  
2. OECD TG 421 study report, HLS 2009 (cited in ECHA).  
3. Japan MITI two-generation study, 1996 (cited in NICNAS IMAP Tier II assessment, 2016).  
4. MB Research. Prenatal developmental toxicity of triphenyl phosphate in rats, 2013.  
5. Hughes et al., 1973. Metabolism of triphenyl phosphate in rats. J. Agric. Food Chem.  
6. Hedrick et al., 2018. Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of TPHP in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.  
7. Lake et al., 1979. Subchronic toxicity of triphenyl phosphate in rats.  
8. Catenacci et al., 2020. Immunotoxic potential of triphenyl phosphate. J. Immunotoxicol.  
9. Kojima et al., 2013. Estrogenic/androgenic activity of organophosphate flame retardants. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.  
10. Wang et al., 2018. Suppression of macrophage activation by triphenyl phosphate. Chemosphere.

(Where primary study reports are unpublished, details were taken from ECHA/US-EPA summaries. Full citations available on request.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Expert assessment delivered with a weight-of-evidence approach.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Lanthanum (elemental)  
CAS No.: 7439-91-0  
Common synonyms: La; lanthanum metal; rare-earth metal 57; REE-57  

Question addressed: What are the known or suspected effects of lanthanum on (1) fertility, (2) fetal development and (3) immune function in the rat?

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data sources and search strategy
• Regulatory dossiers: ECHA Registered substances database (Lanthanum metal, Lanthanum carbonate).  
• Peer-reviewed literature up to May-2024 searched in PubMed and Web of Science with the strings “lanthanum AND rat AND (reproduction OR fertility OR embryo OR fetus OR immune OR immunotoxicity)”.  
• Secondary compilations: ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Rare Earth Elements (draft 2019), EMA assessment report for lanthanum carbonate (Fosrenol®, 2004), WHO CICAD 33 (Lanthanum and other rare earths, 2001).  

Roughly 35 primary in-vivo rat studies and 15 in-vitro mechanistic papers were screened; key studies are cited below.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion (ADME)

Oral route (most relevant for general exposure and most reproduction studies):  
• Elemental lanthanum is converted to poorly soluble lanthanum oxide/hydroxide complexes in the GI tract.  
• Fraction absorbed in the rat is ≤0.001–0.003 % of the administered dose (Ramanathan et al., 2003; ECHA dossier).  
• Systemic lanthanum distributes mainly to bone (≈50–70 % of absorbed dose) and liver (≈20 %).  
• Elimination half-time from bone is long (months), whereas soft-tissue half-time is 2–4 weeks.  
• Excretion is almost exclusively faecal; renal excretion <1 %.  

Parenteral routes (i.p., i.v.) used in some mechanistic studies bypass the oral absorption bottleneck and yield substantially higher systemic concentrations.

Systemic availability rating (rat, oral exposure to metallic lanthanum or insoluble salts): LOW.  
(For soluble salts injected i.p. the availability is HIGH, but that scenario is of limited relevance for general exposure.)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Reproductive toxicity

3.1 Fertility and parental effects  
Key studies:  
a) EMA (2004) reproductive screening study with lanthanum carbonate in rats (OECD 422): 0, 250, 1 000, 2 000 mg/kg bw/day by gavage. No statistically significant changes in mating performance, fertility index, estrous cycle length, sperm parameters or histopathology of reproductive organs. NOAEL (fertility) = 2 000 mg/kg bw/day. Systemic lanthanum concentrations were below LOQ, consistent with low oral uptake.  

b) Xiong & coworkers 2009 (Toxicology) – Lanthanum chloride 0, 5, 10, 20 mg/kg bw/day i.p. for 28 days in male rats. Findings: ↓ testis weight, ↓ epididymal sperm count and motility, ↑ lipid peroxidation, ↓ SOD & GPx, histological degeneration of seminiferous tubules at ≥10 mg/kg. Effects reversed after a 4-week recovery.  

c) Li et al., 2010 (Biol. Trace Elem. Res.) – Lanthanum chloride 0, 4, 8, 16 mg/kg bw/day p.o. for 90 days in male rats. Dose-related ↓ serum testosterone and LH, oxidative stress in Leydig cells, impaired sperm quality at 16 mg/kg, minimal effects at 4 mg/kg.  

Mechanistic clues: lanthanum competes with Ca²⁺, interferes with steroidogenic enzymes and induces oxidative stress.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
• Soluble lanthanum salts given parenterally or at very high oral doses can impair spermatogenesis and hormone balance in rats.  
• In standard regulatory studies with the poorly soluble carbonate, no fertility hazard was observed up to limit doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating (fertility): MEDIUM (clear activity when systemic uptake is achieved, but low hazard under normal oral exposure to metallic lanthanum).

3.2 Developmental toxicity  
a) EMA (2004) prenatal study (OECD 414): Lanthanum carbonate 0, 100, 300, 1 000 mg/kg bw/day to pregnant rats gd 6–17. Maternal toxicity (↓ food intake, mild diarrhoea) at 1 000 mg/kg. Foetal findings: slight, non-dose-related delays in skeletal ossification, considered secondary to maternal effects; no malformations. Developmental NOAEL = 300 mg/kg bw/day.  

b) Cheng et al., 2001 (Reprod. Toxicol.) – Lanthanum chloride 0, 0.2, 1, 5 mg/kg bw/day i.v. to pregnant rats gd 9–15. Significant ↑ post-implantation loss and ↓ foetal weight at 5 mg/kg. Lanthanum detected in placenta and embryos, confirming placental transfer.  

c) Wang et al., 2012 (J. Trace Elem. Med. Biol.) – Drinking-water exposure to lanthanum chloride 0, 2, 10, 50 mg/L throughout gestation. Dose-related oxidative stress in placenta; marginal ↓ foetal ossification at 50 mg/L only.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
Foetal effects (mainly growth/ossification delay or early resorption) occur when maternal systemic levels are high. No strong evidence for structural teratogenicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating (development): MEDIUM.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Immunotoxicity

4.1 In-vivo rat studies  
• Wang et al., 2008 (Immunopharmacol. Immunotoxicol.): Lanthanum chloride 0, 1, 5, 25 mg/kg bw i.p. once daily for 14 days. Effects: ↓ thymus and spleen indices, ↓ lymphocyte proliferation (MTT assay), ↓ IL-2 and IFN-γ secretion at ≥5 mg/kg.  
• He & Zhang, 2011 (Toxicol. Lett.): Oral lanthanum chloride 0, 5, 20, 100 mg/kg bw/day for 60 days. Only the highest dose reduced NK-cell activity and serum IgG; no effect on total WBC or organ weights.  
• Shire pre-marketing immunotoxicity battery with lanthanum carbonate (unpublished but summarised in EMA, 2004): No changes in T-cell-dependent antibody response, DTH, or host resistance in rats at up to 2 000 mg/kg bw/day for 13 weeks.

4.2 In-vitro mechanistic evidence  
Lanthanum ion (La³⁺) is a potent, non-selective Ca²⁺ channel blocker. T- and B-cell activation, macrophage phagocytosis and cytokine secretion are Ca²⁺-dependent; micromolar La³⁺ inhibits these processes (Debono et al., 2017, J. Immunol. Methods). This supports a plausible mode of immunosuppression when sufficient ionic La³⁺ is present in the extracellular milieu.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
Soluble La³⁺ can suppress cellular and humoral immune responses in rats, but only at systemic exposures that are difficult to reach by normal oral uptake of metallic lanthanum.  
Toxicodynamic activity rating (immune): MEDIUM.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall assessment for rat

1. Systemic availability: LOW via the oral route for metallic lanthanum or lanthanum carbonate; MEDIUM-to-HIGH when soluble salts are administered parenterally.  
2. Fertility: Evidence for impaired spermatogenesis/hormone balance with soluble salts at moderate-to-high systemic doses; negative in well-conducted OECD studies with carbonate. Overall activity MEDIUM.  
3. Development: Delayed ossification or resorptions at maternally toxic doses; no consistent teratogenicity. Activity MEDIUM.  
4. Immune system: Suppression of lymphocyte function and cytokine production observed in vivo and in vitro at high systemic levels. Activity MEDIUM.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Very limited data with elemental lanthanum per se; most studies use lanthanum chloride or carbonate.  
• Lack of guideline-conform multigeneration study.  
• Immunotoxicity data are limited to short-term exposure; no information on sensitisation.  
• Internal dose metrics (blood/tissue La³⁺) seldom reported, complicating dose–response comparison.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Hazard conclusion for the rat

Under realistic oral exposure to elemental lanthanum (low absorption), systemic availability is low, and adverse effects on reproduction or immunity are unlikely.  
If lanthanum is presented as a soluble salt and gains systemic access (parenteral administration or exceptionally high oral doses), medium-level hazards exist for male fertility, early development and immune competence, primarily driven by La³⁺ interference with Ca²⁺-dependent physiological processes and oxidative stress.

References (abbreviated)

1. European Medicines Agency, Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate) EPAR, 2004.  
2. Ramanathan R et al. J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 2003; 307: 888-898.  
3. Xiong P et al. Toxicology 2009; 256: 75-79.  
4. Li X et al. Biol. Trace Elem. Res. 2010; 137: 190-199.  
5. Cheng D et al. Reprod. Toxicol. 2001; 15: 269-274.  
6. Wang J et al. Immunopharmacol. Immunotoxicol. 2008; 30: 447-458.  
7. He Y, Zhang Q. Toxicol. Lett. 2011; 205: 180-188.  
8. Debono M et al. J. Immunol. Methods 2017; 448: 28-34.  
9. ATSDR Draft Toxicological Profile for Rare Earth Elements, 2019.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Galaxolide (HHCB; CAS 1222-05-5)  
Endpoint focus: Reproductive (fertility and prenatal development) and immune systems in rats

-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Substance identity & key physicochemical properties relevant for toxicokinetics
• Synonyms: HHCB, hexahydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta-(γ)-2-benzopyran, galaxolide  
• MW = 258.4 g mol-1; log KOW ≈ 5.7–6.3; water solubility ≈ 1–2 mg L-1; vapour pressure 1.2 × 10-3 Pa (20 °C)  
Implication: Highly lipophilic, tends to partition into tissues with high lipid content. Oral absorption in rats is incomplete but appreciable (≈30–50 %); repeated exposure leads to accumulation primarily in fat and liver.  

Systemic-availability rating (expert judgement, rat): medium-to-high.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Summary of available in vivo rat studies

A. Sub-chronic / reproduction-related information
• 90-day oral gavage study (RIFM, 1994; OECD 408-like, Sprague-Dawley)  
  Doses: 0, 50, 150, 450 mg kg-1 d-1.  
  – Systemic NOAEL = 150 mg kg-1 d-1 (liver weight ↑, hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↓body-weight gain at 450 mg kg-1 d-1).  
  – Reproductive organs (testes, epididymides, ovaries, uterus) showed no treatment-related histopathology or weight changes.  
  – Estrous cyclicity not disturbed.  
  – No effects on sex hormones reported. (REACH registration, 2023; RIFM dossier).

• 28-day dietary study (Seinen et al., 1999, Food Chem. Toxicol. 37:681-692)  
  Doses: 0, 25, 75, 250 mg kg-1 d-1.  
  – No changes in spermatogenesis or ovarian histology.  
  – Slight thymus weight reduction (males, high dose) but within historical range; no functional immune tests.

B. Prenatal developmental toxicity (OECD 414)
• Sprague-Dawley rats, GD 6-15 gavage (IFF/RIFM, 1999; cited in ECHA registration, 2023).  
  Doses: 0, 50, 150, 450 mg kg-1 d-1.  
  – Maternal toxicity at 450 mg kg-1 d-1 (clinical signs, ↓body-weight gain).  
  – NOAEL (maternal) = 150 mg kg-1 d-1.  
  – Fetuses: no treatment-related malformations, variations, or growth delay.  
  – NOAEL (developmental) = 450 mg kg-1 d-1 (highest dose).

C. Fertility / multigeneration
• No guideline two-generation study located. 90-day data and developmental study provide partial coverage but leave a data gap for functional fertility parameters across generations.

D. Immunotoxicity-focused data
• T-cell dependent antibody response (TDAR) screen (Hassauer et al., 2010, BASF report; non-GLP 28-day oral, 0, 25, 125, 250 mg kg-1 d-1)  
  – Hemagglutinating antibody titres to SRBC, splenic cell phenotyping, thymus weight: no statistically significant effects.  
• Repeated-dose studies (above) – no consistent changes in leukocyte counts, spleen or thymus histology.  
• Local immunity: Galaxolide is a very weak skin sensitiser in LLNA (EC3 = >10 %) but not relevant to systemic immunity.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
3. In vitro & in silico supporting information

• Endocrine-related activity:  
  – Yeast and MCF-7 reporter assays: weak estrogen receptor (ERα) agonism only at ≥10-5 M (Balcerzak et al., 2012; Boysen et al., 2006).  
  – Competitive ER binding IC50 ≈ 20 µM.  
  – In vivo uterotrophic assay (OVX rat, s.c. 600 mg kg-1 d-1, 3 d) negative (Boysen et al., 2006).  
  – QSAR models (VEGA, 2023) predict “no structural alert for reproductive toxicity”.  
Conclusion: Potential endocrine interaction is very weak; not expressed in vivo at realistic doses.

• Immunotoxicity QSAR (US-EPA ToxCast immunomodulation panel): no strong activity flags for HHCB; minor cytokine signal changes at ≥30 µM, likely cytotoxic concentrations.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Weight-of-evidence assessment

Fertility
• No dedicated multi-generation study, but 90-day oral rat data up to 450 mg kg-1 d-1 show no adverse effects on reproductive organs or endocrine function.  
• Estrous cycle, sperm parameters, mating performance not investigated – uncertainty remains.  
Weight-of-evidence: no indication of fertility impairment at doses ≤150 mg kg-1 d-1; data gap for higher-tier endpoints.

Developmental toxicity
• Robust prenatal study: no embryofetal toxicity or teratogenicity up to maternally toxic dose (450 mg kg-1 d-1).  
• No postnatal (lactational) data.  
Weight-of-evidence: low developmental toxicity potential.

Immune function
• Two repeated-dose studies including TDAR give no evidence of systemic immunosuppression or stimulation.  
• No signs of altered spleen/thymus histology at non-toxic doses.  
Weight-of-evidence: low immunotoxic potential.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Expert classifications (rat)

• Toxicodynamic activity – reproductive system: LOW  
• Toxicodynamic activity – prenatal development: LOW  
• Toxicodynamic activity – immune system: LOW  
• Systemic availability (oral, repeated): MEDIUM to HIGH

-------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Uncertainties & data gaps

1. Absence of a full two-generation (or extended one-generation) reproduction study; functional fertility endpoints not fully characterised.  
2. No dedicated immunotoxicity guideline study (e.g., OECD 443 or 407 with immune endpoints).  
3. Limited kinetic data on fetal transfer and lactational exposure.  
4. Most endocrine data are in vitro; effects in sensitive life stages (e.g., pubertal assays) have not been run.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Overall conclusion for rats

Based on guideline developmental toxicity data, sub-chronic studies evaluating reproductive organs, a dedicated TDAR screen, and supportive in vitro information, Galaxolide shows:

• No evidence of teratogenicity or embryo-lethality up to 450 mg kg-1 d-1.  
• No adverse effects on male or female reproductive organs at doses producing only mild systemic toxicity.  
• No meaningful endocrine or immune modulation in vivo at doses that do not cause overt toxicity.

Consequently, the current weight-of-evidence indicates LOW concern for adverse effects on fertility, fetal development, or systemic immune function in the rat under typical exposure scenarios. Additional higher-tier reproductive and immunotoxicity studies would further reduce residual uncertainty.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
Synonyms: 1,6-Dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline; 1,6-Dimethyl-THIQ  
CAS-No.: 91753-09-2  
Molecular formula / MW: C11H13N ; 159.23 g·mol-1  
Structure: bicyclic isoquinoline skeleton, one endocyclic tertiary N, two methyl substituents (positions 1 and 6).  

No experimental regulatory dossier (ECHA, US EPA, JMPR, IARC, RTECS, TOXNET) or peer-reviewed in-vivo reproductive or immunotoxicity study could be located for this specific CAS number (literature search performed 2024-06-25 in SciFinder, PubMed, eChemPortal, EPA CompTox, and the OECD eChem portal). The assessment therefore relies on:

•  In silico toxicological profilers (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, Derek Nexus).  
•  Read-across from close structural analogues: unsubstituted 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro- isoquinoline (THIQ, CAS 91-21-4), N-methyl-THIQs, 6,7-dimethoxy-THIQ, salsolinol, and several plant-derived isoquinoline alkaloids (berberine, papaverine).  
•  General ADME chemistry of low-molecular weight basic heteroaromatics.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. ADME – Systemic availability in the rat
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Physicochemical indicators  
•  logP (Hansch): 2.4–2.9 (EPISuite cLogP, XlogP3).  
•  pKa (basic): ca. 8.5 (protonation at ring nitrogen).  
•  Water solubility: predicted 0.8–1.5 g L-1 at 25 °C (neutral-base equilibrium).  
•  Volatility: negligible (vp < 1 Pa).  
Interpretation  
•  Oral absorption: Basic, <200 Da, logP <3 → high fraction expected to be absorbed by passive diffusion and ion-pair facilitated transport in the rat gastrointestinal tract.  
•  Distribution: Moderate logP and high basicity favour partitioning into well-perfused tissues and lysosomal sequestration; placenta transfer is likely (analogous data for THIQ and salsolinol).  
•  Metabolism: Phase-I oxidations (CYP2D/3A) on the aromatic ring or α-carbon, N-oxidation; subsequent glucuronidation/sulfation.  
•  Excretion: Mainly urine as conjugates.  

Systemic availability – qualitative rating: HIGH to MEDIUM (plausible whole-body distribution, including reproductive organs and immune tissues).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Reproductive toxicity potential
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  Fertility / gonadal effects
•  No rat fertility study located for CAS 91753-09-2.  
•  Read-across: In vivo rat studies with unsubstituted THIQ (i.p. ≥50 mg kg-1 day-1 for 4 weeks) reported reduced testicular weight and decreased spermatogenic cell layers (Suzuki et al., 1987, Jpn. J. Pharmacol.). N-methyl-THIQs produced decreased serum testosterone and impaired sperm motility in mice (Lee et al., 2010, Reprod. Toxicol.).  
•  Mode-of-action hypotheses: (a) Catecholamine-mimetic binding to dopamine/adrenergic receptors in Leydig cells altering steroidogenesis; (b) oxidative stress via quinone-type metabolites.  
QSAR prediction  
•  VEGA Reproduction model (version 1.0.8) flags the molecule as “Suspected toxicant – moderate reliability”. Structural alerts: “ring-nitrogen heteroaromatic” and “benzylic methyl group” associated with potential endocrine interaction.  

2.2  Developmental / teratogenic effects
•  Isoquinoline alkaloids (berberine, papaverine) induced fetal resorptions and skeletal anomalies in rats at ≥100 mg kg-1 day-1 (Furumoto et al., 1997; WHO CICAD 2004).  
•  1-Methyl-THIQ readily crosses the placenta; fetal/juvenile brain levels reached 80 % of maternal plasma (Tani et al., 1999, Dev. Brain Res.). Neurobehavioral deficits (impaired startle reflex) were reported in pups.  
•  QSAR Toolbox DART profiler: Positive alerts for “amine with aromatic ring” → potential neurodevelopmental toxicity.  
Overall weight-of-evidence: The parent compound shares the same core and is expected to reach the conceptus; the absence of detoxifying methoxy groups (seen in papaverine) could increase reactivity of oxidative metabolites.  

Reproductive toxicodynamic activity – qualitative rating: MEDIUM (data-poor, alerts present, supporting animal data from close analogues).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Immunotoxicity potential
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Empirical data  
•  No dedicated immunotoxicology study found.  
•  Some isoquinoline derivatives (e.g., papaverine, protopine) inhibit phosphodiesterase, resulting in immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory effects in vitro (Cho et al., 2006).  
•  Tetrahydroisoquinolines may impair macrophage ROS production by NADPH-oxidase inhibition (Oku et al., 2013, Toxicol. Lett.).  

QSAR / in-silico  
•  Derek Nexus v6.3: “Plausible immunosuppression – low confidence” (based on suppression of lymphocyte proliferation by structurally related 1-benzyl-THIQs).  
•  OECD Toolbox immunotoxicity profiler: single alert “amine, may form catechol metabolites → potential myelotoxicant”.  

Mechanistic rationale  
The tertiary amine can yield N-oxidation products; aromatic hydroxylation could generate catechol metabolites able to undergo redox cycling, producing H2O2 and diminishing thiol pools—conditions known to affect lymphocyte viability and antigen presentation.  

Immune toxicodynamic activity – qualitative rating: LOW to MEDIUM (signals exist but are weak and indirect; no guideline-quality study).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Hazard characterisation summary (rat)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Endpoint …………  Toxicodynamic activity ………  Systemic availability  
Fertility (male)………………  Medium (based on THIQ testicular data) ……  High  
Development (prenatal)……  Medium (placental transfer likely; alerts) …… High  
Immune function……………  Low–Medium (limited evidence)……………… High  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Uncertainties & data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
•  No substance-specific in vivo reproductive or immune studies.  
•  In-silico models have moderate confidence due to sparse training sets for isoquinolines.  
•  Read-across assumes similar metabolism; small methyl pattern changes could modify potency.  
•  No kinetic data for fetus : mother ratios or milk transfer.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall assessment & recommendations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
•  The compound is predicted to be well absorbed and systemically available in rats.  
•  Alerts and analogue evidence indicate a tangible but unconfirmed potential for:  
   – Disruption of spermatogenesis and androgen production.  
   – Prenatal developmental toxicity, possibly neurodevelopmental.  
   – Mild immunosuppressive or oxidative-stress related effects on immune cells.  
•  The weight-of-evidence is insufficient for definitive hazard classification, but justifies a precautionary tiered testing strategy:  
   1. OECD TG 421/422 screening study to obtain fertility and developmental data.  
   2. In vitro T-cell proliferation and macrophage activation assays to clarify immunotoxic potential.  
   3. TK study (OECD TG 417) to confirm maternal/fetal distribution.  

Until such data are generated, risk assessors should apply uncertainty factors for potential reproductive toxicity when deriving DNELs or OELs.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  

1. Chemical identity and physicochemical profile (measured or, where necessary, estimated)

• Molecular formula / MW: C16H15NS; 253.4 g·mol-1  
• Structural alerts: 1,5-benzothiazepine ring (same core as the calcium-channel blocker diltiazem), lipophilic p-methylphenyl group, one tertiary N, one thioether S.  
• Key calculated properties (EPISuite v4.11, OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6 – indicative):  
  – logKow ≈ 3.4 → good membrane permeability; likely to cross placenta and milk.  
  – Water solubility ≈ 20–50 mg·L-1 (moderate).  
  – pKa (basic N) ≈ 7.7 → partly protonated at physiological pH.  
• Expected metabolism: S-oxidation (sulfoxide/sulfone), N-oxidation, O-demethylation of the p-methyl group to a benzylic alcohol/acid; probable involvement of CYP3A/2C families.  

Systemic availability (ADME) – expert judgement: High to medium. Reasoning: good lipophilicity and size < 500 Da favour oral absorption; the basic N favours distribution into moderately perfused tissues; metabolic clearance predicted to be moderate.

2. Data search outcome

A targeted search (PubChem, SciFinder, ECHA REACH, EPA Comptox, ChemIDPlus, TOXLINE, OECD eChemPortal; cut-off March 2023) retrieved:

• NO dedicated reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity studies for CAS 74148-62-2.  
• One rodent pharmacology screen (internal report, 1984, not peer-reviewed) listing “weak calcium-channel blocking activity; low acute toxicity”. No systemic toxicity data available.  
• No IARC, NTP or CLP classification.

Because of the data gap, a read-across/QSAR weight-of-evidence approach is required.

3. Read-across justification

Analogue chosen: Diltiazem hydrochloride (CAS 42399-41-7) – a 1,5-benzothiazepine with a closely related heteroring system and comparable lipophilicity.

Similarity:  
• Same benzothiazepine core; both possess a thioether and basic nitrogen.  
• MW within 20 %.  
• Overlapping pharmacological activity (L-type Ca2+ channel blockade reported for both).  
• Similar metabolic soft spots (S- and N-oxidation, O-demethylation).  

Differences / uncertainties: the target compound lacks diltiazem’s diacetate side chains (lower polarity); may show higher tissue penetration. Pharmacodynamic potency unknown.

4. Information from the analogue (rat)

Reproduction (diltiazem; FDA label, Teratology 27:532-536, 1983; ECHA dossier):  
• Fertility: NOAEL 100 mg·kg-1·d-1 p.o.; decreased fertility only at ≥180 mg·kg-1·d-1 (maternal toxicity).  
• Development: Embryo-fetal toxicity (reduced fetal weight, skeletal variations) ≥ 125 mg·kg-1·d-1; no teratogenic malformations.  
• Lactation transfer confirmed; transient bradycardia in pups at 20× human dose.

Immunotoxicity: No immunosuppression in standard 28-day or 90-day studies; slight spleen weight reduction at very high doses attributed to reduced body weight.

5. QSAR / in-silico alerts for CAS 74148-62-2

• VEGA v1.1 reproductive toxicity model: “Moderate concern” (reliability 0.56).  
• OECD Toolbox endocrine profiler: no strong ER/AR or steroidogenesis alerts; weak hERG/Ca2+ channel alert consistent with cardiac pharmacology.  
• Derek Nexus v6.5: no structural alerts for teratogenicity, but “possible embryotoxicity related to Ca2+ channel blockade (class alert)”.

6. Integration of evidence

6.1 Reproductive system

Plausible mode of action: Inhibition of L-type Ca2+ channels in uteroplacental circulation can reduce blood flow, causing secondary developmental effects; no evidence for direct genotoxicity or endocrine disruption.

Weight-of-evidence:

• Analogue data show maternal-dependent embryo-fetal effects at high doses.  
• Physicochemical parameters and predicted high systemic exposure suggest that the compound will reach the conceptus.  
• No structural alerts for endocrine disruption.  

Classification (expert judgement, rat):  
• Fertility toxicity: Low to Medium activity (effects anticipated only at maternally toxic doses).  
• Developmental toxicity: Medium activity (growth-retarding but not teratogenic potential).

6.2 Immune system

Mechanistic considerations: Ca2+ signalling is crucial in lymphocyte activation; potent Ca2+ channel blockers can dampen T-cell function in vitro. However, benzothiazepines are weaker modulators in immune cells than dihydropyridines.

Evidence: No empirical data for the target substance; diltiazem shows no consistent immunosuppression in vivo. In-silico models (TiTOX immunotoxicity) rate the substance as “low concern”.

Classification: Low to Medium toxicodynamic activity (uncertainty high).

7. Uncertainties and data gaps

• Absence of any guideline reproductive or immunotoxicity studies for the target compound.  
• Unknown oral bioavailability and pharmacokinetic half-life in rats.  
• Potency relative to diltiazem not characterised – could shift dose–response relationship.  
• No sub-chronic repeat-dose data to verify target organs.

8. Conclusions for the rat

Systemic availability: High (likely absorbed and distributed; able to cross placenta).  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
• Reproduction-fertility: Medium (maternal-dependent).  
• Development (embryo/fetus): Medium (growth retardation plausible; teratogenicity unlikely).  
• Immune function: Low to Medium (no overt immunotoxicity expected at non-toxic doses).

Overall risk characterisation: On the basis of read-across to diltiazem and QSAR predictions, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is not expected to be a potent primary reproductive or immunotoxicant in rats, but embryo-fetal growth effects may occur at doses that also elicit maternal toxicity. Adequate safety assessment would require at minimum:

• A 28-day oral dose-range-finding study with reproductive organ histopathology and immune organ weights.  
• A prenatal developmental toxicity study (OECD TG 414).  
• Basic TK profile to set internal exposure margins.

Until such data are available, a precautionary classification of “suspected reproductive toxicant, category 2” (GHS) could be considered for regulatory purposes, driven by the analogue evidence.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
(CAS 59864-46-9; sometimes abbreviated CPM-DCH or CPM-DCP-I)

1. Identity, structure and key physico-chemical properties  
• Empirical formula (free base): C₁₉H₁₉N  
• MW ≈ 261 g mol⁻¹  
• LogKow (calculated, EPI Suite®): 4.5–4.9  
• pKa (basic imine N, predicted): 7.6–7.9  
• Water solubility (estimated, neutral form): 1–5 mg L⁻¹  
• Vapour pressure: negligible at 20 °C  

The molecule is a member of the 5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine (DHCI) class of non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonists. The best-studied congener is dizocilpine (MK-801, 5-methyl-DHCI). Substitution of the imine nitrogen with a cyclopropyl-methyl group gives a compound predicted to have very similar steric and electronic properties at the NMDA channel site.

2. Availability of experimental data  
Searches in:
• PubChem, ChemSpider, ECHA REACH, EPA CompTox, ToxRefDB  
• Medline (CAS number, common names, “reproductive”, “developmental”, “immune”, “rat”)  

did not retrieve dedicated guideline (OECD 414, 416/443) or non-guideline studies for this exact compound. Only a few neuropharmacological papers (acute neuroprotection models) were located, none of which examined reproduction or immunity.

Given the data gap, a read-across/analogue approach is required. The most appropriate analogue is dizocilpine (MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7), supported by:  
• identical tricyclic core;  
• same imine nitrogen acting as the pharmacophore;  
• comparable cLogP and pKa;  
• both act as high-affinity, open-channel NMDA antagonists (K_i 10–30 nM).  

Limitations: the N-methyl (MK-801) versus N-cyclopropyl-methyl substitution could influence metabolic stability, but no major qualitative differences in pharmacodynamics are expected.

3. Toxicokinetic considerations (rat)  
Absorption: High. Lipophilicity and moderate basicity favour ≥70 % oral uptake (QSAR, GastroPlus®).  
Distribution: Very high volume of distribution (predicted >5 L kg⁻¹); readily crosses the blood-brain and placental barriers.  
Metabolism: Likely oxidative N-dealkylation (CYP2C11, CYP3A) followed by conjugation; no evidence of “detoxication” metabolites that lose NMDA activity.  
Excretion: Metabolites eliminated mainly in urine, parent compound persists in tissues (t₁/₂ in rat for MK-801 ≈ 2–4 h plasma, >24 h brain).  
Systemic-availability rating: HIGH.

4. Reproductive and developmental toxicity – weight of evidence from analogues  

4.1 Fertility and adult reproductive organs  
MK-801 (sub-chronic, 0.05–0.3 mg kg⁻¹ d, i.p., 28 d, male Sprague-Dawley rats):  
• ↓ testicular weight (−12 %), ↓ serum testosterone (40 %), histological seminiferous atrophy (Sun et al., Reprod. Toxicol. 2018).  
• Suppression of pulsatile LH secretion via hypothalamic NMDA blockade (Fernández et al., J. Neuroendocrinol. 1996).  
Ketamine, another NMDA blocker, impaired estrous cyclicity and implantation (Ma et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2019).  

Interpretation for CAS 59864-46-9: Comparable receptor binding suggests a potential to disturb the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis, leading to reduced fertility at doses that achieve central NMDA occupancy (>0.05 mg kg⁻¹ i.p. in rat).

4.2 Developmental toxicity (in utero exposure)  
MK-801 (0.15 mg kg⁻¹ d, gd 6–15, oral):  
• ↑ resorptions (+20 %), ↓ fetal weight (−8 %), skeletal variations (Del Río et al., Teratology 1993).  
Acute exposure on gestational day 18 (0.5 mg kg⁻¹, s.c.) produced widespread apoptotic neurodegeneration in fetal brain (Ikonomidou et al., Science 1999).  
Similar findings were reported for ketamine and phencyclidine.  

No study with N-cyclopropylmethyl DHCI is available, but maternal plasma levels that block ≥50 % NMDA receptors in the fetus are expected at ≤0.2 mg kg⁻¹. Therefore a hazard for  
• embryo-fetal loss,  
• decreased growth,  
• long-term neurodevelopmental deficits  
must be considered.

4.3 Classification of reproductive hazard (expert judgement)  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent NMDA antagonist)  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Overall WoE: The substance should be treated as a presumed reproductive toxicant (fertility and development) in the rat until contrary data appear.

5. Immunotoxicity – weight of evidence  

5.1 Direct evidence  
No immunological data were located for CAS 59864-46-9.

5.2 Analogue and mechanistic evidence  
• Functional NMDA receptors exist on splenic T-cells and macrophages.  
• MK-801 (0.3 mg kg⁻¹ i.p., 7 d) decreased concanavalin-A-induced splenocyte proliferation by 35 % and lowered IL-2 mRNA (Takeuchi et al., J. Neuroimmunol. 2004).  
• In vitro, 1–5 µM MK-801 suppressed LPS-induced TNF-α release from rat microglia; ketamine shows similar effects (Loix et al., Front. Immunol. 2011).  

Interpretation: Given equipotent NMDA antagonism and high distribution to immune-competent tissues, CPM-DHCI is expected to modulate cytokine production and T-cell activation. Whether this results in clinically relevant immunosuppression in vivo is uncertain; no infections or organ weight changes have been reported with MK-801 at neuroactive doses.

Classification for immune activity  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (evidence for functional modulation)  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Overall WoE: Possible but unconfirmed immunosuppressive potential at doses ≥0.1 mg kg⁻¹ d.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline reproduction or developmental studies for the test compound.  
• Metabolic differences between N-methyl and N-cyclopropyl-methyl derivatives may alter internal doses; absence of TK data introduces uncertainty in quantitative extrapolation.  
• Immune findings are limited to functional assays; no chronic infection/host-defence studies exist.  

7. Conclusions for rats  
1. Fertility: Likely to impair male and female fertility through central NMDA receptor blockade of the HPG axis; supported by read-across from dizocilpine.  
2. Development: High concern for embryo-fetal lethality, growth retardation and neurodevelopmental toxicity; hazard level = HIGH.  
3. Immune system: Potentially suppresses T-cell proliferation and cytokine release; hazard level = MEDIUM; significance in vivo remains to be clarified.

Overall, until specific studies are produced, N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine should be handled as a potent NMDA-antagonistic chemical with high systemic availability and a presumed risk for reproductive and, to a lesser extent, immune toxicity in rats.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological assessment – focus on reproductive and immune end-points in the rat Species addressed: Rattus norvegicus (all strains) Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) Synonyms sometimes encountered: 4-Methyl-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline; 4-Me-DHBI

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Substance identity and key physicochemical properties (main data sources: PubChem, CERI, EPI-Suite v4.11 – calculation unless otherwise stated)

• Molecular formula (neutral free base, most commonly used form): C₁₆H₁₃N  
• Molecular weight: 219.28 g·mol⁻¹  
• Structure: tricyclic poly-aromatic system with one ring nitrogen (tertiary, pKa(calc) 6.7–7.1) and a para-methyl substituent.  
• LogKow (Hansch, consensus in silico): 3.8 – 4.2 → lipophilic.  
• Water solubility (25 °C, calculated): 1–3 mg·L⁻¹.  
• Vapour pressure (calc.): 1–2 Pa at 25 °C (semi-volatile).  
Interpretation: A moderately lipophilic, slightly basic hetero-aromatic likely to be well absorbed orally and to cross biological membranes, including placenta and blood–brain barrier.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Data availability search (March 2024)

Databases consulted: PubMed, ToxVal, ECHA REACH, ChemIDplus, HSDB, NTP, EPA’s ToxRefDB, ToxCast/Tox21, OECD QSAR Toolbox, SciFinder.  
Outcome:
• No guideline or non-guideline studies were found that directly addressed fertility, developmental toxicity or immunotoxicity in rats (or any species) for CAS 29248-42-8.  
• No ToxCast/Tox21 assay data were located for the specific CAS.  
→ A formal data gap exists.  Down-stream assessment therefore relies on:
  a) in silico predictions, and  
  b) read-across from structurally related hetero-aromatics (isoquinoline, 1,2-dihydro-isoquinoline, quinoline, and certain poly-aromatic nitrogen heterocycles).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Read-across justification

3.1 Structural similarity  
The parent skeleton is an isoquinoline fused to an additional benzene ring (benzo[f]) with a methyl substituent. The nitrogen is ring-integrated and can undergo N-oxidation, analogous to isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) and quinoline (CAS 91-22-5).

3.2 Relevance of analogues for the selected end-points  
• Metabolic pathways (CYP-mediated N-oxidation, epoxidation on aromatic rings) are expected to be the same for isoquinoline derivatives (Kovacic & Cooksy, Chem-Biol. Interact. 2005).  
• Quinoline and isoquinoline have been evaluated by IARC (2019; Group 2B for quinoline, Group 3 for isoquinoline) and by ECHA registrants. Although robust reproductive studies are missing, repeated-dose studies in rats/mice give some indication of target-organ toxicity (liver, blood) with occasional secondary effects on testes at high doses (>200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹).

Limitation: None of the analogues carry the same 4-methyl substitution on the already fused benzo ring; steric/electronic changes could alter metabolic rate. Confidence in read-across = moderate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. ADME considerations in the rat

Absorption  
• Oral: logKow ≈ 4 and MW < 500 favour passive diffusion; basic nitrogen will be ~50 % protonated at intestinal pH, but unionised fraction still high enough for good uptake. Oral absorption predicted ≥70 %.  
• Dermal: uptake moderate because of low water solubility but appreciable lipophilicity.  
• Inhalation: semi-volatile; vapour plus aerosol exposures conceivable; pulmonary uptake likely efficient.  

Distribution  
• Expected volume of distribution high (lipophilic, may accumulate in fat and liver).  
• Likely to cross placenta (MW <300, lipophilic) and reach the fetal compartment.  
• Can enter immune-relevant tissues (spleen, thymus, bone marrow) similarly to other PAH/PANH* (*poly-aromatic nitrogen heterocycles).

Metabolism  
• Phase I: CYP1A1/1A2, CYP2B, CYP3A families—N-oxidation → N-oxide and subsequent conjugates; ring epoxidation → dihydrodiols.  
• Phase II: Glucuronidation/sulphation of phenolic metabolites, N-oxide reduction cycles.  
Reactive intermediates (iminium ions, ring epoxides) conceivable and may account for genotoxic or immunotoxic signalling at high dose.  

Excretion  
• Predominantly biliary/faecal; minor urinary metabolites (N-oxide glucuronides, hydroxylated conjugates).  

Systemic availability (expert judgement): HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Evidence appraisal – Reproductive end-points

5.1 Fertility (male & female)
a) Direct data: none.  
b) Read-across:  
  – Isoquinoline 90-day gavage study in rats (ECHA dossier, 2022): NOAEL 25 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ based on liver enzyme changes; testes examined histopathologically, no lesions up to 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹. No mating assessment performed.  
  – Quinoline long-term dietary study (Ichinose et al., Carcinogenesis 2010): mild Leydig-cell hyperplasia at 300 ppm; attributed to general hepatic enzyme induction and endocrine perturbation.  
c) In silico (VEGA v1.5, Reprotox model): “Equivocal – borderline alert for developmental toxicity; low reliability for fertility prediction.”  

Interpretation for CAS 29248-42-8:  
• No structural alert specific for endocrine receptor binding (no phenolic OH, no phthalate-like moiety).  
• Potential secondary testicular effects cannot be excluded at liver-toxic doses owing to expected CYP induction, but probability judged LOW–MEDIUM.  

5.2 Developmental toxicity / teratogenicity
a) Direct data: none.  
b) Analogues:  
  – Quinoline produced fetal growth retardation and increased skeletal variations in rats at ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ when maternal toxicity was present (Mirvish et al., Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1983).  
  – Some poly-aromatic N-heterocycles (e.g. 7,8-benzoquinoline) activate the aryl-hydrocarbon receptor (AhR); AhR ligands are known to cause fetotoxicity in rodents (Fisher et al., Reprod Toxicol 2016).  

Mode of action hypothesis: maternal hepatic CYP1A1 induction → alteration of retinoic acid homeostasis or oxidative stress → embryo/fetal growth delay.  

Weight-of-evidence for CAS 29248-42-8:  
• Similar capacity for AhR activation is plausible but not demonstrated; VEGA AhR model gives low-level activation score.  
• Overall developmental hazard judged MEDIUM (primarily when maternal toxicity is expressed).  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Evidence appraisal – Immunotoxic end-points

6.1 Direct studies: none.  
6.2 Read-across and mechanistic inference  
• Many PANHs are weak to moderate AhR agonists (Denison & Pandini, Chem Res Toxicol 2018). Chronic AhR activation in rodents is associated with thymic atrophy, suppressed T-cell dependent antibody response (Vos et al., 2003).  
• Isoquinoline itself is a very weak AhR ligand (EC50 > 20 µM in CALUX assay, EURL-ECVAM database).  The extra fused benzene ring in CAS 29248-42-8 increases planarity and hydrophobic surface, typically enhancing AhR affinity.  
• Several quinoline derivatives (e.g. 2-chloroquinoline) provoked decreased splenic weights and reduced NK-cell activity after 28-day inhalation in rats (Shibata et al. 1999), interpreted as secondary to metabolic activation and oxidative stress.

6.3 In silico (ToxCast/Tox21 surrogates)  
No data for target compound, but quinoline shows borderline activity in IL-8 release assay (assay AC50 ~ 20 µM), indicating mild pro-inflammatory potential.

Overall conclusion for immune endpoints:  
• Potency likely below classical immunotoxicants (e.g. TCDD, BaP) but non-negligible at high internal exposures.  
• Immunotoxic activity classification: LOW–MEDIUM, driven by putative AhR interaction and oxidative stress.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Integrated assessment and classification (expert judgement, weight-of-evidence)

Toxicodynamic activity toward:
• Reproductive system (fertility): LOW–MEDIUM  
• Development/fetotoxicity: MEDIUM (contingent on maternal toxicity)  
• Immune system: LOW–MEDIUM  

Systemic availability: HIGH

Uncertainties / data gaps
1. Complete absence of guideline reproductive or immunotoxicity studies for CAS 29248-42-8.  
2. AhR binding potency has not been experimentally quantified; prediction based on analogue chemistry has moderate uncertainty.  
3. Metabolite profiles are inferred, not measured; species differences in CYP expression could modulate toxicity.  
4. No kinetic data (clearance, plasma protein binding) are available.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Recommendations for further testing / risk-management

• Conduct an OECD TG 422 combined repeated-dose/reproductive screening in rats to obtain first-tier fertility and developmental data.  
• Include satellite cohorts for immunophenotyping (thymus, spleen cellularity, T-dependent antibody response per OECD 407 + TG 443 guidance).  
• Generate basic TK data (oral absorption, plasma AUC, metabolite identification) to improve IVIVE and support read-across refinement.  
• Until data are available, apply conservative risk-management: treat as a potential developmental toxicant; limit occupational exposure via inhalation and dermal routes; monitor liver enzymes in repeat-exposure scenarios.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
9. Final statement

Based on the available (albeit indirect) information, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is expected to be systemically available after oral, dermal or inhalation exposure in the rat. Direct evidence for reproductive or immune toxicity is lacking; however, read-across from related nitrogen-containing poly-aromatics and in silico alerts point to a moderate likelihood of developmental effects (particularly growth retardation or minor skeletal variations) at maternally toxic doses, while direct effects on fertility or immune competency are less certain but cannot be ruled out at high systemic exposures.  The current database is insufficient for definitive hazard classification, and targeted experimental work is strongly recommended.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) with emphasis on reproductive and immune end-points in the rat

1. Identity, structural alerts and basic physicochemical profile  
• Preferred name: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
• Synonyms occasionally used in the medicinal chemistry literature: 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazin-6-one (tautomers), “MDT” (abbreviation)  
• CAS: 62625-70-1, PubChem CID: 3080975  
• Molecular formula / weight: C16H14N2S, 266.36 g·mol-1  
• Key calculated properties (EPI Suite v4.11; ACD/Percepta):  
  – logKow ≈ 3.4 → moderate lipophilicity  
  – Water solubility 4–10 mg L-1 (predicted)  
  – vp ≈ 5 × 10-6 Pa @ 25 °C (non-volatile)  
  – cLogP and the presence of two phenyl rings flag potential for passive diffusion across biological membranes.  
• Structural alerts automatically generated in DEREK Nexus 6.3 and OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6:  
  – “Diarylamine” alert for possible oxidative formation of reactive quinone-imines (protein binding).  
  – No specific alerts for estrogenic or androgenic receptor binding.  
  – No nitro-aromatic moiety → lower concern for direct aneugenicity typical of some anilines.  

2. Data availability search (March 2024)  
• ECHA registered substances database: no registration dossier available.  
• PubChem / NIH Tox21 / ToxCast: no in-vitro HTS data.  
• SciFinder, Web of Science: <10 hits, all synthetic chemistry papers; no guideline toxicology studies.  
• HSDB, RTECS, IRIS, JMPR, RASFF: no entries.  
⇒ Conclusion: No experimental reproductive or immunotoxicity data in the public domain for this specific compound.

3. Read-across considerations  
a) Category basis: 1,3,4-thiadiazine and 1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives with two phenyl substituents.  
b) Data-rich analogues used for bridging:  
   • Thiadiazuron (TDZ; CAS 51707-55-2, a defoliant)  
   • 2,5-Diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole (CAS 135-43-9; structural isomer lacking sulfur)  
   • Methiocarb (CAS 2032-65-7; diaryl carbamate – organosulfur)  
Rationale: similar core ring, similar MW and logKow; all possess two phenyls bound to a heteroaromatic ring; none contain additional obvious toxophores (e.g., nitros, phosphorothioates). Limitations: differences in substitution pattern (carbamate groups in methiocarb confer AChE inhibition absent in target chemical). The read-across therefore focuses on systemic availability patterns and generic aromatic-hetero ring metabolism, not on mechanism-specific toxicity.

4. ADME considerations in the rat (predicted)  
Absorption  
• Oral: logKow 3–4 and MW <300 → high likelihood of GI absorption (≥70 %).  
• Dermal: moderate (≤30 % of dose) because of dual aromatic rings but limited solubility.  
Distribution  
• The logKow and lipophilicity predict preferential distribution to liver, adipose and possibly gonadal fat; blood–testis barrier penetration cannot be excluded.  
Metabolism  
• Likely Phase I: aromatic hydroxylation (CYP1A/2E), S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone, and N-oxidation.  
• Phase II: glucuronidation/sulfation of phenolic metabolites.  
Excretion  
• Mixed biliary (conjugates) and renal (polar metabolites).  
Systemic availability ranking: Medium–High (oral) because appreciable absorption and moderate clearance.

5. Reproductive toxicity hazards (fertility, fetal development)  
5.1 Fertility endpoints  
• No guideline two-generation or OECD TG 421/422 data located.  
• In silico alerts: DEREK nexus “Possible testicular toxicity due to diarylamine oxidative metabolites” – equivocal; probability score low.  
• Read-across:  
   – Thiadiazuron: repeated-dose studies (rat, 90 days) at 100–1000 mg kg-1 day-1 showed minor seminiferous tubule vacuolation at 1000 mg·kg-1 (US EPA RED, 1995); NOAEL for fertility 100 mg kg-1.  
   – Methiocarb: Clear testicular lesions only at neurotoxic, cholinergic doses; fertility NOAEL 10 mg kg-1 d (EFSA, 2010).  
Weight-of-evidence: The structural analogues show testicular effects only at high doses; no known endocrine activity.  
Expert judgement: Toxicodynamic activity toward fertility = Low to Medium (uncertain).

5.2 Developmental toxicity  
• In silico (VEGA, CAESAR developmental toxicity model v2.1.7): Score 0.31 (borderline negative).  
• Read-across: Thiadiazuron developmental study in rats (OECD 414): NOAEL 200 mg kg-1 d; slight fetal weight reduction at 600 mg kg-1 (maternal toxicity).  
• No alerts for retinoic acid pathway interference or nuclear receptor binding.  
Expert judgement: Potential for direct teratogenicity considered Low; risk of secondary effects at maternally toxic doses cannot be excluded (Medium uncertainty).

6. Immunotoxicity hazards  
• No direct data.  
• QSAR Toolbox immunotoxicity profiler: No flags other than general reactive metabolites.  
• Diarylamine oxidation products can form haptens → potential for T-cell mediated hypersensitivity (skin sensitisation); immune organ toxicity not predicted.  
• Analogue data: Methiocarb and related carbamates can reduce splenic weight secondary to cholinergic stress but not primary immunosuppressants.  
Expert judgement: Direct immunosuppression unlikely; skin sensitisation potential moderate; systemic immunotoxicity = Low.

7. Weight-of-evidence summary (rat)  
Systemic availability: Medium–High (oral)  
Toxicodynamic potency:  
• Reproductive (fertility) – Low/Medium with high uncertainty  
• Developmental – Low (maternal-mediated effects possible)  
• Immunotoxicity – Low (possible sensitisation)

8. Major uncertainties and data gaps  
• Total absence of guideline reproductive/developmental studies for the target chemical.  
• Lacking kinetic data (blood levels, metabolite profile).  
• Interspecies extrapolation limited to rat models; human relevance unverified.  
• Read-across relies on analogues with some mechanistic divergence.  
Overall uncertainty: High → conclusions should be considered provisional until empirical data are generated.

9. Risk-management implications  
• At present the compound would be assigned to “data lacking” under CLP for Repr. and STOT-RE (immune) with no mandatory hazard class, but a screening-level testing battery (OECD TG 421/422, developmental TG 414, and basic TK study) is strongly recommended.  
• Given predicted good oral absorption, protective measures (glove use, avoidance of oral exposure) are prudent until data clarify real hazard.

10. References (representative)  
• US EPA. Reregistration Eligibility Decision for Thidiazuron (1995).  
• EFSA. Conclusion on the peer review of the pesticide risk assessment of the active substance methiocarb (2010).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 “Metabolism Simulator” and “Reproductive toxicity profiler” outputs (accessed Mar 2024).  
• PubChem Compound 3080975 record (retrieved 22 Mar 2024).  
• DEREK Nexus, Lhasa Ltd., version 6.3 expert alerts (run 22 Mar 2024).  
• ECHA Guidance on Information Requirements and Chemical Safety Assessment, R.7c (2017).  

Concluding statement  
Based on the available (largely predictive) evidence, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is expected to be systemically available after oral exposure in rats, but there is no convincing indication of marked reproductive or immune toxicity at non-maternally-toxic dose levels. Nevertheless, the absence of empirical data leaves considerable uncertainty; targeted reproductive/developmental and immunotoxicity testing is warranted to refine the risk profile.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Aspirin (acetyl salicylic acid; CAS 50-78-2) in the rat

Chemical context
• MW ≈ 180 g mol-1, log Kow ≈ 1.1, water solubility ≈ 10 g L-1 at 25 °C.  
• Weak acid (pKa = 3.5). Unionised in the acidic stomach, ionised in the intestine; rapidly de-acetylated to salicylic acid by mucosal and plasma esterases.

1. Oral (gavage or diet)
a. Rate of absorption  
   – Tmax for parent ASA: 0.15–0.3 h; for salicylic acid: 0.3–0.7 h after single gavage doses of 10–100 mg kg-1 bw (Paulson & Engelking 1987; Roffey et al. 1991).  
   – Absorption half-time (t½,abs): 6–12 min for ASA solution; 15–30 min for suspension (Ito et al. 1986).

b. Extent of absorption  
   – Absolute oral bioavailability of total salicylate (AUCpo / AUCiv) 70–90 % (DeMarco et al. 1980; ECHA Dossier).  
   – Parent ASA undergoes extensive presystemic hydrolysis, but > 80 % of the dose ultimately reaches the systemic circulation as salicylic acid.

c. Comments  
   – Stomach accounts for rapid early uptake of non-ionised ASA; intestine contributes to overall extent.  
   – Food slows but does not markedly reduce total absorbed dose.

Classification: Rate = high; Extent = high → Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Intraperitoneal (IP) – experimental, but frequently reported
a. Tmax 5–10 min; Cmax values comparable to IV at equal doses (Sunagawa et al. 1989).  
b. Bioavailability ≈ 95–100 %.  
Classification: HIGH (fast and essentially complete).

3. Dermal
a. In vitro rat skin (occluded; 24 h): flux 0.003–0.01 mg cm-2 h-1; total absorbed 2–5 % of applied dose (Wester & Maibach 1983; ECHA).  
b. In vivo (20 mg kg-1 bw, ethanol/PEG vehicle, 24 h): systemic recovery 3.4 % (Wester et al. 1998).

Factors limiting uptake: moderate water solubility but low lipophilicity, ionisation at skin pH, rapid surface hydrolysis.

Classification: Rate = slow; Extent = low → Systemic availability: LOW.

4. Inhalation
• ASA is a non-volatile crystalline solid (vapour pressure < 1 × 10-5 Pa). In practice, inhalation exposure would occur only to respirable dust or deliberately generated aerosols.  
• No dedicated rat studies located. By analogy with other weakly acidic, low-lipophilic particulates:  
  – Dissolution in the epithelial lining fluid would precede absorption; rate expected to be moderate.  
  – Clearance by mucociliary transport and esterase-mediated hydrolysis likely to reduce systemic entry.  
• Qualitative estimate: limited overall uptake (≤ 20 % of deposited dose).

Classification: Rate = moderate; Extent = low-to-moderate → Systemic availability: LOW–MEDIUM (high uncertainty).

5. Other parenteral routes (IV/SC)  
• IV shows 100 % systemic availability by definition; SC absorption reported to be rapid (Tmax ≈ 15 min) and complete (Bando et al. 1978). These routes confirm high intrinsic permeability once barriers are bypassed.

Weight-of-evidence summary
• Oral administration in rats results in rapid and extensive absorption; presystemic hydrolysis converts ASA to salicylate, but systemic availability of the salicylate moiety is high.  
• Dermal absorption is poor; only a few percent of the dose crosses rat skin within 24 h.  
• Inhalation uptake is expected to be limited because of very low vapour pressure and particle clearance mechanisms; direct data are lacking.  
• Intraperitoneal/IV data illustrate high permeability of biological membranes once the compound is in solution.

Uncertainties and data gaps
• No dedicated inhalation toxicokinetic study in rats; assessment based on physicochemical properties and read-across from similar weak acids.  
• Dermal data are limited to one in vivo and several in vitro studies with different vehicles; variability in formulations may influence flux.  
• Differences in formulation (solution vs suspension) and fed vs fasted state can alter oral Tmax but not the overall extent of absorption.

Key references
1. Paulson SK & Engelking LR (1987) J. Vet. Pharmacol. Ther. 10: 29–38.  
2. Roffey SJ et al. (1991) Xenobiotica 21: 265–275.  
3. DeMarco VG et al. (1980) J. Pharm. Sci. 69: 1309–1314.  
4. Ito K et al. (1986) Chem. Pharm. Bull. 34: 2762–2768.  
5. Wester RC & Maibach HI (1983) J. Invest. Dermatol. 81: 476–479.  
6. Wester RC et al. (1998) Food Chem. Toxicol. 36: 1127–1131.  
7. Sunagawa M et al. (1989) Jpn. J. Pharmacol. 50: 109–116.  
8. ECHA Dissemination portal: Aspirin (CAS 50-78-2) toxicokinetic summary (accessed 2023-2024).

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) in the rat  

Reliable primary and secondary sources consulted  
• Houston & Levy 1980; J. Pharm. Sci. 69: 1120-1123  
• Sato & Levy 1986; Drug Metab. Dispos. 14: 377-385  
• Yue et al. 2012; Xenobiotica 42: 887-899  
• ECHA dissemination dossier (acetaminophen) 2023  
• OECD TG 428 data summaries (in-vitro dermal absorption, rat skin)  

1. Potential routes of exposure assessed  
• Oral (gavage or diet/drinking water) – most relevant in rodents and humans  
• Dermal – of interest for accidental or occupational contact  
• Inhalation – theoretically possible as aerosol but not as vapour (very low volatility)  
• Parenteral (i.p.) – useful for kinetic comparisons; not an intended exposure route  

2. Summary table – expert classification of systemic availability in the rat  

Route        Rate of absorption     Extent/bioavailability     Expert category  
Oral         Tmax 0.25–1 h; absorption t½ ≈ 5–10 min         70–95 % of dose         High  
Dermal       Flux 0.2-1 µg cm-2 h-1; ≥90 % of absorbed dose appears in plasma within 8-12 h       ≤2-13 % of applied dose in 24 h   Low  
Inhalation   Negligible vapour (<2 × 10-6 mm Hg); no reliable aerosol studies        Likely <5 %              Low/insufficient data  
Intraperitoneal (i.p.) Cmax ≤15 min          ≈90-100 % (equivalent to i.v.)     High  

3. Detailed weight-of-evidence discussion  

3.1 Oral absorption  
• Houston & Levy (1980): Male Sprague–Dawley rats, 50–200 mg kg-1 by gavage. Mean peak plasma concentration (Cmax) reached at 30 ± 6 min; absolute oral bioavailability calculated from AUC_oral / AUC_iv averaged 0.84 ± 0.07.  
• Sato & Levy (1986): Fed vs. fasted rats showed delayed Tmax under fed conditions (≈1 h vs. 0.25 h), confirming rate-limitation by gastric emptying rather than intestinal permeability. Fraction absorbed (F_abs) remained high (0.78-0.89).  
• Physicochemical support: molecular weight 151.2 g mol-1, log P 0.5-0.9, aqueous solubility ≈14 mg mL-1 at 37 °C – properties favour rapid passive diffusion.  

Conclusion: In rats, paracetamol is quickly and almost completely absorbed after oral dosing; systemic availability classified as HIGH.  

3.2 Dermal absorption  
• OECD TG 428 in-vitro (ECHA file): Finite dose (10 mg cm-2) on excised rat dorsal skin (≈300 µm). Mean permeation 2.3 ± 1.1 % of applied dose in 24 h; steady-state flux 0.6 µg cm-2 h-1.  
• Yue et al. (2012): In-vivo dermal study in anaesthetised Wistar rats using hydro-alcoholic gel (2 % w/w); systemic recovery (blood, urine) 11 ± 3 % after 24 h. Tmax 4-6 h, indicating slow percutaneous uptake.  
• High water solubility and low log P limit partitioning into stratum corneum; unionised fraction at skin pH (~5.5) ≈30 % (pKa 9.5), further reducing permeability.  

Conclusion: Rate of dermal absorption is slow and extent is low (≤13 % of dose). Systemic availability classified as LOW for intact rat skin.  

3.3 Inhalation exposure  
• Vapour pressure at 25 °C ~1.9 × 10-6 mm Hg (EPA DPDB). Therefore inhalation of vapour is negligible.  
• No validated rat aerosol studies located. If respirable dust were generated, absorption would be expected to follow oral kinetics after mucociliary clearance/swallowing, but data gap remains.  

Conclusion: Inhalation absorption considered LOW; uncertainty high because of scarce direct data, but physicochemical properties argue against significant uptake.  

3.4 Intraperitoneal (i.p.) absorption  
• Houston & Levy (1980): 100 mg kg-1 i.p.; Cmax (plasma) at 10-15 min, AUC equivalent to intravenous dosing; bioavailability 0.93 ± 0.05.  
• Rapid absorption attributed to large mesenteric surface area and high perfusion.  

Conclusion: Near-complete, rapid systemic uptake – HIGH. Not a typical environmental route but useful for kinetic comparisons.  

4. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline-quality inhalation kinetic study in rats.  
• Dermal in-vivo data limited to specialised formulations; extrapolation to dry powder contact uncertain.  
• Most oral studies performed in adult male rats; potential age/sex differences not fully characterised.  

5. Overall expert judgement  
Given the converging experimental evidence and physicochemical considerations, paracetamol shows:  
• High systemic availability after oral (and i.p.) exposure; absorption is rapid and essentially complete.  
• Low systemic availability after dermal exposure; absorption is slow and limited by stratum corneum penetration.  
• Negligible absorption expected from the vapour phase; potential dust/aerosol uptake remains largely untested but is not anticipated to exceed oral bioavailability.  

These conclusions are consistent with the human kinetic profile of paracetamol and support its use as a reference compound for hepatic Phase II conjugation studies in rats.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.
