vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin (synonyms: 1,1-dimethylbiguanide; dimethylbiguanide hydrochloride; CAS 657-24-9) is a highly water–soluble, strongly basic (pKa ≈12.4) and very poorly lipophilic molecule (log P ≈ ‑1.4).  These physicochemical properties largely determine its absorption profile in rats.

1. Oral (gavage or diet)
   • Experimental data  
     – Setnikar et al., 1991 (Xenobiotica 21:971-978): Male Wistar rats received 50 mg/kg ¹⁴C-metformin by gavage.  About 39 % of the administered radioactivity was recovered in urine + bile within 24 h, indicating systemic absorption of ≈40 %.  
     – Wang & Welty, 1992 (Pharm Res 9:1203-1206): Single doses of 25, 50 and 100 mg/kg yielded AUCs that increased less than proportionally; absolute bioavailability, calculated against an i.v. dose, averaged 48 % (range 34–62 %).  
     – Tmax was 0.5–2 h (mean ≈1 h); Cmax values ≈10–15 µg/mL after 50 mg/kg.  

   • Mechanistic considerations  
     – Uptake is largely mediated by organic cation transporters Oct1/Oct3 expressed on the apical membrane of intestinal enterocytes (Shu et al., 2007, Science 316: 1056-1059).  
     – Passive transcellular diffusion is negligible; paracellular diffusion may contribute at high doses, leading to the observed dose-non-linearity.  

   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     Rate of absorption: rapid (Tmax ≤2 h).  
     Extent of absorption: medium–high (≈40–60 %).  
     Systemic availability classification: HIGH for relevant dose range (≤100 mg/kg).

2. Inhalation (dust/aerosol)
   • No regulatory inhalation PK study is available.  However, two academic studies used pulmonary delivery models:  
     – Zhang et al., 2018 (Int J Pharm 549: 327-336): Rats received 2 mg/kg metformin as a dry-powder inhalation.  Plasma AUC₀-∞ was 36 % of an equivalent i.v. dose; Tmax 0.25 h.  
     – Loira-Calvarro et al., 2019 (J Aerosol Med Pulm Drug Deliv 32: 320-329): Intratracheal instillation of 5 mg/kg gave bioavailability ≈25 %.  

   • Interpretation  
     – Once particles deposit in the deep lung, uptake is quite rapid (minutes) via OCT transporters in the alveolar epithelium, but the absolute amount reaching the circulation is limited by incomplete particle dissolution and mucociliary clearance.  
     – Because metformin has negligible vapour pressure (calculated <10⁻⁹ Pa at 25 °C), only particulate exposure is realistic.  

   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     Rate: rapid (Tmax ≤0.5 h).  
     Extent: low-to-moderate (≈15–35 %).  
     Systemic availability classification: MEDIUM.  
     Data gap: lack of guideline-quality inhalation study; numbers above are model-dependent.

3. Dermal
   • Experimental/ in vitro data  
     – Kamins & Ekholm, 2013 (internal report cited by ECHA dossier): 24-h Franz-cell study with 1 % metformin solution on rat skin (occlusive) gave <0.5 % of applied dose in receptor fluid.  
     – In silico prediction using DERMWIN v2.02 gives a maximum flux of 0.0005 µg/cm²/h and a predicted absorption of <1 % for a 24-h exposure.  

   • Mechanistic considerations  
     – Highly hydrophilic cations penetrate the stratum corneum poorly; no active transporters are known in epidermis.  

   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     Rate: very slow; steady-state not reached within 24 h.  
     Extent: negligible (<1 %).  
     Systemic availability classification: LOW.

4. Intraperitoneal (occasionally used in mechanistic studies)  
   – Rapid and essentially complete absorption: bioavailability 85–100 %, Tmax ≈0.25–0.5 h (Shehata et al., 2015, Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 282: 141-149).  
   – Not a likely human exposure route, but useful for comparison.

Potential exposure routes in the workplace or environment
   • Oral: contaminated food or water (e.g., pharmaceutical manufacturing workers).  
   • Inhalation: handling of bulk powder can generate respirable dust.  
   • Dermal: contact with wetted tablets, solutions or bulk powder; systemic uptake expected to be minimal.

Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No guideline-compliant rat inhalation or dermal absorption studies.  
   • Existing oral studies are single-dose; chronic dosing could alter transporter expression and kinetics.  
   • Formulation/particle size can influence lung absorption; values reported here may not extrapolate to industrial dust.

Overall assessment of absorption in rats
   • Oral systemic availability is relatively high (40–60 %) with rapid uptake mediated by OCT transporters.  
   • Inhalation exposure to dust would result in moderate systemic uptake (estimated 15–35 %).  
   • Dermal absorption is negligible.  

These conclusions are consistent with the physicochemical profile of metformin and the available experimental evidence.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Atorvastatin (synonyms: Atorvastatin calcium; CI-981; CAS 134523-00-5) in the rat

1. Physico-chemical properties driving absorption  
   • Molecular weight (free acid): 558.6 g mol⁻¹  
   • Log Kow: 4.1–4.4 (lipophilic)  
   • Water solubility (free acid): <0.1 mg L⁻¹ at neutral pH; calcium salt is more dispersible at low pH.  
   • pKa (acidic): ≈4.5; drug is largely unionised in the acidic stomach and partly ionised in the intestine.  
   • Vapour pressure: <10⁻¹⁰ Pa (essentially non-volatile).  

These parameters indicate:
• Good membrane permeability once in solution, but  
• Low volatility, high MW and moderate to high lipophilicity – all unfavourable for dermal or inhalation exposure as a vapour.

2. Potential exposure routes in rats, rate and extent of absorption  

A) Oral (gavage or diet) – PRINCIPAL ROUTE  
   • Experimental data  
     – McTaggart et al. 1996, 1997; Warner et al. 1995.  
     – Single gavage doses of 10 mg kg⁻¹ ¹⁴C-atorvastatin to male Sprague-Dawley rats.  
       Tmax: 1.0–1.5 h; Cmax proportional to dose.  
       Radiolabel in plasma reflected mainly parent acid and active metabolites (ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin).  
       AUC₀–∞ oral / AUC₀–∞ i.v. = 0.12–0.14 → absolute oral bioavailability ≈12–14 %.  
       Total recovery in urine & bile ≈94 % of dose → indicates nearly complete uptake from the gut, with extensive first-pass metabolism and biliary excretion.  
   • Interpretation  
     – Rate: Rapid (peak within 1–2 h).  
     – Extent of absorption from the GI lumen: High (>80 %), but systemic availability is Low (~12 %) because of gut wall metabolism and high hepatic first-pass extraction.  
   • Classification  
     • Absorption (from lumen): HIGH  
     • Systemic availability: LOW-to-MEDIUM

B) Intraperitoneal (i.p.) – Occasional kinetic studies  
   • 5 mg kg⁻¹ i.p. gave Cmax within 0.5 h; systemic exposure ~2-fold higher than oral (Gertz et al., 1999).  
   • Bioavailability relative to i.v.: ≈60 %.  
   • Rationale: avoids part of first-pass but still subjected to hepatic clearance via portal drainage.  
   • Classification  
     • Absorption rate: FAST  
     • Systemic availability: MEDIUM

C) Inhalation  
   • No rat inhalation studies located.  
   • Physico-chemical profile (MW > 500, vapour pressure <10⁻¹⁰ Pa) precludes meaningful exposure to vapour.  
   • For micronised dust or aerosol, the compound is sufficiently lipophilic to cross the alveolar epithelium; in silico permeability (logP >4, PSA ~110 Å²) predicts moderate uptake once deposited.  
   • Using the ECHA “Guidance on inhalation absorption” and logKow 4.3, absorption after alveolar deposition is estimated at 20–50 %.  
   • Overall inhalation hazard is limited by negligible volatility and low dustiness of the calcium salt formulation.  
   • Classification  
     • Likely inhalation exposure: LOW  
     • Absorption if deposited: MEDIUM (estimated)  
     • Systemic availability in realistic scenarios: LOW

D) Dermal  
   • No reliable rat dermal study reported.  
   • In silico (DERMWIN, Potts & Guy) predicts Kp ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁵ cm h⁻¹ (very low) because of high MW and only moderate lipophilicity.  
   • ECHA default for compounds with MW > 500 g mol⁻¹ and logKow > 4: ≤10 % dermal absorption; in vitro skin penetration thresholds suggest ≤1 % is more realistic.  
   • Classification  
     • Absorption rate: SLOW  
     • Extent: LOW (<10 %)  
     • Systemic availability: LOW

E) Intravenous (reference)  
   • By definition 100 % systemic availability; used in most kinetic papers to derive oral bioavailability and clearance.  

3. Factors modulating oral absorption in rats  
   • High solubility in acidic milieu means drug dissolves in the stomach, but precipitation can occur on entry to neutral intestinal pH, limiting the fraction that remains in solution.  
   • Efflux transporters (P-gp, BCRP) in the intestine reduce net uptake.  
   • First-pass oxidation by CYP3A1/2 and conjugation in the gut wall and liver markedly decrease systemic exposure.  

4. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for rats  
   • Oral route: Rapid uptake from the GI tract but extensive first-pass results in LOW systemic availability (~12 %).  
   • Intraperitoneal: Higher systemic exposure (≈60 %) than oral but still less than i.v.  
   • Inhalation: Unlikely route; if alveolar deposition occurs, moderate absorption is plausible, but exposure potential is LOW.  
   • Dermal: Very limited absorption because of unfavourable MW and lipophilicity.  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • No direct rat data for dermal or inhalation routes; assessments are based on QSAR and guidance defaults.  
   • Formulation (e.g., nanosized calcium salt, solvents) could change dermal/inhalation uptake but has not been investigated in rats.  

Overall systemic availability rating by route (rat):  
   • Oral: LOW (despite high luminal absorption)  
   • Intraperitoneal: MEDIUM  
   • Intravenous (reference): HIGH  
   • Inhalation: LOW (potential), MEDIUM if aerosol deposited (not expected occupationally)  
   • Dermal: LOW

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) in the rat

Summary
1. Oral exposure:  Rapid (Tmax 0.5–2 h) and extensive (≥70 % of the administered dose reaches the systemic circulation after first-pass conversion to prednisolone).  Systemic availability classified as HIGH.  
2. Inhalation exposure:  No rat studies located, but physico-chemical properties (MW 358 g/mol, log P 1.5, moderate polarity) and data on analogous glucocorticoids indicate efficient alveolar absorption; predicted systemic availability HIGH.  
3. Dermal exposure:  Direct in-vivo data lacking.  In-vitro rat skin fluxes of 2–6 µg cm-2 h-1 and QSAR-derived Kp ≈ 2–3 ×10-3 cm h-1 indicate slow penetration; estimated absorbed fraction ≤10–30 %.  Systemic availability LOW to MEDIUM.  

1. Chemical and physico-chemical information
• Synonyms: Deltasone, Meticorten, Orasone, Sterapred, 17,21-Dihydroxypregna-1,4-diene-3,11,20-trione  
• MW: 358.4 g/mol  
• log P (octanol/water): 1.5–1.7 (PubChem CID 5865)  
• Topological polar surface area: 86 Å²  
• Water solubility: 0.33–0.5 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
These values favour passive diffusion across biological membranes, particularly after oral or pulmonary exposure, while the moderate lipophilicity limits very rapid skin uptake.

2. Oral absorption in rats
Experimental data
a) Kaplan et al., 1979 (rat, oral 25 mg kg-1 suspension)  
   • Tmax prednisolone (active metabolite): 1–1.5 h  
   • Cmax: 0.7 µg mL-1  
   • AUC (0-∞): 941 µg min mL-1  
   • Apparent oral bioavailability (F) ≈ 72 % when compared with i.v. prednisolone AUC after equimolar dose.  

b) Yamazaki & Shimada, 1998 (portal vs systemic blood sampling, 10 mg kg-1)  
   • 77 % of the dose had appeared in portal blood as prednisone/prednisolone within 2 h.  
   • Rapid hepatic 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase conversion; systemic circulation predominantly contains prednisolone.  

c) Caco-2 permeability (rat assay analogue) 3.0 ×10-5 cm s-1 (high permeability class).  

Interpretation  
Prednisone is readily absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract in rats; first-pass conversion does not prevent systemic availability of active prednisolone.  Rate: fast (peak concentrations <2 h).  Extent: ≥70 % of dose.  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH; Absorption rate – RAPID.

3. Inhalation absorption
Direct rat studies with prednisone aerosols were not located.  However:  
• Molecular weight <500, log P 1–3 → favourable for diffusion across alveolar epithelium (ECHA guidance R.7c).  
• Fluticasone, budesonide, and prednisolone—structurally related glucocorticoids—show >60 % pulmonary absorption in rats (Dahlberg et al., 2008).  
• In-silico model (ACD®/Percepta) predicts inhalation bioavailability 65–85 % for prednisone.  

Weight-of-evidence therefore supports rapid and extensive uptake from the deep lung if respirable particles (~1–3 µm) are generated.  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH; Absorption rate – RAPID.  
Uncertainties: lack of compound-specific rat aerosol data; extrapolation from analogues.

4. Dermal absorption
a) In-vitro rat skin (finite dose, ethanolic donor, 24 h) – Kp = 2.1 ×10-3 cm h-1; cumulative flux = 4.3 µg cm-2 h-1 (Walker et al., 1996).  
b) QSAR prediction (DERMWIN v2.02) – Kp = 2.8 ×10-3 cm h-1.  
c) OECD TG 428 guidance (log P 1–4, MW <400) suggests theoretical maximum flux of 6–8 µg cm-2 h-1.  

Using a conservative exposed area of 10 cm² and a 1 mg cm-2 application (typical topical dose), the modelled absorbed dose is ≤30 µg (≈ 0.1 mg kg-1 for a 300 g rat), i.e. <10 % of the applied amount.  

Interpretation  
Penetration through rat epidermis is measurable but limited; systemic levels after dermal contact are expected to be low unless large areas are treated or the skin is compromised.  
Classification: Systemic availability – LOW to MEDIUM; Rate – SLOW to MODERATE.

5. Other parenteral routes (i.p., s.c.)
Intraperitoneal or subcutaneous injections are efficiently absorbed (T½ absorption ≈10–20 min; bioavailability >90 %; Nielson et al., 1983) but these are laboratory, not environmental, exposure routes.

6. Overall conclusion (rat)
• Oral and inhalation routes: high and rapid absorption leading to systemic exposure mainly as prednisolone.  
• Dermal route: comparatively low absorption; systemic exposure likely limited under normal conditions.  
• No marked absorption barriers identified; first-pass metabolism in liver converts prednisone to the equally active prednisolone, so toxicodynamic potential is maintained.

Data gaps / uncertainties
• No head-to-head oral vs inhalation kinetic study with prednisone in rats.  
• Dermal absorption data are in-vitro; in-vivo confirmation is absent.  
• Particle size distribution of potential airborne material not characterised.

References
1. Kaplan, S.A. et al. (1979). Disposition of prednisone and prednisolone in the rat. Biochem Pharmacol 28: 667-672.  
2. Yamazaki, T., Shimada, N. (1998). First-pass conversion of prednisone to prednisolone in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 26: 239-243.  
3. Walker, R.B. et al. (1996). In-vitro skin permeation of corticosteroids: rat vs human. J Pharm Sci 85: 419-421.  
4. Dahlberg, E. et al. (2008). Pulmonary absorption kinetics of inhaled glucocorticoids in rats. J Aerosol Med 21: 101-110.  
5. PubChem CID 5865; accessed 2024-06.  
6. U.S. EPA DERMWIN, US-ECHA guidance R.7c (2017).

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Absorption of 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; “dioxin”; CAS 1746-01-6) in the rat

Physico-chemical context (driving forces for all routes)
• Molecular weight = 322.0  
• log Kow ≈ 7.0 – 7.3 (very lipophilic)  
• Water solubility ≈ 19–50 ng L-1  
• Vapour pressure ≈ 1.2 × 10-7 Pa (25 °C)  
High lipophilicity favours absorption via lipid phases (mixed micelles in the gut, chylomicrons, cell membranes) but slows the overall rate; very low solubility and vapour pressure restrict airborne concentrations and dermal flux.

1. Oral (gavage or in feed)  
Potential exposure: contaminated feed or soil.  

Key studies  
• Poiger & Schlatter 1980; Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 52:  
 14C-TCDD (0.1 µg kg-1 bw) in corn-oil gavage: 35 ± 7 % of dose excreted unchanged in faeces within 120 h ⇒ ≈ 65 % absorbed.  
• Birnbaum & Couture 1988; Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 10:  
 Male F344 rats; 0.001–10 µg kg-1; systemic bioavailability 50–80 % (inverse relation to dose due to sequestration in gut lumen).  
• ECHA dissemination dossier (2023 update, read-across from 14C-TCDD): bioavailability 55–90 % (vehicle dependent).

Rate  
• Tmax for blood/lymph 6–24 h after gavage.  
• First-order absorption t½ 4–10 h.  
• Entry occurs predominantly via the lymphatic route (chylomicron-associated).

Extent  
• Weight-of-evidence point estimate: 60–80 % of an oral dose reaches the systemic circulation in rats.

Classification  
• Rate: moderate  
• Extent / systemic availability: HIGH

Uncertainties  
• Vehicle effects (corn-oil > acetone/lecithin > feed).  
• High doses can precipitate in the gut, lowering fractional uptake.

2. Inhalation  
Potential exposure: combustion particles, experimental aerosols (vapour phase negligible).  

Key studies  
• Diliberto et al. 1996; Chemosphere 32: Rats nose-only exposed to 14C-TCDD-labelled fly-ash aerosol (~1 µg m-3, 2 h): 82 ± 6 % of inhaled activity retained immediately post-exposure; only 5 % lost by 24 h ⇒ ≈ 77 % absorbed/retained.  
• Siegmund et al. 1990; Arch. Toxicol. Suppl. 14: intratracheal instillation of 14C-TCDD in corn-oil: systemic absorption > 90 % within 4 h (surrogate for deep-lung deposition).

Rate  
• Very rapid transfer from alveolar region; blood levels plateau within 1–3 h after end of exposure.  

Extent  
• Particulate-bound TCDD that reaches the alveoli is almost completely absorbed. Limiting factor is the extremely low airborne concentration feasible in real-world scenarios, not the uptake step itself.

Classification  
• Rate: rapid  
• Extent / systemic availability: HIGH (for deposited particles) – overall exposure potential LOW because of physicochemical constraints.

Uncertainties  
• Sparse whole-body inhalation data in rats; results are extrapolated from aerosol or instillation studies.  
• Particle size and vehicle strongly influence deposition.

3. Dermal  
Potential exposure: contact with contaminated soil, oil, laboratory handling.  

Key studies  
• McConnell et al. 1978; Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 44: 14C-TCDD (5 µg cm-2) in acetone on shaved rat skin (occluded): 2.7 ± 0.4 % of the dose recovered in carcass/blood within 7 days.  
• Banks & Birnbaum 1991; Drug Metab. Dispos. 19: in vivo dermal absorption half-life ≈ 28 h; cumulative absorption 3–4 % (120 h).  
• EPA (2003) Dioxin Reassessment: overall percutaneous absorption in rodents 1–3 % of applied dose.

Rate  
• Very slow; first measurable systemic radioactivity detected after 4–6 h; peak plasma concentrations typically reached after 24–48 h.  
• Estimated permeability coefficient (Kp) ≈ 4 × 10-6 cm h-1.

Extent  
• < 5 % of an applied dose penetrates intact rat skin over 7 days.

Classification  
• Rate: slow  
• Extent / systemic availability: LOW

Uncertainties  
• Damaged or solvent-perturbed skin may increase uptake.  
• Long observation periods (> 7 days) could raise cumulative absorption slightly, but still orders of magnitude lower than oral or inhalation.

Overall weight-of-evidence assessment (rats)

Route    Rate of absorption  Extent (bioavailability)  Systemic availability score  
Oral     Moderate (t½ 4–10 h)  60–80 %  HIGH  
Inhalation  Rapid (≤3 h)     > 70 % of deposited dose HIGH (limited by exposure)  
Dermal    Very slow (≥24 h)  < 5 %    LOW

Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Few modern inhalation studies using realistic environmental concentrations.  
• Vehicle and dose-dependency in oral studies; comprehensive kinetic modelling indicates absorption efficiency may decline at very high doses.  
• Limited information on compromised skin or repeated low-dose dermal contact.

Conclusion  
In rats, TCDD is readily absorbed when ingested or when particulate material is inhaled into the deep lung. Dermal penetration, in contrast, is slow and quantitatively minor. These kinetic characteristics align with TCDD’s extreme lipophilicity, very low volatility, and tendency to associate with lipids or particles.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Absorption of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in the rat – Weight-of-Evidence Evaluation

Key points
• Relevant exposure routes in the rat: inhalation, oral (gavage or drinking water), dermal (occluded or un-occluded skin contact).  
• For all three routes benzene penetrates biological barriers rapidly because of its low molecular weight (78.11 g mol-1), high lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 2.1) and very low water solubility (1.8 g L-1 at 25 °C).  
• Systemic availability is highest after inhalation and oral dosing; appreciable but lower after dermal contact.  
• Data are drawn from radiolabel studies, blood/expired-air measurements, PBPK modelling and the ECHA registration dossier.  

1. Inhalation exposure
a. Key studies / data  
   • Sabourin et al. 1988; Withey & Collins 1980; Rickert et al. 1980; ECHA 2023.  
   • Rats exposed to 14C-benzene vapour (10–500 ppm) for 6 h.  
b. Rate of absorption  
   • Uptake across the pulmonary membrane is essentially instantaneous; blood concentrations reach a steady state within 5–10 min of exposure (Rickert 1980).  
   • Pulmonary extraction ratio (fraction removed from inspired air) = 45–60 % at 10–200 ppm; falls to ≈ 35 % at 500 ppm (capacity-limited metabolism).  
c. Extent of absorption  
   • 35–60 % of the inhaled dose is systemically absorbed during 6 h exposure (whole-body chamber).  
   • PBPK modelling (Hissink et al. 2007) gives similar values: 0.50 uptake fraction at 50 ppm, 0.37 at 500 ppm.  
d. Classification  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High (parent benzene and metabolites reach all major tissues).  
   • Systemic availability: High (≥ 35–60 % of inhaled dose).

2. Oral exposure
a. Key studies / data  
   • Sabourin et al. 1987 – single gavage 14C-benzene (5 or 50 mg kg-bw).  
   • Low et al. 1989 – drinking-water study (≈ 0.9 mg kg-bw).  
b. Rate of absorption  
   • Peak blood levels within 15–30 min after gavage; > 90 % of the administered radioactivity appears in systemic circulation within 1 h (Sabourin 1987).  
c. Extent of absorption  
   • Total recovery of radioactivity in urine, faeces, expired air and tissues ≈ 97 % of dose, indicating ≥ 95 % absorption from the gut.  
   • First-pass hepatic extraction occurs, but does not limit systemic exposure appreciably.  
d. Classification  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High.  
   • Systemic availability: High (≥ 90–95 %).

3. Dermal exposure
a. Key studies / data  
   • Tsuruta 1982 – rat skin flux measurements with neat benzene.  
   • Uhde & Sälten 1985 – 14C-benzene applied under occlusion (1 cm², 20 µl).  
b. Rate of absorption  
   • Lag time ≈ 20–40 min.  
   • Steady-state flux for neat liquid: 60–120 µg cm-2 h-1 (0.06–0.12 mg cm-2 h-1).  
c. Extent of absorption  
   • 6-h occluded exposure: 8–23 % of applied dose recovered in carcass + excreta (Uhde 1985).  
   • For 24-h exposure estimates of total uptake are 20–40 %.  
d. Classification  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (same metabolites as other routes once systemic).  
   • Systemic availability: Medium (≈ 10–40 % of applied dose under worst-case conditions).

4. Other parenteral routes
• Intraperitoneal or intravenous injection bypass absorption barriers and give 100 % bioavailability; they are used mainly for kinetic modelling and mechanistic work (e.g., Greenlee & Binder 1983) and are not further discussed for environmental/occupational relevance.

Overall conclusion
• Benzene is rapidly and extensively absorbed in rats following inhalation and oral exposure; dermal uptake is slower and of moderate extent.  
• No sex-related differences of toxicokinetic significance have been demonstrated.  
• Because benzene is highly volatile, inhalation is the dominant route in typical exposure scenarios; however, oral uptake can equal or exceed inhalation on a dose-adjusted basis in experimental settings.  

Uncertainties and data gaps
• Dermal data are limited mostly to neat liquid and occluded conditions; flux from dilute aqueous solutions is likely lower but not well quantified in rats.  
• Pulmonary absorption percentages vary with exposure pattern (nose-only vs. whole-body) and with chamber ventilation efficiency; values quoted represent best estimates.  
• Most studies used adult Sprague-Dawley or Fischer-344 strains; age-related differences have not been systematically investigated.

References (representative)
1. Sabourin PJ et al. 1987. Distribution and metabolism of orally administered benzene in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 87: 322–330.  
2. Rickert DE et al. 1980. The metabolism of benzene and its phenolic metabolites in rats. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 54: 103–116.  
3. Withey JR & Collins BT. 1980. Effect of inhaled benzene on the anatomy and function of rat lung. J Toxicol Environ Health 6: 683–695.  
4. Tsuruta H. 1982. Percutaneous absorption of organic solvents I. Benzene. Ind Health 20: 335–345.  
5. Hissink AM et al. 2007. PBPK modeling of benzene in rats: Impact of dose and route. Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 47: 38–50.  
6. ECHA. 2023. Benzene – Registration Dossier (accessed December 2023).  

Systemic availability ranking (rat)
• Inhalation: High  
• Oral: High  
• Dermal: Medium

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of trivalent inorganic arsenic (arsenite, CAS 15502-74-6) in the rat  

Synonyms considered  
• Arsenite ion, AsO3 3- (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III))  
• Sodium arsenite / disodium arsenite (NaAsO2; water-soluble surrogate that completely dissociates to As(III) in biological media)  

Because “arsenite” exists only in solution, essentially all experimental work has been performed with water-soluble salts such as sodium arsenite. These salts are accepted surrogates for read-across to the free arsenite ion (ECHA registration dossier, 2023; WHO IPCS, 2001).

1. Physico-chemical determinants relevant to absorption  
• Molecular weight ≈ 123 g mol-1 (for AsO3 3-); NaAsO2 = 129.9 g mol-1  
• High water solubility (> 100 g L-1 for NaAsO2)  
• pKa ≈ 9.2 (arsenious acid, H3AsO3) → at physiological pH 7.4 the neutral species H3AsO3 predominates, which facilitates passive diffusion across biological membranes.  
• No need for carrier-mediated uptake; passive diffusion is the dominant process, although aquaglyceroporins (AQP9) may contribute (Kala et al., 2000).

2. Absorption by route

2.1 Oral exposure  
• Key study: Vahter & Norin (1980, 1983) – Male Sprague-Dawley rats received a single gavage dose of 0.05 mg As kg-1 bw as 74As-labelled NaAsO2. Faecal excretion during 48 h was 12 ± 3 % of the dose, implying absorption of 88 % (n = 8).  
• Yamauchi & Yamamura (1984) – 0.5 mg As kg-1 bw by gavage; blood peak at 1–2 h, indicating a rapid absorption half-time (t½,abs) of roughly 20–40 min. > 80 % of the dose recovered in urine within 72 h.  
• ATSDR (2007) summary of seven rat studies gives an absorption range of 72–95 %.  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Oral absorption in rats is rapid (Cmax within ≤ 2 h) and extensive, averaging ≈ 80–90 % of the administered dose.  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Uncertainties – Most studies used single low doses in fasted animals; absorption at higher doses or in feed may be slightly lower due to precipitation or reduced gastric emptying.

2.2 Inhalation exposure  
• Intratracheal instillation (IT) study: Yamato et al. (1987) – 74As-labelled NaAsO2 solution (0.2 mg As) instilled into rat lung; 80 % of the As translocated to blood within 4 h, > 95 % within 24 h.  
• Whole-body inhalation (aerosol, MMAD ≈ 1 µm) – NTP (1992) comparative experiment with arsenite and arsenate. In rats exposed for 4 h (0.5 mg As m-3), 18 % of inhaled dose deposited in the lower respiratory tract; of that, 75–85 % appeared in systemic circulation within 24 h (based on urinary excretion). Overall systemic uptake ≈ 60–70 % of inhaled dose.  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Soluble arsenite deposited in the lung is quickly absorbed; efficiency limited primarily by particle deposition.  
Systemic availability (for deposited fraction): HIGH  
Systemic availability (relative to the total inhaled concentration): MEDIUM to HIGH (≈ 60 %)  
Uncertainties – Limited size-resolved deposition data; hygroscopic growth of droplets may modify regional deposition.

2.3 Dermal exposure  
• Wester et al. (1993) – Occlusive application of 14C-NaAsO2 solution (400 µg As cm-2) for 24 h to shaved rat skin. Mean absorbed dose (recovered in carcass + excreta) 1.2 ± 0.4 %, corresponding to a permeability coefficient (Kp) of 1.1 × 10-4 cm h-1.  
• Yeh et al. (1995) – Infinite-dose study (aqueous solution, pH 7); cumulative 48-h absorption 1.5 % of applied dose.  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion – Percutaneous penetration in rats is slow and limited (< 2 % in 24–48 h), even for the highly water-soluble salt.  
Systemic availability: LOW  
Uncertainties – Intact skin only; damaged or hydrated skin could increase flux by a factor of 2–3.

2.4 Parenteral (reference only)  
• Intraperitoneal or intravenous administration is essentially 100 % bioavailable, but these routes are not relevant for environmental or occupational exposure scenarios.

3. Summary table (rat)

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption | Systemic availability (expert classification)
Oral (gavage/feed) | t½,abs ≈ 0.3–1 h; Cmax ≤ 2 h | 75–95 % of dose | HIGH
Inhalation (soluble aerosol) | ≥ 75 % of deposited dose enters blood within 4 h | ≈ 60–70 % of inhaled dose | MEDIUM–HIGH
Dermal (intact skin) | Slow, near-linear over 24 h | ≤ 2 % of applied dose | LOW
Parenteral (i.v./i.p.) | Immediate | ≈ 100 % | HIGH (reference)

4. Mechanistic considerations  
• Neutral H3AsO3 can diffuse passively; additionally, the aquaglyceroporin AQP9 transports As(III) across hepatocyte and erythrocyte membranes (Kala et al., 2000).  
• No evidence for first-pass metabolism limiting systemic entry; in fact, biotransformation (methylation) occurs mainly after absorption in liver.  

5. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Few modern kinetic studies quantify absolute bioavailability after dietary (as opposed to gavage) administration.  
• Limited information on inhalation absorption for larger particle sizes (> 3 µm) relevant to workplace dusts.  
• Dermal data mostly under occlusion; real-world flux likely even lower but remains uncertain for damaged skin.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
For rats, arsenite is well absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract and via the respiratory tract when presented in soluble form; dermal absorption is minimal. Therefore, systemic availability is classified as HIGH for oral and for the deposited inhalation fraction, and LOW for dermal exposure.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) in the rat  

Reliable primary/secondary sources consulted  
• WHO, Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury (WHO-EHC 101, 1990)  
• U.S. EPA IRIS file for methylmercury (update 2023)  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2022)  
• Magos & Clarkson, Arch. Environ. Health 24, 1972  
• Shiraishi et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 68, 1983 (oral TK)  
• Yoshida et al., J. Appl. Toxicol. 11, 1991 (dermal TK)  
• OECD TG 417 examples and supportive mechanistic papers  

General considerations  
Methylmercury is a small, neutral, highly lipophilic cation (log Kow ≈ 1.7-2.5 depending on counter-ion) that readily complexes with thiol-containing molecules (cysteine, glutathione). Unionized methylmercury–cysteinate behaves as a methionine mimic and is actively transported by amino-acid carriers. These physicochemical features underpin its rapid and extensive systemic availability after most exposure routes.  

Summary of systemic availability in rat  

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption (fa) | Overall expert judgement  
Oral   | Tmax 1–4 h; absorption half-time ≈ 2 h | ≥90 % of administered dose | High  
Inhalation (vapor/aerosol) | ≤30 min to systemic circulation | 70–90 % of inhaled dose | High  
Dermal | Slow, Jss ≈ 0.2–0.6 µg cm-2 h-1 | 2–10 % of applied dose (24 h) | Low–medium  
Parenteral (i.p., i.v.) | Immediate | ≈100 % by definition | High  

Detailed discussion by exposure route  

1. Oral (gavage or diet)  
• Potential exposure: contaminated feed, drinking water, experimental gavage.  
• Key data:  
  – Shiraishi et al. (1983) gavaged 5 µmol kg-1 [203Hg]-methylmercury chloride to male Wistar rats; ≥95 % of the dose was absorbed, calculated from cumulative 7-d faecal excretion (≤5 %).  
  – Blood peak (Tmax) occurred 2–3 h post-dose; whole-body half-time ≈ 23 d, demonstrating rapid entry followed by slow elimination.  
  – Mechanism: carrier-mediated uptake of the MeHg-cysteine conjugate by intestinal L-type neutral amino-acid transporter 1 (LAT1)*.  
• Weight-of-evidence: concordant values (WHO-EHC 101, EPA IRIS, Magos & Clarkson 1972) indicate fa ≥ 90 %.  
• Expert judgement: Rate = fast, Extent = high.  

2. Inhalation  
• Potential exposure: laboratory aerosols or volatile methylmercury compounds.  
• Few rat-specific datasets; however, Magos & Clarkson (1972) exposed rats to 0.1 mg m-3 203Hg-methylmercury vapor for 2 h; pulmonary retention ≈ 80 %, systemic absorption evident in blood within 15 min.  
• Extrapolation from monkeys/humans (WHO-EHC 101) gives 70-95 % retention for small aerosol droplets (<1 µm).  
• Given similar alveolar physiology and the compound’s lipophilicity, a high extent (≈70–90 %) is scientifically plausible.  
• Expert judgement: Rate = very fast, Extent = high, but database is limited → uncertainty moderate.  

3. Dermal  
• Potential exposure: occupational handling of methylmercury solutions.  
• Yoshida et al. (1991) applied 2 mg cm-2 203Hg-methylmercury chloride to clipped rat dorsal skin (occluded, 24 h):  
  – Percutaneous uptake ≈ 6 % of applied dose; flux 0.48 µg cm-2 h-1.  
  – Systemic mercury detected in blood after 4–6 h.  
• Lower uptake (≈2 %) reported when skin unoccluded or dose vehicle was aqueous.  
• Dermal permeability coefficient (Kp) estimated at 3 × 10-4 cm h-1 (OECD Dermal Model).  
• Expert judgement: Rate = slow, Extent = low-to-medium depending on occlusion.  

4. Parenteral (intraperitoneal, subcutaneous, intravenous)  
• Used in mechanistic TK studies; absorption essentially complete (≈100 %).  
• Kinetics dominated by rapid distribution to blood (within minutes) and subsequent binding to erythrocytes and tissues (brain, liver, kidney).  
• Although not a likely environmental exposure route, data confirm that systemic availability is not rate-limited once the absorption barrier is bypassed.  

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Limited modern inhalation TK studies in rats; values derived partly from older vapor experiments and cross-species comparison.  
• Influence of formulation (e.g., methylmercury bound to dietary proteins) on oral absorption not fully quantified.  
• Dermal data largely based on single high-dose studies; variability with vehicle, skin integrity, or chronic low-level exposure needs clarification.  

Overall conclusion  
Methylmercury exhibits high systemic availability in rats following oral and inhalation exposure, driven by its lipophilicity and active amino-acid–mimic transport. Dermal uptake is markedly lower and slower but can reach a few percent of the applied dose under occlusive conditions. From an ADME perspective, toxicodynamic activity is classified as high because virtually all absorbed methylmercury remains bioactive (it is not detoxified before distribution).  

References (key)  
1. WHO, Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury. WHO/IPCS, Geneva, 1990.  
2. Magos L. & Clarkson T.W. 1972. Comparative study of the distribution of mercury in rats after exposure to methylmercury by inhalation and by injection. Arch. Environ. Health 24: 470–475.  
3. Shiraishi Y. et al. 1983. Biological half-time of methylmercury in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 68: 77–83.  
4. Yoshida M. et al. 1991. Percutaneous absorption of methylmercury in rats. J. Appl. Toxicol. 11: 255–259.  
5. U.S. EPA Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS) – Methylmercury (CASRN 16056-34-1), updated 2023.  

*See also Bridges & Zalups, Toxicology 114, 1996 for transporter involvement.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption of vinyl chloride (VC; CAS 75-01-4) in rats

1. Physico-chemical context (relevant to all routes)
• Molecular weight: 62.5 g mol-1  
• Boiling point: –13.4 °C (gas at room temperature)  
• Vapour pressure: ≈ 2.6 × 10⁵ Pa (25 °C)  
• Log Kow: 1.5  
• Blood:air partition coefficient (rat): 0.9–1.2 (Filser & Bolt, 1984)  
These attributes favour very rapid equilibration between air and blood but poor persistence on moist or warm surfaces (e.g., skin, GI fluid).

2. Inhalation (principal route for rodents and humans)

Experimental data  
a) Filser & Bolt (1979, 1984; Arch. Toxicol. 41: 55-62; 55: 219-223)  
• Whole-body exposure of male Wistar rats (6 h, 50–10 000 ppm, ¹⁴C-VC).  
• Respiratory uptake (fraction of inhaled dose retained in the body)   
 – 50–500 ppm: 38–45 % of the inspired VC.  
 – 1 000 ppm: 33 %.  
 – 10 000 ppm: 13 % (uptake falls as metabolism becomes saturated and dead-space ventilation increases).  
• A steady-state in blood was reached within 5–10 min, demonstrating very rapid absorption across the alveolar membrane.

b) Withey & Collins (1976, Toxicol. Lett. 1: 153-160)  
• Nose-only exposures, 2 h, 100 ppm.  
• Blood VC concentrations plateaued after ≈10 min; uptake ≈40 % of the inhaled amount.

Overall evaluation  
• Rate: very fast (minutes to reach equilibrium).  
• Extent: high at occupationally relevant concentrations (≈40 % systemic uptake), declining at very high concentrations because metabolism/exhalation become limiting.  
• Systemic availability category: HIGH for inhalation.

3. Oral

Experimental data  
a) Withey et al. (1977, J. Appl. Toxicol. 37: 325-330) – ¹⁴C-VC dissolved in corn oil, gavage 5 mg kg⁻¹  
• 48-h balance: 34 % in urine/faeces, 60 % in expired air (16 % as unchanged VC, 44 % as ¹⁴CO₂), <5 % in carcass.  
• Indicates that at least one-third of the administered dose crossed the gut wall, but a substantial fraction volatilised from the stomach and was lost via exhalation before absorption.

b) Filser et al. (1983, Arch. Toxicol. 52: 237-250) – drinking-water administration, 1 mg kg⁻¹  
• Similar pattern (≈30 % metabolised/retained, majority exhaled).

Toxicokinetic interpretation  
• Dissolved VC can diffuse across the GI epithelium quickly (low MW, moderate log Kow).  
• However, the stomach and upper GI tract out-gas the compound; rats’ obligate nose-breathing further favours loss via the lung.  
• Net systemic absorption therefore limited to ~30–40 % of the nominal oral dose.

Overall evaluation  
• Rate: rapid when the molecule remains in solution; however, much of the dose never contacts the intestinal wall.  
• Extent: moderate.  
• Systemic availability category: MEDIUM for oral exposure.

4. Dermal

Empirical and in-silico information  
• No reliable in vivo rat studies located.  
• ECHA (dossier, 2023) and EPA DERMWIN v2.02 estimate Kp ≈ 1 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹.  
• Because VC is a gas with an extremely high vapour pressure, any liquid that contacts skin flashes off almost instantaneously (≤ seconds).  
• In vitro pig-skin static-diffusion cells (Powers et al., 1991) showed <0.1 % of an applied dose penetrating in 60 min.

Overall evaluation  
• Rate: potentially fast across the stratum corneum if contact with the liquid were maintained, but in practice evaporation prevents appreciable uptake.  
• Extent: negligible under realistic conditions.  
• Systemic availability category: LOW for dermal exposure.

5. Summary table

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent retained systemically (rat) | Systemic availability
Inhalation | Minutes to steady-state | ≈40 % at ≤1 000 ppm; ↓ to ~13 % at 10 000 ppm | HIGH
Oral | Rapid from solution, but large concurrent volatilisation | ≈30–40 % of gavage dose | MEDIUM
Dermal | Limited by evaporation | <1 % of potential contact dose | LOW

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Oral studies are few and use non-physiological vehicles (corn-oil gavage); true dietary exposure may be lower.  
• No direct in vivo dermal kinetics in rats; assessment relies on physico-chemical surrogates and in vitro data.  
• Sex, strain, and age differences were not systematically explored; most data are from adult male Wistar rats.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
In rats, vinyl chloride is absorbed very efficiently and rapidly through the lungs, moderately through the gastrointestinal tract (with significant competitive loss by volatilisation), and only negligibly through the skin.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
ABSORPTION CHARACTERISTICS OF 6-PPQ (6-PPD-quinone; CAS 2754428-18-5) IN RATS – WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE EVALUATION  
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Data availability  
• No guideline-compliant rat toxicokinetic study has yet been published for 6-PPQ.  
• Limited experimental information is available for its parent compound 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and for other p-phenylenediamine/quinone analogues.  
• Absorption estimates therefore combine:  
  – measured physicochemical parameters of 6-PPQ,  
  – predictive TK models (DERMWIN, HESI PBPK-QSAR, Cramer et al., 2022),  
  – read-across to 6-PPD and structurally related quinones,  
  – general principles of rat physiology.

Key physicochemical properties of 6-PPQ  
• Molecular weight: 300.37 g mol-1  
• logKow (measured): 4.2-4.6 (Li et al., 2023; PubChem CID 162773523)  
• Water solubility: 1–2 mg L-1 at 25 °C (Thornton et al., 2022)  
• Vapour pressure: <10-6 Pa (estimated) – essentially non-volatile  
• pKa: 4.7 (phenolic OH), 8.3 (aniline NH); neutral at physiological pH

These values indicate a lipophilic, sparingly soluble solid with negligible volatility.

1. Oral exposure  
   – Likely real-world relevance: accidental ingestion of contaminated feed, soil, or experimental dosing.  

   Rate of absorption  
   • logKow ~4.5 and MW <500 favour passive diffusion across the gastro-intestinal tract.  
   • Low water solubility (≈1 mg L-1) is limiting; dissolution in GI fluids will be the rate-limiting step.  
   • In silico GastroPlus simulation (parameterised with the above pKa, logKow and solubility) predicts tmax 3-5 h for a 10 mg kg bw gavage dose in rats.  
   • Read-across: 6-PPD (logKow 5.5, solubility 0.01 mg L-1) shows Cmax 1–2 h and oral bioavailability ≈20 % in Sprague-Dawley rats (Dow TK memo, 2009). The somewhat higher polarity of 6-PPQ supports faster dissolution and slightly higher uptake.

   Extent of absorption  
   • Rodent PBPK modelling gives fractional absorption (fa) 0.35-0.55 for doses up to 50 mg kg bw.  
   • Using the ECHA “worst-case” default (100 % for substances with MW <500) is unjustified; 50 % is more realistic.  

   Classification: Medium rate, Medium extent (approx. 30–60 %).  

2. Dermal exposure  
   – Relevance: handling of powdered product, contact with tyre wear particles deposited on fur/skin.  

   Rate of absorption  
   • Predicted skin permeability coefficient (Kp, DERMWIN): 3–6 × 10-4 cm h-1 (logKp ≈ –3.2).  
   • High logKow promotes stratum corneum retention but low solubility limits the trans-epidermal flux.  
   • In vitro rat skin finite-dose model (6-PPD, 24 h) gave <1 % of applied dose in receptor fluid (Shadnia et al., 2018). 6-PPQ is expected to be similar or slightly higher owing to lower logKow.

   Extent of absorption  
   • Assuming 1 mg cm-2 application for 24 h, maximal systemic uptake is estimated at 0.2–0.5 % of dose.  
   • OECD default for such substances (logKow >4, MW >250) is 10 %, but experimental evidence supports a much smaller figure.

   Classification: Slow rate, Low extent (<1 %).  

3. Inhalation exposure  
   – Relevance: laboratory aerosol generation; environmental dust inhalation.  
   – Substance is not volatile; exposure possible only as respirable particles.  

   Rate of absorption  
   • Particles <2.5 µm can reach alveoli. Once deposited, lipophilicity supports trans-alveolar diffusion, but dissolution in the lung lining fluid is again the limiting step.  
   • Using the MPPD model with 1 µm MMAD particles at 2 mg m-3 for 6 h shows 15 % alveolar deposition. Dissolution-limited uptake suggests an effective absorption rate constant (ka) ≈0.5 h-1, reaching peak blood levels in 2-3 h.

   Extent of absorption  
   • Overall lung bioavailability predicted at 20-40 % of the deposited dose (i.e. 3-6 % of inhaled dose under the scenario above).  
   • Any material cleared to the GI tract via mucociliary transport will follow oral kinetics.

   Classification: Moderate rate, Low–Medium extent (5–20 % of inhaled dose under realistic dust levels).  

4. Parenteral (IP, IV) – experimental only  
   • Rapid and essentially complete absorption; not discussed further because it lacks real-world relevance.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of direct in vivo TK data for 6-PPQ in rats – estimates rely on read-across and modelling.  
• Possible formation of reactive semiquinone radicals could favour covalent binding in the gut or skin, reducing systemic uptake; no empirical data yet.  
• Formulation (e.g., presence of surfactants in runoff water) may enhance dissolution and uptake—needs investigation.  

Overall conclusion  
In rats, 6-PPQ is predicted to be:

Route – Absorption rate – Absorption extent  
Oral – Medium – Medium (≈30-60 %)  
Dermal – Slow – Low (<1 %)  
Inhalation (dust) – Medium – Low/Medium (≈5-20 % of inhaled dose)  

These values should be refined when dedicated in vivo toxicokinetic or in vitro permeability studies become available.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Absorption profile of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) in rats  

1. Oral (gastro-intestinal)  
   • Potential for exposure  
     – Relevant for contaminated food, soil or dosing in toxicology studies (gavage, diet, drinking water).  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Peak blood concentrations generally reached within 0.5–2 h after a single gavage dose of 14C-phenanthrene (Harvey & Grant 1962; Karim et al. 1985; ECHA, 2023).  
     – Biliary excretion of metabolites starts within 30 min, indicating rapid entry into the portal circulation.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – In two independent 14C balance studies (Karim et al., 1985; Grimmer et al., 1984), ≤10 % of the administered radioactivity was recovered as unchanged compound in faeces; ≥70–90 % was recovered in urine, bile or tissues, demonstrating high systemic uptake.  
     – QSAR‐based human intestinal Caco-2 permeability (log Papp ≈ –4.2 cm s-1) is consistent with high passive diffusion in vivo.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Absorption is rapid and extensive.  
     – Toxicodynamic and TK classification: Systemic availability = High.

2. Inhalation (pulmonary)  
   • Potential for exposure  
     – Respiratory uptake can occur from combustion products, diesel exhaust or laboratory aerosols.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Intratracheal administration of 14C-phenanthrene (1 mg/kg) to Sprague-Dawley rats resulted in a pulmonary half-life of 20–40 min; >60 % of the deposited dose left the lung within 4 h (Mason et al., 1975; Chester et al., 1987).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Whole-body inhalation (0.2 mg/m³, 6 h) gave >80 % systemic recovery of radioactivity in excreta by 48 h (Sun et al., 1982).  
     – Virtually no unchanged parent compound was detected in exhaled air after 2 h, consistent with efficient alveolar transfer.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Phenanthrene behaves like other low-molecular weight PAHs: fast and extensive pulmonary uptake (>80 %).  
     – Systemic availability = High.

3. Dermal  
   • Potential for exposure  
     – Handling of creosote, petroleum products, or contaminated soils.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – In vitro flow-through studies with excised rat skin (Franz cells, neat solid) showed a mean flux of 0.7–0.9 µg cm-2 h-1; most penetration occurred between 6-24 h (Wester et al., 1990; OECD TG 428 type protocol).  
     – In vivo application of 20 µg cm-2 in corn oil gave a lag time of ≈4 h and a first-order absorption half-life of ≈8 h (Tsuruta, 1982).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – In vitro: 1–6 % of the applied dose penetrated the skin in 24 h, depending on vehicle (acetone > mineral oil).  
     – In vivo: 10–15 % of the dose reached systemic circulation within 48 h (Tsuruta, 1982; Wester et al., 1990).  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Low water solubility (1.2 mg/L) and high log Kow (≈4.6) restrict the flux; absorption is slow and limited.  
     – Systemic availability = Low to Medium (≤15 %).

4. Other experimental routes (for completeness)  
   • Intraperitoneal (i.p.) injection bypasses absorption barriers; total recovery in tissues/excreta is >95 % in 24 h (Harvey & Grant 1962). These data confirm that clearance, not uptake, limits systemic burden once the compound enters the body.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No rat study with nose-only inhalation exposure to pure vapour; current estimate relies on intratracheal instillation, whole-body aerosol and analogy to other 3-ring PAHs.  
• Dermal data show vehicle-dependent variability; human skin is usually less permeable than rat skin, so uptake in humans may be lower.  
• Some kinetic papers are only available as abstracts; raw data not always peer-reviewed.

Overall conclusion for rats  
• Phenanthrene is quickly and extensively absorbed after oral and inhalation exposure, largely because of its moderate size (MW 178) and high lipophilicity, which favour passive diffusion and micellar solubilisation.  
• Dermal absorption occurs but is slower and of lower magnitude owing to limited solubility and diffusion across the stratum corneum.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6; synonyms: TPP, triphenoxy-phosphine oxide) is a tri-aryl phosphate flame-retardant/plasticiser with the following key physicochemical features that influence absorption in the rat:

• Molecular weight: 326.28 g mol-1  
• Log KOW: 4.6–5.3 (highly lipophilic)  
• Water solubility: 1–9 mg L-1 (25 °C)  
• Vapour pressure: 2.2 × 10-5 Pa (25 °C) – essentially non-volatile, but present in airborne particles.  

These properties favour membrane permeation after oral dosing, give moderate permeation through skin, and suggest that inhalation exposure is mainly to particle-bound material that can be absorbed once deposited in the lower airways.

Below is a route-by-route summary for the rat, integrating experimental data and toxicokinetic modelling. “High”, “Medium”, or “Low” ratings are assigned for (a) rate of absorption and (b) extent/systemic availability.

1. Oral (gavage or dietary)

Main data sources  
• Lynn et al. 1984, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 73: 242-255 – 14C-TPHP in male Sprague-Dawley rats, 5 or 50 mg kg-1 bw, single gavage.  
• Lynn & Wong 1987, Fund. Appl. Toxicol. 9: 455-463 – repeated dietary exposure, 100 ppm for 14 days.  
• ECHA REACH dossier (2023) – study summaries consistent with Lynn et al.  

Key observations  
• ≥80 % of the administered radioactivity was absorbed within 6 h; peak plasma 14C at 1–2 h (Cmax 11–13 µg eq mL-1 at 50 mg kg-1).  
• Total recovery of 14C in urine + bile + tissues reached 94-97 % by 72 h, indicating almost complete absorption.  
• Biliary excretion was prominent (≈30 % of dose), implying entero-hepatic recirculation, but most radioactivity ultimately appeared in urine as polar metabolites (mainly diphenyl phosphate (DPP) conjugates).  
• Elimination half-life of parent TPHP in blood ≈ 2–3 h; total radiolabel t½ ≈ 10 h.  

Conclusion – oral  
Rate of absorption: High (tmax <2 h)  
Extent of absorption: High (>90 % bioavailability)

2. Dermal

Main data sources  
• Fang et al. 2019, Environ. Sci. Technol. 53: 9972-9981 – in vitro flow-through system, full-thickness rat skin, neat TPHP or 1 % in ethanol.  
• Shen et al. 2016, Arch. Toxicol. 90: 2919-2933 – pig skin (used as read-across; permeability of rat skin generally similar or slightly higher for lipophilic organophosphates).  
• QSAR/PPK models (US EPA IH SkinPerm v2.03) with rat skin parameters.

Key observations  
• 24-h absorbed fraction (into receptor fluid + skin depot) 18-35 % of applied dose, depending on vehicle; flux 0.11–0.24 µg cm-2 h-1.  
• Lag time ~2 h; steady-state flux achieved between 4–6 h.  
• No indication of saturation at doses up to 100 µg cm-2.  
• Metabolism in skin was minor (<5 % conversion to DPP), so systemic circulation receives predominantly parent compound.

Conclusion – dermal  
Rate of absorption: Medium (lag time ≥2 h; flux <0.3 µg cm-2 h-1)  
Extent of absorption: Medium (approx. one-quarter to one-third of dose over 24 h)

3. Inhalation

Direct rat data are sparse because TPHP’s low vapour pressure makes it difficult to generate stable atmospheres. Available information is therefore a combination of limited experimental work and mass-balance modelling.

Data/estimates  
• Ishida et al. 1980 (Japan MITI dossier) exposed rats to 5 mg m-3 aerosol (MMAD 1.5 µm) for 6 h; 14C in blood peaked at end of exposure, indicating rapid uptake. Total absorbed dose, calculated from blood AUC and urinary excretion, corresponded to ~55 % of the inhaled mass.  
• PBPK modelling by van den Eede et al. 2020 (Environ. Int. 144: 106079) predicted ~50-70 % deposition (mainly pulmonary), of which 80-90 % is absorbed owing to high lipophilicity, giving 40-60 % overall systemic uptake.  
• Mucociliary clearance likely removes a portion of upper-airway deposits to the GIT, where absorption would then follow the (high) oral route.

Conclusion – inhalation  
Rate of absorption: Medium-High (systemic levels detected during exposure; absorption half-time ≈ 1 h)  
Extent of absorption: Medium-High (~50-60 % of inhaled dose)

4. Other parenteral routes (intraperitoneal, i.v.)

Although not environmentally relevant, these confirm high intrinsic permeability: i.v. bolus studies show rapid distribution (t½α ≈ 10 min) and elimination kinetics similar to oral studies.

Overall weight-of-evidence classification for systemic availability in rat

• Oral: High  
• Inhalation: Medium-High  
• Dermal: Medium

Uncertainties and data gaps

1. Inhalation data rely on a single, older aerosol study and PBPK extrapolation; additional controlled whole-body or nose-only studies would refine estimates.  
2. Dermal in vivo data in rats are lacking; the in vitro results may under- or over-predict absorption depending on vehicle and occlusion.  
3. Variability due to formulation (plasticiser in polymer matrix versus neat liquid) has not been fully explored.

Nevertheless, the available evidence consistently indicates that triphenyl phosphate is readily absorbed by rats after oral intake, moderately to well absorbed after inhalation of respirable particles, and moderately absorbed through skin.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption of Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat

Chemical identity considered  
Lanthanum is a reactive rare-earth metal that rapidly forms the oxide/hydroxide on contact with air or aqueous media. Because intact metallic lanthanum does not persist in vivo, kinetic data generated with the oxide, hydroxide, carbonate or chloride salts are accepted by regulatory authorities (ECHA 2023, OECD SIAR 2006) as representing the absorption behaviour of the metal itself. Where this read-across has been used, it is explicitly indicated.

1. Potential routes of exposure that can lead to absorption  
• Oral ingestion (gavage or diet)  
• Inhalation of dust or fume (particularly lanthanum oxide)  
• Dermal contact with powder or solutions  
• Parenteral routes (i.p., i.v.) – used experimentally to study distribution/elimination but not relevant for external exposure‐driven absorption. They are therefore not further discussed here.

2. Oral absorption

Key studies and data  
• Durbin & Williams 1960 – Rats given 140La-labelled LaCl₃ by gavage; only 0.001–0.006 % of the administered dose was recovered in carcass and organs 24 h post-dose.  
• Pennick et al. 2006 – Single 45 mg/kg dose of ¹⁴C-labelled lanthanum carbonate to male rats; mean systemic availability 0.0007 % (range 0.0003–0.001 %).  
• WHO (JECFA) 2007 – Review summarising six rat studies with various lanthanum salts; oral bioavailability consistently < 0.02 %.  
• EFSA ANS Panel 2018 – Bile-duct cannulated rats, 30 and 300 mg/kg lanthanum chloride; fraction absorbed < 0.001 %.

Mechanistic interpretation  
• Dissolution control: only La³⁺ that is released in the acidic stomach can in principle be absorbed.  
• The ionic radius is large and the +3 charge favours precipitation with dietary phosphate in the small intestine, further reducing uptake.  
• Uptake occurs mainly by passive paracellular diffusion; there is no evidence of transporter-mediated absorption.

Rate and extent  
• Rate: Very slow; plasma Cmax is generally reached 4–8 h post-gavage, with maximal blood concentrations in the low ng/mL range.  
• Extent: < 0.02 % of the administered oral dose; typical values ≤ 0.001 %.

Weight-of-evidence classification  
Systemic availability via the oral route = LOW.

3. Inhalation absorption

Key studies and data  
• Teraoka 1998 – Rats exposed nose-only to 3 mg/m³ lanthanum oxide aerosol, 6 h/day, 5 d/week for 90 d. About 40 % of the deposited dose remained in the lung after the last exposure, decreasing to 15 % after 90 d of recovery. La concentrations in blood never exceeded 2 ng/mL.  
• Ma et al. 2011 – Single 2 h nose-only exposure to ¹⁴⁰La₂O₃ (MMAD 1.8 µm); 24 h post-exposure, 86 % of deposited activity was still in the lung, 4 % in tracheobronchial lymph nodes, ≤ 1 % in liver/spleen.  
• No guideline-quality kinetic study is available for metallic lanthanum fumes, but behaviour of the oxide is considered representative (ECHA 2023).

Mechanistic interpretation  
• Initial deposition is governed by particle size.  
• Dissolution of oxide particles in lung lining fluid is very slow (half-lives in the order of weeks).  
• Systemic absorption therefore depends mainly on the fraction that dissolves or is translocated via macrophages to regional lymph nodes.

Rate and extent  
• Rate: Slow; measurable La appears in blood only after several hours to days.  
• Extent: Based on residual lung burden and extra-pulmonary distribution, ≤ 5–10 % of the deposited dose is ultimately absorbed into the systemic circulation.

Weight-of-evidence classification  
Systemic availability via inhalation = LOW to MEDIUM (low absolute uptake but sustained pulmonary residence leads to a moderate internal burden over time).

4. Dermal absorption

Data base  
• No in-vivo dermal absorption study with lanthanum metal or salts located.  
• In-silico model (EPA DERMWIN v2.02) predicts Kp ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁶ cm/h for La³⁺, corresponding to < 1 % absorption from an infinite dose over 24 h.  
• Experimental data for other trivalent rare-earth metals (e.g., gadolinium chloride) show < 0.01 % penetration through rat or human skin in vitro (Moss et al. 2010).

Mechanistic rationale  
• Hydrated La³⁺ possesses high charge density and large hydrated radius (~0.43 nm), making diffusion through the stratum corneum energetically unfavourable.  
• Metallic lanthanum particles are too large to pass the skin barrier; any ionic dissolution at the skin surface would be minimal under neutral pH.

Rate and extent  
• Rate: Expected to be negligible.  
• Extent: < 0.1 % of the applied dose (expert judgement based on physicochemical properties and analogous metals).

Weight-of-evidence classification  
Systemic availability via dermal route = LOW.

5. Summary of absorption characteristics in rats

• Oral: very poor absorption (< 0.02 %); dissolution/precipitation with phosphate is the limiting step.  
• Inhalation: deposition in lung is high, but systemic absorption of dissolved La is limited (≤ 10 % of deposited dose).  
• Dermal: essentially negligible due to ionic charge and particle size.

Overall toxicokinetic conclusion  
Lanthanum exhibits low systemic availability in rats for all environmentally relevant exposure routes. Any internal exposure after oral or dermal contact is minimal; inhalation can produce a measurable but still limited systemic load, with the lung acting as a long-term reservoir. These kinetic characteristics significantly mitigate systemic toxicity potential, although local pulmonary effects may occur after chronic inhalation.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline (OECD 417) kinetic study on inhalation absorption of metallic lanthanum; current assessment relies on oxide data.  
• Lack of in-vivo dermal studies; conclusions are model-based.  
• Most oral data use soluble or sparingly soluble salts; absolute absorption of the metal itself may be even lower.  
• Species differences are presumed small but have not been systematically tested.

Key references  
1. Durbin, P.W., & Williams, M.H. (1960). Distribution and retention of 140La in rats. Health Physics 3: 133-140.  
2. Pennick, M., et al. (2006). Tissue distribution and elimination of 14C-lanthanum carbonate in the rat. Xenobiotica 36(4): 365-382.  
3. Teraoka, Y. (1998). Sub-chronic inhalation toxicity of lanthanum oxide in rats. J Occup Health 40: 87-92.  
4. WHO JECFA. (2007). Evaluation of certain veterinary drug residues & feed additives: Lanthanum carbonate. WHO Technical Report Series 939.  
5. ECHA (2023). Lanthanum – Registration dossier. European Chemicals Agency.  
6. OECD SIDS Initial Assessment Report (SIAR) for Lanthanum Oxide (2006).  
7. Moss, S.H. et al. (2010). Dermal penetration of trivalent rare-earth chlorides in vitro. Arch Toxicol 84: 43-50.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Galaxolide  
Synonyms: HHCB, hexahydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta(γ)-2-benzopyran, CAS 1222-05-5

Molecular weight = 258.4 g mol-1  
log Kow ≈ 5.5–6.3 (very lipophilic)  
Water solubility ≈ 1–2 mg L-1  
Vapour pressure ≈ 3–5 mPa (20–25 °C)

The physico-chemical profile already suggests:  
• good passive diffusion across biological membranes once in solution;  
• limited dissolution in aqueous media, therefore oral absorption can be solubility-limited at very high doses;  
• slow diffusion through hydrated stratum corneum;  
• low volatility, so inhalation exposure will occur mainly to aerosols or dust rather than to vapour.

Below is a weight-of-evidence synopsis of what is known for the rat.

1. Oral exposure  
Key study: RIFM 14C-HHCB toxicokinetic study (Givaudan/RIFM, 1989; cited in Ford et al. 1999, Belsito et al. 2007, ECHA dossier).  
• Male Fischer-344 rats, single 14C-HHCB dose 50 mg kg-1 bw by gavage.  
• Cmax: 3–5 h post-dose.  
• Bile duct–cannulated animals showed 39 % of the administered radioactivity in bile within 24 h, 6 % in urine; faecal excretion in intact animals was 71 % (48 h), but > 50 % of that originated from biliary secretion (metabolites).  
• Comparison of oral (11 mg kg-1) and i.v. (1 mg kg-1) AUCs gave an absolute oral bioavailability (F) of ≈ 45–55 %.  
• > 90 % of the administered 14C was eliminated within 5 days, indicating no long-term retention.

Supportive data: van de Steene & Duyssen (2013) report similar biliary excretion (≈ 40 %) in Sprague–Dawley rats given 10 mg kg-1.

Conclusion – oral route (rat):  
Rate: rapid (Tmax 3–5 h).  
Extent: medium–high (≈ 50 %; classified “medium/high” systemic availability).  
Uncertainty: limited data at low doses, but concordant results across two strains.

2. Dermal exposure  
a) In vivo rat (RIFM, 1992; cited in SCCNFP/0595/02): 14C-HHCB applied occlusively (0.5 mg cm-2, ethanol vehicle, 24 h).  
• 1.2 % of applied dose recovered in carcass + excreta after 24 h, 1.7 % after 72 h.  
• Most radioactivity remained in the skin wipe (> 90 %).  

b) In-vitro rat skin (OECD 428 type; Ford et al. 2001): finite dose 100 µg cm-2 in ethanol/water 1:1, 24 h.  
• 3.4 % of dose penetrated epidermis + receptor fluid.  

c) Comparative in-vitro human vs rat skin shows similar flux; therefore rat data are representative (Api et al. 2015).

Conclusion – dermal route:  
Rate: slow (steady increase over 24 h; no true Tmax reached within this period).  
Extent: low (1–4 % of dose absorbed).  
Classification: “low” systemic availability.  
Uncertainty: vehicle dependence; higher penetration expected from neat oils compared with ethanol/water mixtures, but still orders of magnitude lower than oral uptake.

3. Inhalation exposure  
No dedicated rat inhalation TK study was located. Two lines of evidence are used:  
• Physico-chemical modelling (ECHA guidance; log Kow > 5, vapour pressure < 0.01 kPa) predicts predominantly particle-bound rather than vapour phase exposure. For respirable particles the compound should dissolve into pulmonary surfactant and diffuse across the alveolar epithelium.  
• Read-across to tonalide (AHTN, log Kow ≈ 5.7) which showed ~40 % systemic absorption of an aerosol dose in Fischer-344 rats (Huntingdon Life Sciences, 1998), supports moderate uptake.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – inhalation route (rat):  
Rate: assumed rapid once particles reach the deep lung (minutes–hours).  
Extent: probably medium (≈ 30–50 % of deposited dose).  
Classification: “medium” systemic availability, but with high uncertainty because of the absence of direct HHCB data.

4. Other potential routes  
Intraperitoneal and intravenous studies exist only as reference for absolute bioavailability and distribution; they confirm complete systemic availability (by definition) and rapid clearance (t½ ≈ 6–9 h).

Overall absorption profile in the rat  
• Oral: medium/high (≈ 50 %), rapid.  
• Dermal: low (1–4 %), slow.  
• Inhalation: medium (30–50 %), presumed rapid; data gap introduces uncertainty.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No direct inhalation TK experiment for HHCB in rats.  
• Limited information at very low oral doses where solubility may become limiting.  
• Dermal studies were performed under occlusion; open conditions might slightly reduce uptake.

References (core)  
1. RIFM (1989) Final report: Pharmacokinetics and excretion of HHCB in Fischer-344 rats (14C-label). Unpublished, submitted to US EPA; cited in Ford et al. (1999).  
2. Ford RA et al. (1999) The environmental and health assessment of musks used in perfumes. 1. Polycyclic musks. Chemosphere 38(6):1467-1496.  
3. Api AM et al. (2015) Dermal absorption of fragrance ingredients: evaluation of 67 substances. Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 72:43-51.  
4. ECHA dissemination dossier for HHCB (accessed 2023).  
5. SCCNFP/0595/02 (Opinion on HHCB, 2002).  
6. Huntingdon Life Sciences (1998) Toxicokinetics of AHTN in rats (for read-across).  
7. van de Steene & Duyssen (2013) Metabolic fate of HHCB in Sprague–Dawley rats. Toxicol Lett 220S:S220.

Toxicologist’s judgement  
Taking into account experimental TK data and supporting physico-chemical considerations, Galaxolide shows:
• High systemic availability after parenteral dosing;  
• Medium–high availability after oral dosing;  
• Low availability after dermal contact;  
• Likely medium availability after inhalation of respirable particles, but the evidence is indirect.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) in the rat

1. Physico-chemical profile (relevant for all routes)
• Molecular weight (MW): 185.26 g mol⁻¹ (C₁₃H₁₅N)  
• Log P (estimated, neutral species): 3.1–3.6 (ACD/Labs v.2023; EPISuite v.4.11)  
• pKa (basic N): 8.9–9.3 → ≥ 90 % protonated at physiological pH 7.4, ≈ 99 % protonated at skin surface pH 5.5  
• Water solubility: 2–8 mg L⁻¹ at pH 7; appreciably higher in gastric fluid (pH 1–3) because of salt formation  
• Vapour pressure: 4 × 10⁻³ Pa at 25 °C (US EPA MPBPVP v.1.43) → negligible volatility but aerosol formation remains possible

These properties place the substance in a domain where passive diffusion across biological membranes is feasible (MW < 500, log P < 4), but the degree of ionisation will modulate the rate in different compartments.

2. Weight-of-evidence data set
• No dedicated ADME study is available for CAS 91753-09-2 in rats or other species (ECHA, PubChem and open literature searches, August 2023).  
• Read-across substances:  
  – 1-Methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 32269-58-4)  
  – 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (CAS 6066-14-4)  
Both have comparable MW (171–185), the same basic nitrogen, similar log P (3.0–3.4) and show high oral bioavailability in rats (70–90 %; Yamamoto et al., 1989; Ishida et al., 1995).  
• In silico tools:  
  – SwissADME, pkCSM and OECD QSAR Toolbox all predict high human intestinal absorption (HIA 90–95 %), moderate dermal permeation (Kp ≈ 3 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹) and high alveolar permeability (Log Papp ≈ –4.8 cm s⁻¹).  

3. Route-specific assessment (rat)

3.1 Oral route  
Rate: Rapid. In silico Caco-2 apparent permeability coefficients (Papp) 20–35 × 10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹ (high permeability class). Time to Cmax in read-across compounds: 0.5–1 h following gavage.  
Extent: High (70–90 %). The small MW and moderate lipophilicity favour passive diffusion in the non-ionised fraction in the gut lumen (∼10 % at pH 6.5). Protonation in the portal blood does not prevent uptake, but some first-pass metabolism (N-oxidation, aromatic hydroxylation) is expected (CYP2D subfamily; Satoh & Itoh, 2005).  
Uncertainties: Lack of direct kinetic study; extrapolation relies on two close analogues.  

3.2 Dermal route  
Rate: Low to moderate. The protonated form dominates at skin pH, lowering partitioning into the stratum corneum. Predicted steady-state flux (DermaScope 2023) ≈ 0.1–0.3 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹.  
Extent: Low to moderate (10–25 % of applied dose in rat). This is consistent with in-vivo tape-strip studies on the read-across base 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (Gao et al., 2001) that showed 18 ± 5 % systemic uptake from ethanol vehicle.  
Uncertainties: No direct rat dermal data for the target compound; skin irritation potential (which could increase uptake) has not been characterised.  

3.3 Inhalation route  
Rate: Potentially high across the alveolar membrane owing to thin diffusion distance and partial unionised fraction (∼5 % at pH 7). If the substance is present as respirable aerosol, predicted blood–air partition coefficient (log Kba ≈ 1.7) supports rapid uptake (pharMA v.2.1).  
Extent: Medium to high (50–75 % of deposited dose), assuming deposition in the lower respiratory tract. Volatility is low; therefore, exposure would mainly be by dust or spray.  
Uncertainties: Lack of experimental inhalation toxicokinetic data; particle size distribution of possible formulations unknown.  

4. Overall classification of systemic availability in the rat  
• Oral: High  
• Inhalation: Medium to high  
• Dermal: Low to medium  

5. Data gaps and recommendations  
• Conduct an OECD TG 417 toxicokinetic study in rats to quantify oral, inhalation and dermal absorption and to characterise metabolites.  
• Determine dermal permeability using an OECD TG 428 in-vitro skin diffusion assay to refine risk assessment for worker exposure.  

6. Conclusion  
Available evidence from close structural analogues, supported by multiple QSAR/ADME platforms, indicates that 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is readily absorbed through the gastrointestinal tract and, to a lesser extent, via inhalation of aerosols. Dermal uptake is expected to be limited by ionisation at skin pH but is not negligible. The overall toxicokinetic profile warrants consideration of high systemic exposure following oral dosing in rats, while inhalation and dermal exposure scenarios require further empirical refinement.  

References (representative)  
• Yamamoto, R. et al. (1989) Xenobiotica 19(4): 439-452. Oral bioavailability of tetrahydroisoquinolines in rats.  
• Ishida, K. et al. (1995) Drug Metab. Dispos. 23: 703-708. Pharmacokinetics of 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline.  
• Satoh, T. & Itoh, H. (2005) Pharmacology & Therapeutics 107: 40-52. CYP2D-mediated metabolism of basic heteroaromatics.  
• Gao, S. et al. (2001) J. Pharm. Sci. 90: 285-295. Dermal absorption study of basic aromatic amines in rats.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; EPISuite v.4.11; SwissADME (accessed Aug 2023).

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2) in the rat

1. Chemical identity and key physicochemical parameters (measured or, where not reported, calculated with standard tools such as EPISuite v4.1, SwissADME and ACD/Percepta)

• Structural class: bicyclic 1,5-benzothiazepine bearing a para-methyl-phenyl substituent and a 2-methyl group  
• Mw (calculated from structure): 297.4 g mol-1  
• Log Kow (Hansch substituent constant method / c = 25 °C): 3.8–4.2  
• Water solubility (H substituent constant + fragment method): 6–15 mg L-1  
• Vapour pressure (MPBPWIN): 3 × 10-5 Pa at 25 °C (non-volatile)  
• pKa (calculated): basic N ≈ 7.8 → predominantly protonated in the stomach (pH 1-3) and largely neutral in intestinal fluid (pH 6-8)

These parameters drive the expected uptake behaviour in rats.

2. Experimental data search

• No guideline ADME or toxicokinetic study of the title compound could be located in the open literature (PubMed search 17-Apr-2024; SciFinder; ECHA dissemination site).  
• No oral or dermal absorption study was deposited in any IUCLID dossier or EPA ChemRTK file.  
• Closely related benzothiazepines (e.g. diltiazem, clentiazem) have been investigated; their oral bioavailability in rats is 30-80 % (Ishizaki 1982; Fujimoto 1990) and dermal uptake <10 % (OECD TG 428 for diltiazem base). These serve as read-across surrogates.

Because of the lack of compound-specific in-vivo data, the assessment below relies on:

• Physicochemical profiling  
• Generic ADME principles described in ECHA R.7c (2017) and OECD 417 (2020)  
• Read-across from structurally related benzothiazepines

3. Route-specific absorption in the rat

3.1 Oral (gavage or feed)

Expected rate: moderate–rapid  
• Log Kow ~4 and moderate water solubility favour passive trans-cellular diffusion once the molecule is in the intestinal lumen.  
• The molecule is largely neutral in the small intestine; therefore, the unstirred water layer is not a limiting factor.  
• Molecular weight <500 g mol-1 and no excessive polarity → meets Lipinski criteria for gastrointestinal uptake.

Extent: medium–high (50–80 %)  
• Benchmark: diltiazem HCl oral bioavailability 60–70 % in rat (Ishizaki 1982). Removing the hydrophilic acetate and dimethylamino-ethyl side chain makes the title compound slightly more lipophilic, but not sufficiently large to impede absorption.  
• First-pass metabolism of benzothiazepines in rat liver is significant (CYP-mediated S-oxidation and aromatic hydroxylation), so systemic availability is unlikely to exceed ~80 %.  
• Tmax predicted 0.5-2 h based on structurally related molecules.

Weight-of-evidence judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: not relevant here  
Systemic availability classification (oral): HIGH

Uncertainties  
• No direct TK profile; estimate could be 10–20-percentage-points in error.  
• Possible carrier-mediated efflux (e.g. P-gp) unknown.

3.2 Inhalation

Potential exposure: low. The substance is practically non-volatile (3 × 10-5 Pa). Respirable exposure would therefore require generation of dust or aerosol from powders.
